topics.title: Functions and Objectives of Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions and Objectives of Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: FUNCTIONS AND OBJECTIVES OF MAINTENANCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Maintenance (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Maintenance is the combination of all technical, administrative, and managerial actions performed during the life cycle of an item to retain it in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its required function.

In the context of modern industrial engineering, maintenance is no longer viewed as a reactive "breakdown-fix" activity. Instead, it is recognized as a **strategic management function** that directly influences plant availability, product quality, production cost, occupational safety, and the overall profitability of an enterprise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The very first learning outcome of this course (Module 1) emphasizes understanding the **functions and objectives of maintenance** as the foundation upon which advanced topics like Condition Monitoring, Tribology, and Predictive Maintenance are built. Mastery of this introductory topic is mandatory before progressing to vibration analysis and oil analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **two-wheeler (motorcycle)**. You do not wait until the engine seizes or the brakes fail completely. Instead, you:

- Change the engine oil every **3,000 km** (Preventive Maintenance)
- Get the brakes checked periodically (Scheduled Inspection)
- Replace worn-out chain sprockets before they snap (Predictive Replacement)
- Service the bike after a long monsoon ride (Corrective Maintenance)
- Keep a record of every service (Maintenance Documentation)

The **bike is the equipment**, the **servicing centre is the maintenance department**, and the **goal is to keep the bike reliable, safe, and economical to operate** — exactly the same logic applies to a CNC machine, a power plant boiler, or an aircraft turbine.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Introduced in Module 1

The following standard terms must be memorized verbatim for KTU examinations:

- **Maintenance Engineering:** The discipline and profession of applying engineering concepts, theories, and methods to the maintenance of industrial equipment.
- **Maintenance Management:** The organization of maintenance within an enterprise, encompassing planning, scheduling, execution, and control of maintenance resources.
- **Plant Availability:** The fraction of time that a piece of equipment is actually in a ready-to-run state versus the total time it is required.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** A reliability metric expressed in hours.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** The average downtime per failure event, expressed in hours.
- **Reliability, Maintainability, and Availability (RAM):** The three pillars of equipment performance engineering.

### 1.4 Scope of Maintenance in Modern Industry

The scope of maintenance has expanded dramatically over the decades:

| Era | Maintenance Philosophy | Dominant Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-1950 | Run-to-Failure | Reactive / Breakdown Maintenance |
| 1950–1975 | Scheduled Service | Preventive Maintenance (PM) |
| 1975–2000 | Reliability-Centered | RCM and Predictive Maintenance |
| Post-2000 | Smart Manufacturing | IoT-based Condition Monitoring, Industry 4.0 |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** In a 14-mark KTU question, always begin your answer by defining maintenance in your *own* words, then follow it with a neatly classified list. Examiners reward structured answers that demonstrate conceptual clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Core Functions of Maintenance Management

Maintenance management is conventionally broken down into **five universal management functions**, parallel to the classical Fayol's management principles. Each function is critical and directly testable in KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 Planning Function of Maintenance

Planning is the **foremost** and most important function. It answers the question: *"What must be done, when, by whom, and how?"*

- Identification of maintenance tasks from equipment manuals, failure history, and statutory regulations.
- Preparation of long-term (annual), medium-term (quarterly), and short-term (weekly/daily) maintenance schedules.
- Estimation of spare parts, tools, manpower, and budget requirements.
- Determination of maintenance intervals based on reliability data (MTBF) and OEM recommendations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Planning is Critical:** Unplanned maintenance typically costs **3 to 10 times** more than planned maintenance. Proper planning converts reactive firefighting into a controlled, budgeted activity.

### 2.1.2 Organizing Function of Maintenance

Organizing establishes the **structural framework** — who reports to whom and what are the responsibilities.

- Creation of the maintenance organizational hierarchy (Maintenance Manager → Engineers → Supervisors → Technicians → Helpers).
- Formation of specialized maintenance crews (mechanical, electrical, instrumentation, civil).
- Defining authority, responsibility, and accountability at each level.
- Establishing coordination links with production, stores, purchase, and finance departments.

### 2.1.3 Staffing Function of Maintenance

Staffing ensures the **right people with the right skills** are available at the right time.

- Recruitment, selection, and training of maintenance personnel.
- Skill matrix development and multi-skilling (cross-training) of technicians.
- Motivation, incentive schemes, and performance appraisal of maintenance staff.
- Compliance with statutory safety training requirements.

### 2.1.4 Directing Function of Maintenance

Directing is the **leadership function** that motivates the workforce to execute the plan.

- Issuing clear work orders and job instructions.
- Supervising ongoing maintenance work for quality and safety.
- Resolving technical and operational bottlenecks on the shop floor.
- Applying motivational techniques and team briefings (toolbox talks).

### 2.1.5 Controlling Function of Maintenance

Controlling is the **feedback loop** that measures performance against the plan and initiates corrective action.

- Monitoring Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) such as MTBF, MTTR, Availability, and Maintenance Cost per Unit Produced.
- Conducting Root Cause Analysis (RCA) for repeated failures.
- Variance analysis — comparing planned vs. actual maintenance hours and costs.
- Continuous improvement through Kaizen, TPM, and Six Sigma methodologies.

### 2.1.6 Summary Table — Five Functions of Maintenance

| Function | Core Question Addressed | Key Deliverable |
|---|---|---|
| **Planning** | What, When, How? | Maintenance Schedule & Budget |
| **Organizing** | Who reports to whom? | Organizational Structure |
| **Staffing** | Who will do the job? | Trained Workforce |
| **Directing** | How to motivate execution? | Work Orders & Supervision |
| **Controlling** | Are we on track? | Performance Reports & RCA |

---

## 2.2 Objectives of Maintenance

The objectives of maintenance are **multi-dimensional** and must be understood holistically. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate at least **6 to 8 objectives** with one-line justifications.

### 2.2.1 Primary Objectives

1. **Maximize Equipment Availability and Reliability**
   Equipment must be ready to perform its intended function whenever required by the production schedule.

2. **Minimize Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**
   This includes direct maintenance cost (labour, spares) and indirect cost (production loss, quality rejects, safety penalties).

3. **Ensure Safety of Personnel, Plant, and Environment**
   A failing pressure vessel, a worn-out brake, or a leaking hydraulic hose can cause catastrophic accidents.

4. **Preserve / Extend Useful Life of Equipment**
   Proper lubrication, alignment, and timely replacement of worn parts delay capital replacement.

5. **Optimize Product Quality**
   Out-of-tolerance machinery produces out-of-specification products. Maintenance ensures repeatability.

6. **Conserve Energy and Resources**
   A poorly maintained steam trap or compressed air leak wastes enormous amounts of energy.

7. **Comply with Statutory and Regulatory Requirements**
   Periodic inspection of boilers, lifts, cranes, and pressure vessels is mandated by law (Factories Act, Boiler Regulations).

8. **Maintain the Aesthetic and Functional Standard of Plant**
   A clean, well-maintained plant reflects professionalism and boosts workforce morale.

### 2.2.2 Strategic vs. Tactical Objectives

| Category | Objective | Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | Long-term asset reliability, lifecycle cost minimization | 5–20 years |
| **Tactical** | Annual maintenance budget, downtime reduction targets | 1–12 months |
| **Operational** | Daily work order closure, response time to breakdowns | Daily / Weekly |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Maintenance Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following six formulas. They are repeated almost every year in KTU Module 1 questions.**

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless (or %) | Fraction of time equipment is ready |
| 2 | **Unavailability (U)** | $U = 1 - A$ | dimensionless | Fraction of time equipment is down |
| 3 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Average uptime per failure cycle |
| 4 | **Mean Time To Repair** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | hours | Average repair duration |
| 5 | **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | failures/hour | Frequency of failure |
| 6 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless | World-class metric for TPM |

Where:
- $A$ = Availability
- $P$ = Performance efficiency
- $Q$ = Quality rate

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Caution:** Whenever you write the availability formula on the exam answer sheet, write it as a horizontal fraction. In your personal notes, never use the vertical pipe `\vert x \vert` inside markdown tables — always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The functions and objectives of maintenance are not abstract textbook concepts — they are the **operational backbone** of every asset-intensive industry:

- **Power Generation:** A single unscheduled shutdown of a 500 MW thermal unit can cost a utility over ₹1 crore per day in lost revenue. Maintenance planning directly prevents this.
- **Aerospace:** Maintenance objectives are so critical that aviation maintenance is governed by rigid international standards such as **EASA Part-145** and **FAA Part-145**.
- **Manufacturing:** In a semiconductor fabrication plant, vibration of a precision stepper motor by even 0.1 microns can render an entire wafer unusable. Predictive maintenance has zero tolerance for failure.
- **Oil & Gas:** Refineries schedule turnarounds (major maintenance shutdowns) 3–5 years in advance, executing the entire planning function with military precision.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Availability Equation

The single most important formula in Module 1 is the **Availability equation**. We will derive it from first principles so that you can reproduce the steps on the exam paper.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Time Cycle**

Consider a single piece of equipment operating over a long observation period. The total calendar time $T_{total}$ can be divided into two mutually exclusive states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{uptime} + T_{downtime}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Uptime in Terms of MTBF**

By definition, **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** is the average duration of successful operation between two consecutive failures:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{T_{uptime}}{N_{f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N_{f}$ is the total number of failures observed during the observation period.

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{uptime} &= N_{f} \times MTBF
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Downtime in Terms of MTTR**

By definition, **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** is the average downtime per failure event:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \frac{T_{downtime}}{N_{f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{downtime} &= N_{f} \times MTTR
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Total Time Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= N_{f} \times MTBF + N_{f} \times MTTR \\
T_{total} &= N_{f} \times (MTBF + MTTR)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the Formal Definition of Availability**

Availability is the fraction of total time that the equipment is in the operational (up) state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{T_{uptime}}{T_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Expressions Derived in Steps 2 and 4**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{N_{f} \times MTBF}{N_{f} \times (MTBF + MTTR)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the Common Factor $N_{f}$**

The number of failures $N_{f}$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical availability formula** required for all KTU Module 1 calculations.

### 3.1.2 Conversion to Percentage Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \, (\%) &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A critical pump operates for an average of **900 hours** between failures and requires an average of **18 hours** for repair. Calculate its availability and comment on whether it meets world-class standards.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= 900 \text{ hours} \\
MTTR &= 18 \text{ hours} \\
A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
A &= \frac{900}{900 + 18} \\
A &= \frac{900}{918} \\
A &= 0.9804 \\
A \, (\%) &= 98.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

- An availability of **98.04%** is excellent — well above the typical industrial benchmark of 95%.
- A world-class manufacturing facility targets **OEE ≥ 85%**, which corresponds to availability usually ≥ 95%.
- If MTTR were reduced to 9 hours (by improving diagnostic procedures and stocking critical spares), availability would rise to $\frac{900}{909} = 99.01\%$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Functions vs. Objectives Cross-Mapping

This matrix helps you write a high-scoring KTU answer by linking each management function to the objective it primarily serves.

| Management Function ↓ \ Objective → | Maximize Availability | Minimize Cost | Ensure Safety | Extend Life | Comply with Law |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Planning** | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ |
| **Organizing** | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Staffing** | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ |
| **Directing** | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Controlling** | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ |

Legend: ✓✓✓ = Strong influence, ✓✓ = Moderate influence, ✓ = Indirect influence.

---

## 3.3 Lifecycle Cost Derivation (Supplementary)

Although not always asked in Part A, lifecycle cost appears in 14-mark Module 1 questions. The **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for an asset over its service life $L$ years is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO &= C_{acquisition} + C_{installation} + \sum_{t=1}^{L}\left(C_{maintenance,t} + C_{downtime,t} + C_{energy,t}\right) + C_{disposal}
\end{aligned}
$$

where each $C_{x,t}$ is the cost component in year $t$. This equation demonstrates why maintenance is not merely an expense but a **strategic investment decision** — every dollar spent on preventive maintenance reduces the much larger downstream cost of downtime and quality rejection.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Five Functions of Maintenance Management

This flowchart shows the closed-loop relationship between the five management functions. Note that every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to satisfy Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT: Equipment Population & Operating Demand"] --> nodeB["FUNCTION 1: PLANNING - Maintenance Schedule, Budget, Spares, Manpower Plan"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["FUNCTION 2: ORGANIZING - Hierarchy, Crews, Departments, Coordination Links"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FUNCTION 3: STAFFING - Recruitment, Training, Skill Matrix, Motivation"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["FUNCTION 4: DIRECTING - Work Orders, Supervision, Toolbox Talks"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["EXECUTION: Maintenance Activities on Plant Equipment"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["FUNCTION 5: CONTROLLING - KPI Monitoring, RCA, Variance Analysis"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"Are KPIs Met?"}
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI["OUTPUT: High Availability, Low Cost, Safe Plant"]
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeJ["FEEDBACK LOOP: Corrective Action Sent to Planning"]
    nodeJ --> nodeB
```

**Reading the diagram:** The five functions form a **continuous improvement cycle**. If controlling detects any deviation from the planned KPIs (e.g., MTBF dropping, MTTR rising), a feedback signal is sent back to planning, which initiates revised schedules and budgets.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Objectives Hierarchy of Maintenance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    objRoot["MISSION: Ensure Equipment Performs Its Required Function Throughout Life Cycle"]
    objRoot --> objA["STRATEGIC OBJECTIVES - Long term, 5 to 20 years"]
    objRoot --> objB["TACTICAL OBJECTIVES - Medium term, 1 to 12 months"]
    objRoot --> objC["OPERATIONAL OBJECTIVES - Daily and weekly"]
    
    objA --> objA1["Maximize Asset Reliability and Availability"]
    objA --> objA2["Minimize Total Cost of Ownership"]
    objA --> objA3["Extend Useful Service Life of Equipment"]
    
    objB --> objB1["Achieve Annual Maintenance Budget Targets"]
    objB --> objB2["Reduce Planned and Unplanned Downtime"]
    objB --> objB3["Comply with Statutory Inspection Schedules"]
    
    objC --> objC1["Close All Work Orders Within Target Time"]
    objC --> objC2["Maintain MTBF Above Threshold"]
    objC --> objC3["Keep MTTR Below Threshold"]
    objC --> objC4["Ensure Zero LTI Lost Time Injuries"]
```

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["Maintenance Planning"] --> kpi1["MTBF Rises"]
    inp2["Spare Parts Management"] --> kpi1
    inp3["Skilled Workforce"] --> kpi2["MTTR Falls"]
    inp4["Diagnostic Tools"] --> kpi2

    biz3 --> final

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define maintenance. List any four objectives of maintenance management.**

**Model Answer:**

Maintenance is the combination of all technical, administrative, and managerial actions performed during the life cycle of an item to retain it in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its required function.

The four primary objectives of maintenance are:

1. To maximize equipment availability and reliability so that production schedules are met.
2. To minimize the total cost of ownership, including both direct maintenance cost and indirect cost of production loss.
3. To ensure the safety of personnel, plant, and the surrounding environment.
4. To extend the useful service life of equipment and delay capital replacement.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between MTBF and MTTR. Why is the ratio MTBF/MTTR important to maintenance engineers?**

**Model Answer:**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average duration of successful operation of equipment between two consecutive failures. It is a measure of **reliability**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time required to restore a failed piece of equipment back to working condition, including diagnosis, repair, testing, and restart. It is a measure of **maintainability**.

The ratio $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTTR}$ is important because it directly governs the **availability** of the equipment. A higher ratio indicates that the equipment runs much longer than it spends under repair, which is the engineering goal. This single ratio is used by maintenance engineers to benchmark plant performance, justify spare parts stocking decisions, and compare alternative equipment designs.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`

**A. (a)** Explain in detail the **five functions of maintenance management** with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The five functions of maintenance management are derived from the classical principles of industrial management and are universally applicable to any maintenance department.

**1. Planning:**
Planning determines in advance what maintenance work must be done, when, by whom, and with what resources. Example: A maintenance planner uses CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System) data and OEM manuals to create a 52-week schedule for overhauling all 50 centrifugal pumps in a chemical plant. The schedule includes spare part forecasts, manpower allocation, and budget provision.

**2. Organizing:**
Organizing creates the structural framework of authority and responsibility. Example: The maintenance manager creates three specialized sections — mechanical, electrical, and instrumentation — each headed by a sectional engineer who reports to the deputy maintenance manager. Coordination links are established with the production department to ensure minimal disruption to manufacturing.

**3. Staffing:**
Staffing ensures that the right skilled people are available. Example: The HR department, in consultation with maintenance leadership, recruits 12 multi-skilled technicians, trains them on vibration analysis and laser alignment, and certifies them on lockout-tagout procedures.

**4. Directing:**
Directing is the leadership function that motivates the workforce to execute the plan. Example: The maintenance supervisor conducts a daily toolbox talk at 8:00 AM, distributes work orders to each technician, and supervises a critical gearbox replacement during the shift.

**5. Controlling:**
Controlling measures actual performance against the plan. Example: At the end of each month, the maintenance controller generates a dashboard showing MTBF, MTTR, Availability, and Maintenance Cost per Unit. If availability falls below 95%, a root cause analysis (RCA) is initiated and corrective actions are fed back to the planning function.

---

**(b)** A machine has an MTBF of **1200 hours** and an MTTR of **24 hours**. Calculate (i) the availability, (ii) the failure rate, and (iii) the unavailability of the machine. Comment on whether the machine qualifies for world-class standards. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
$MTBF = 1200$ hours, $\quad MTTR = 24$ hours.

**(i) Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
A &= \frac{1200}{1200 + 24} \\
A &= \frac{1200}{1224} \\
A &= 0.9804 \\
A \, (\%) &= 98.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Failure Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{1}{MTBF} \\
\lambda &= \frac{1}{1200} \text{ failures per hour} \\
\lambda &= 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Unavailability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 1 - A \\
U &= 1 - 0.9804 \\
U &= 0.0196 \\
U \, (\%) &= 1.96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:**
An availability of **98.04%** is excellent and is considered world-class for critical rotating equipment. The failure rate is low, and the unavailability of under 2% indicates that the maintenance strategy is well-planned. However, there is still room to reduce MTTR further by improving diagnostic capability and pre-staging critical spares.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**B. (a)** Discuss the **major objectives of maintenance** in a modern manufacturing industry. Explain how each objective contributes to the overall profitability of the enterprise. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Modern maintenance engineering pursues multiple, mutually reinforcing objectives. The major objectives are:

1. **Maximize Equipment Availability:** Higher availability means more production hours per year, directly increasing revenue. For a plant with a turnover of ₹500 crore per year, even a 1% improvement in availability can yield ₹5 crore of additional revenue with negligible extra cost.

2. **Minimize Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):** Maintenance decisions must consider the entire lifecycle. A cheaper component that fails frequently may end up costing more in downtime than an expensive but longer-lasting OEM component.

3. **Ensure Safety:** Safety failures lead to catastrophic accidents, legal liability, and loss of reputation. Insurance premiums for plants with poor maintenance records are significantly higher.

4. **Extend Equipment Life:** A well-maintained CNC machine can serve reliably for 20 years instead of 12, deferring capital expenditure of several crore rupees.

5. **Optimize Product Quality:** Out-of-tolerance machinery produces defects. Reducing defect rate from 2% to 0.5% on a high-volume line can save crores in scrap and rework cost.

6. **Conserve Energy:** A leaking compressed air line at 7 bar wastes approximately 1 kW per 1 mm hole. Maintenance directly recovers this energy loss.

7. **Regulatory Compliance:** Failure to comply with statutory inspection schedules (e.g., for boilers, lifts, cranes) can result in forced shutdown by regulatory authorities, causing major financial loss.

Each objective contributes to profitability either through **revenue enhancement** (availability, quality), **cost reduction** (TCO, energy, life extension), or **risk mitigation** (safety, compliance).

---

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **closed-loop relationship between the five functions of maintenance management**. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The five functions of maintenance management — **Planning, Organizing, Staffing, Directing, and Controlling** — operate as a closed-loop continuous improvement cycle, very similar to the Deming PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fa["PLANNING - Schedule, Budget, Spares, Manpower Plan"] --> fb["ORGANIZING - Hierarchy, Crews, Coordination"]
    fb --> fc["STAFFING - Recruitment, Training, Motivation"]
    fc --> fd["DIRECTING - Work Orders, Supervision, Communication"]
    fd --> fe["EXECUTION - Actual Maintenance Work"]
    fe --> ff["CONTROLLING - KPI Monitoring, RCA, CAPA"]
    ff --> fg{"KPIs Met?"}
    fg -- YES --> fh["OBJECTIVES ACHIEVED"]
    fg -- NO --> fi["FEEDBACK TO PLANNING"]
    fi --> fa
```

**Explanation:**

- The cycle starts with **Planning**, where the maintenance requirements are determined in advance.
- **Organizing** establishes the structure in which the plan will be executed.
- **Staffing** ensures the right human resources are in place.
- **Directing** translates plans into action by guiding the workforce.
- After execution, **Controlling** measures actual performance using KPIs such as MTBF, MTTR, and Availability.
- If the KPIs are not met, the feedback loop returns the system to **Planning**, where corrective actions are incorporated into the next cycle. This ensures continuous improvement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** write the availability formula as $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTTR}$. The denominator must be $(MTBF + MTTR)$. Marks are deducted instantly for this error.
> 2. **Do not** confuse MTBF with MTTR in definitions. MTBF measures **uptime reliability**; MTTR measures **downtime repairability**.
> 3. When asked about "functions of maintenance," many students incorrectly list **types** of maintenance (preventive, predictive, etc.). The question is asking about **management functions** (planning, organizing, etc.).
> 4. Always show units in your numerical answers (hours, failures/hour, %).
> 5. In the closed-loop diagram question, you must draw the **feedback arrow** from controlling back to planning — examiners explicitly look for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Functions and Objectives of Maintenance:**

- **Definition:** Maintenance = combination of all technical, administrative, and managerial actions to retain or restore equipment to its required function.
- **Five Functions (mnemonic: P-O-S-D-C):** Planning, Organizing, Staffing, Directing, Controlling.
- **Planning** is the most critical function — it converts reactive firefighting into a controlled activity.
- **Organizing** establishes the maintenance hierarchy and coordination links with production, stores, and purchase.
- **Staffing** focuses on recruitment, training, multi-skilling, and motivation of maintenance personnel.
- **Directing** issues work orders, supervises execution, and conducts toolbox talks.
- **Controlling** monitors KPIs (MTBF, MTTR, Availability, OEE) and feeds back corrective action.
- **Eight Primary Objectives:** maximize availability, minimize cost, ensure safety, extend life, optimize quality, conserve energy, comply with regulations, maintain plant standards.
- **Canonical Availability Formula:**
  $$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$
- **Failure Rate Formula:** $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$.
- **Unavailability:** $U = 1 - A$.
- **World-Class Availability Benchmark:** ≥ 95% for general industry; ≥ 99% for critical continuous processes.
- **Closed-Loop Principle:** All five functions are linked by a feedback loop from Controlling back to Planning — this is the essence of continuous improvement.
- **Common Examiner Traps:** Confusion between functions and types of maintenance; wrong denominator in the availability formula; missing units in numerical answers; absence of the feedback arrow in diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance Strategy** is defined as a planned, systematic, and documented engineering management approach that prescribes **when**, **how**, and **to what extent** engineering assets (machines, systems, structures, or components) will be serviced, inspected, restored, or replaced, in order to preserve their functional capability, reliability, safety, and economic value across the operational lifecycle.

In the framework of KTU's **PEPET863** syllabus, maintenance strategies form the strategic layer above the tactical tools of condition monitoring. They dictate the **policy-level decisions** of an industrial organization — for example, whether to wait until a pump fails (reactive) or to service it every 3000 running hours (preventive), or to replace bearings only when vibration spectra exceed ISO 10816 thresholds (predictive).

### 1.2 The Six Canonical Maintenance Strategies

The modern maintenance management literature (and KTU module content) groups strategies into six families:

1. **Reactive (Breakdown / Run-to-Failure) Maintenance** — *no action until failure occurs*
2. **Preventive (Scheduled / Time-Based) Maintenance** — *service at fixed calendar or usage intervals*
3. **Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance** — *service when monitored parameters cross thresholds*
4. **Proactive (Root-Cause / Precision) Maintenance** — *eliminate failure mechanisms at the design and operating stage*
5. **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** — *logic-tree based strategy selection per failure mode*
6. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — *organization-wide culture of ownership and autonomous maintenance*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an engineering plant as a **human body**, and maintenance strategies as **healthcare philosophies**.
>
> - **Reactive** ≈ Visiting the doctor *only* after a heart attack (emergency care, surgery).
> - **Preventive** ≈ A scheduled annual full-body check-up *whether or not you feel sick* (a fixed calendar check).
> - **Predictive** ≈ Smart wearables (Fitbit, Apple Watch) that constantly monitor your **heart rate, SpO₂, ECG** and alert you *only when an actual anomaly appears* — saving both effort and false alarms.
> - **Proactive** ≈ Lifestyle engineering — exercise, diet, sleep — that addresses the **root cause** of disease rather than symptoms.
> - **RCM** ≈ Personalized medicine — different organs receive different care strategies based on criticality and failure consequences.
> - **TPM** ≈ A culture where every family member looks after their own health daily (autonomous care), not just the doctor.

This analogy makes the philosophy of each strategy immediately obvious to a first-time learner.

### 1.4 Why Maintenance Strategies Matter — KTU Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industry studies repeatedly show that **properly chosen maintenance strategies can reduce plant downtime by 30–70%**, **cut maintenance costs by 20–40%**, and **extend asset life by 20–50%**. In modern petrochemical, power, and manufacturing industries, the **maintenance budget alone can exceed 15–60%** of total production costs — making strategy selection a board-level decision.

### 1.5 Strategic Hierarchy Diagram (Conceptual Map)

The strategies are not mutually exclusive — mature industries apply a **mix** where critical assets are managed predictively, semi-critical assets preventively, and non-critical assets reactively. The trade-off axis is **Cost vs. Risk vs. Information Availability**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Strategy 1 — Reactive (Breakdown) Maintenance

**Operating Logic:**
- No maintenance activity is performed until a failure occurs.
- The asset is repaired or replaced **post-failure**.
- Suited to **non-critical, redundant, low-cost** assets where failure has minimal safety/economic consequence.

**Decision Logic Steps:**
1. Asset fails → production halts (or redundant unit takes over).
2. Maintenance crew is dispatched.
3. Repair is performed (corrective action).
4. Asset is restarted and returned to service.

**Advantages:**
- Zero planned maintenance cost.
- Maximum component utilization (uses full service life).
- Minimal inventory of spares.

**Disadvantages:**
- Unplanned downtime — typically **3 to 10 times costlier** than planned downtime.
- Safety risks, secondary damage (cascade failures).
- Higher spare-parts inventory held as emergency buffer.

**Real-World Utility:** Used historically (pre-1950s) in low-tech industries; today confined to items like office furniture, light bulbs, and very low-criticality spares.

---

### 2.2 Strategy 2 — Preventive (Scheduled / Time-Based) Maintenance

**Operating Logic:**
- Maintenance is executed at **pre-determined intervals** regardless of asset condition.
- Interval may be **calendar-based** (every 6 months) or **usage-based** (every 5000 hours).
- Driven by OEM recommendations, statutory regulations, or historical failure statistics.

**Decision Logic Steps:**
1. Establish base interval *T* (from OEM / Weibull analysis).
2. Schedule downtime for inspection, lubrication, calibration, replacement.
3. Execute planned task during shutdown.
4. Record work and reset the interval clock.

**Categories of Preventive Maintenance:**
- **Scheduled Restoration** — disassemble, inspect, restore to like-new condition at fixed intervals.
- **Scheduled Discard** — replace component at fixed interval regardless of wear (e.g., aircraft tires, fire extinguishers).

**Advantages:**
- Reduces unplanned downtime.
- Better spare parts planning.
- Improved safety and statutory compliance.

**Disadvantages:**
- Performs maintenance even when not needed (over-maintenance).
- Cost of preventive action may exceed failure cost for low-criticality items.
- Does not adapt to actual wear — may fail before schedule or be replaced while still healthy.

---

### 2.3 Strategy 3 — Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance

**Operating Logic:**
- Maintenance is performed **only when monitored condition indicators** show degradation.
- Relies on continuous or periodic monitoring: **vibration, thermography, oil analysis, acoustic emission, ultrasonic, motor current signature analysis (MCSA), etc.**
- Triggered by threshold-crossing logic.

**Decision Logic Steps:**
1. Install sensors / use portable instruments.
2. Collect baseline "healthy" signatures.
3. Trend key parameters over time.
4. Apply **alarm bands** (e.g., ISO 10816 vibration zones).
5. Trigger maintenance only when threshold is breached.

**Advantages:**
- Maximizes component utilization (no over-maintenance).
- Allows failure prediction **weeks or months in advance**.
- Reduces spares inventory by 20–30%.
- Supports **root-cause analysis** through trend data.

**Disadvantages:**
- High initial investment (sensors, software, trained analysts).
- Requires **skilled manpower** to interpret data.
- Not feasible for low-value or inaccessible assets.

---

### 2.4 Strategy 4 — Proactive (Root-Cause / Precision) Maintenance

**Operating Logic:**
- Focuses on **eliminating the failure mechanism itself**, not just its symptoms.
- Key activities: precision alignment, precision balancing, lubrication contamination control, elimination of resonance, root-cause failure analysis (RCFA).
- Originated from John Moubray's *Reliability-Centered Maintenance* philosophy.

**Five Pillars of Proactive Maintenance:**
1. Precision alignment (laser-based).
2. Precision balancing.
3. Lubrication excellence (oil cleanliness codes ISO 4406).
4. Vibration / resonance control.
5. Operating-condition optimization.

**Real-World Utility:** Used in high-speed rotating equipment (turbines, compressors, pumps) where imbalance, misalignment, and contamination cause > 80% of failures.

---

### 2.5 Strategy 5 — Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)

**Operating Logic:**
- A **systematic logic-tree methodology** (SAE JA1011 / JA1012 standard) used to determine the optimal maintenance strategy for **each failure mode of each asset**.
- Built on **seven classical questions** (the Moubray RCM question set).
- Outputs a unique mix of preventive, predictive, and run-to-failure tasks per failure mode.

**RCM Decision Flow (Condensed):**
1. What are the functions and performance standards of the asset?
2. In what ways can it fail to fulfill them?
3. What causes each functional failure?
4. What happens when each failure occurs? (consequences)
5. In what way does each failure matter? (safety, environmental, operational, non-operational)
6. What can be done to prevent each failure? (proactive task design)
7. What should be done if a suitable preventive task cannot be found? (default = run-to-failure + redesign)

---

### 2.6 Strategy 6 — Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

**Operating Logic:**
- Originated in **Japan (Nippondenso, 1971)** as part of the broader **Lean Manufacturing** movement.
- Philosophy: **every operator owns the equipment they operate** — daily cleaning, lubrication, inspection, tightening.
- Built around **eight pillars** (autonomous maintenance, planned maintenance, quality maintenance, focused improvement, early management, training, safety, TPM in administration).

**Eight Big Losses Addressed by TPM:**
1. Breakdown losses
2. Setup / adjustment losses
3. Minor stoppages
4. Reduced speed losses
5. Defect / rework losses
6. Start-up losses
7. Shutdown losses
8. Management / clerical losses

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative expression you will need for solving Module-1 numerical problems in PEPET863.

| **Metric / Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | $\mathrm{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum t_{\text{up},i}}{N_f}$ | hours | Average up-time between consecutive failures |
| MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) | $\mathrm{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum t_{\text{repair},i}}{N_f}$ | hours | Average downtime per failure event |
| MDT (Mean Down Time) | $\mathrm{MDT} = \mathrm{MTTR} + \mathrm{Logistic\_Delay}$ | hours | Includes spares mobilization delay |
| Inherent Availability $A_i$ | $A_i = \dfrac{\mathrm{MTBF}}{\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Theoretical availability (ideal support) |
| Operational Availability $A_o$ | $A_o = \dfrac{\mathrm{MTBF}}{\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MDT}}$ | dimensionless | Real-world availability including delays |
| Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$ | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | failures / hour | Instantaneous hazard rate |
| Reliability $R(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Probability of survival to time $t$ (constant $\lambda$) |
| OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | $\mathrm{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless | World-class benchmark $= 85\%$ |
| Total Maintenance Cost $C_{\text{tot}}$ | $C_{\text{tot}} = C_{\text{prev}}(t) + C_{\text{down}}(t) + C_{\text{fail}}(t)$ | currency / unit time | Sum of preventive + downtime + failure costs |
| Optimum PM Interval $T^*$ | $\dfrac{dC_{\text{tot}}}{dT} = 0$ | hours | Where marginal cost of prevention = marginal cost of failure |
| Weibull Shape Parameter $\beta$ | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | dimensionless | $\beta<1$: infant mortality; $\beta=1$: random; $\beta>1$: wear-out |
| Bathtub Curve Hazard | $\lambda_{\text{total}}(t) = \lambda_{\text{infant}} + \lambda_{\text{useful}} + \lambda_{\text{wear}}$ | failures/hour | Three-region failure-rate model |

**Critical Pitfall Reminder:** When writing availability expressions in your answer sheet, **never confuse $A_i$ and $A_o$** — examiners frequently award marks for correctly identifying whether **logistic delay** is included.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** RCM-driven, predictive maintenance, with mandatory scheduled discard of safety-critical parts (no run-to-failure allowed).
- **Power Generation:** Predictive vibration monitoring on turbines, with RCM logic-tree defining PM tasks per failure mode.
- **Petrochemical & Refineries:** TPM + RCM hybrid; SAP-based CMMS for work order management.
- **Manufacturing (Automotive, FMCG):** Heavy TPM culture with OEE dashboards above each production line.
- **Marine & Shipping:** Proactive maintenance dominates due to harsh duty cycles and remote operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inherent Availability from First Principles

The **availability** of a repairable system at any instant *t* is the probability that the system is in the *operating* (UP) state at time *t*. For a single-unit, non-repairable case this is simply the reliability $R(t)$. For a **repairable** system in long-run steady-state operation, we reason from the renewal-reward theorem:

> **Logical Premise:** Over a very long observation window $T$, a repairable asset cycles repeatedly through UP and DOWN states. The fraction of time spent in the UP state equals the system's long-run availability.

**Step 1 — Define the observation window.**
Let $T$ be a very long observation period (e.g., one year). In this window, the asset experiences $N_f$ failures.

**Step 2 — Express total UP-time.**
Each UP period between two consecutive failures has average duration $\mathrm{MTBF}$. Therefore:

$$\text{Total UP-time} = N_f \cdot \mathrm{MTBF}$$

**Step 3 — Express total DOWN-time.**
Each repair action has average duration $\mathrm{MTTR}$. Therefore:

$$\text{Total DOWN-time} = N_f \cdot \mathrm{MTTR}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the availability definition.**
By the renewal-reward theorem, availability is the ratio of expected UP-time to total time:

$$A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\text{Expected UP-time in } T}{T}$$

For one full cycle of length $\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MTTR}$:

$$A = \frac{\mathrm{MTBF}}{\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MTTR}}$$

**Step 5 — Distinguish Inherent vs Operational.**
If we consider **only the active repair time** (ideal logistic support, spares on-shelf), we obtain **Inherent Availability**:

$$A_i = \frac{\mathrm{MTBF}}{\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MTTR}}$$

If we include **logistic/administrative delays** (waiting for spares, technician mobilization, permits), we replace MTTR with **MDT (Mean Down Time)** to obtain **Operational Availability**:

$$A_o = \frac{\mathrm{MTBF}}{\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MDT}}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example.**
A centrifugal pump has MTBF = 900 hours and MTTR = 12 hours (with active repair only). When spares logistics are included, MDT rises to 24 hours.

$$A_i = \frac{900}{900 + 12} = \frac{900}{912} \approx 0.9868 \quad (98.68\%)$$

$$A_o = \frac{900}{900 + 24} = \frac{900}{924} \approx 0.9740 \quad (97.40\%)$$

**Interpretation:** Even a 12-hour logistic delay drops availability by **~1.3 percentage points** — a significant cost driver in plant economics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimum Preventive Maintenance Interval $T^*$

The **optimum PM interval** is the value of $T$ (in operating hours or km) that minimizes the **total expected maintenance cost per unit time**. This is a classical optimization problem in maintenance engineering.

**Step 1 — Define cost components per unit time.**

- $C_p$ = cost of one preventive maintenance action (currency)
- $C_f$ = cost of one corrective (failure) action (currency); typically $C_f \gg C_p$
- $C_d$ = production loss per unit downtime (currency/hour)
- $T$ = interval between PM actions (hours)

**Step 2 — Expected number of failures per unit time.**
Assume failure probability within interval $T$ is $F(T)$ (from Weibull or exponential reliability model). Then expected failures per cycle $= F(T)$.

**Step 3 — Expected downtime per unit time.**
Average downtime per cycle $= \mathrm{MTTR} \cdot F(T)$ (neglecting scheduled PM downtime for simplicity, or adding it separately). Expected cost per unit time:

$$C_{\text{tot}}(T) = \frac{C_p + C_f \cdot F(T) + C_d \cdot \mathrm{MTTR} \cdot F(T)}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\,C_{\text{tot}}}{dT} = 0$$

For the **exponential reliability** case $F(T) = 1 - e^{-\lambda T}$, the optimality condition simplifies (for $C_d = 0$ and large $C_f / C_p$) to the well-known engineering rule:

$$T^* \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_p}{\lambda^2 \cdot C_f}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.**
A motor has failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ / hour, $C_p = \text{₹}15{,}000$, $C_f = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$.

$$\lambda^2 = 1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T^* \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 15000}{1 \times 10^{-6} \times 150000}} = \sqrt{\frac{30000}{0.15}} = \sqrt{200000} \approx 447 \text{ hours}$$

So preventive maintenance should be scheduled roughly every **447 hours** of operation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

**Step 1 — Define the three multiplicative components.**

$$A = \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$$

$$P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}$$

$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$$

**Step 2 — Combine.**

$$\mathrm{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**
A CNC machine runs 480 min/day planned. Downtime losses = 60 min. Ideal cycle = 1.2 min/piece; produces 320 pieces in operating time. 18 pieces are rejects.

$$A = \frac{480 - 60}{480} = 0.875$$

$$P = \frac{1.2 \times 320}{420} = \frac{384}{420} \approx 0.914$$

$$Q = \frac{320 - 18}{320} = \frac{302}{320} \approx 0.944$$

$$\mathrm{OEE} = 0.875 \times 0.914 \times 0.944 \approx 0.755 \quad \text{or} \ 75.5\%$$

This is **typical for an average Indian manufacturing plant** (world-class OEE $\geq$ 85%).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Strategy Selection Matrix

The table below is the **decision-making backbone** that maps an asset's characteristics to the optimal strategy. This is high-yield content for KTU viva and 14-mark questions.

| **Asset Characteristic** | **Failure Cost $C_f$** | **Failure Mode Predictability** | **Monitoring Feasibility** | **Recommended Strategy** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Non-critical, low-value, redundant | Very low | Random | Not feasible | Reactive |
| Safety / environmental critical | Very high | Wear-out (known) | Possible | Scheduled Preventive + Inspection |
| High value, rotating equipment | High | Wear-out | Easily monitored | Predictive (vibration, oil) |
| High-speed machinery, contamination-prone | High | Overheating, imbalance | Feasible | Proactive (precision maintenance) |
| Complex multi-mode systems | Variable | Multi-mode | Partial | RCM logic-tree |
| Entire plant / production line | Aggregate | All modes | Across organization | TPM |

---

### 3.5 Reliability Function Comparison

For **constant failure rate** (random failure, useful life region):

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

For **Weibull** distribution (covers infant mortality, useful life, and wear-out):

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Behavior of $\beta$:**
- $\beta < 1$ → decreasing failure rate → infant mortality (debugging phase).
- $\beta = 1$ → constant failure rate → exponential / random.
- $\beta > 1$ → increasing failure rate → wear-out (use PM or predictive here).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Maintenance Strategy Evolution Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    baseA["Reactive Maintenance"]:::base --> baseB["Preventive Maintenance"]:::base
    baseB --> baseC["Predictive Maintenance"]:::mid
    baseC --> baseD["Proactive Maintenance"]:::mid
    baseD --> baseE["RCM Framework"]:::top
    baseE --> baseF["TPM and Asset Management"]:::top

    subgraph L1["Maturity Hierarchy"]
      direction TB
      L1a["Level 1 - Run to Failure"]
      L1b["Level 2 - Scheduled Tasks"]
      L1c["Level 3 - Condition Triggered"]
      L1d["Level 4 - Root Cause Eliminated"]
      L1e["Level 5 - Logic Tree Optimized"]
      L1f["Level 6 - Culture of Ownership"]
    end

    baseA --- L1a
    baseB --- L1b
    baseC --- L1c
    baseD --- L1d
    baseE --- L1e
    baseF --- L1f

    classDef base fill:#FFD8A8,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mid fill:#A8D8FF,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef top fill:#B6FFB6,stroke:#1A661A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Functional Architecture of a Modern Maintenance Management System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Plant Equipment Layer"]:::layer1 --> s2["Sensor and Data Acquisition"]:::layer1
    s2 --> s3["Condition Monitoring Diagnostics"]:::layer2
    s3 --> s4["Maintenance Strategy Engine"]:::layer3
    s4 --> s5["Work Order and CMMS"]:::layer3
    s5 --> s6["Maintenance Crew and Execution"]:::layer4
    s6 --> s7["Performance Feedback and KPIs"]:::layer4
    s7 --> s4
    s7 --> s3

    subgraph kpi["KPI Loop"]
      k1["MTBF MTTR Availability"]
      k2["OEE Reliability"]
      k3["Cost per Unit Production"]
    end
    s7 --- kpi

    classDef layer1 fill:#FFE599,stroke:#7F6000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef layer2 fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef layer3 fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef layer4 fill:#D5A6BD,stroke:#741B47,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — RCM Decision Logic Tree (Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["Identify Asset Functions"] --> q2["List Functional Failures"]
    q2 --> q3["Identify Failure Modes and Causes"]
    q3 --> q4["Evaluate Failure Consequences"]
    q4 --> q5a{"Safety or Environmental Impact?"}
    q4 --> q5b{"Hidden Failure Possible?"}
    q5a -- YES --> q6a["Mandatory Scheduled PM or Redesign"]
    q5a -- NO --> q5b
    q5b -- YES --> q6b["Scheduled Failure Finding Task"]
    q5b -- NO --> q7{"Economic Consequence Significant?"}
    q7 -- YES --> q8["Predictive or Preventive Task"]
    q7 -- NO --> q9["Run to Failure Acceptable"]

    classDef decision fill:#FFD966,stroke:#B45F06,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef action fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef terminal fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — Strategy Selection Flowchart (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Asset Under Review"] --> crit{"Criticality?"}
    crit -- LOW --> react["Reactive Maintenance"]
    crit -- MEDIUM --> prev["Preventive Maintenance"]
    crit -- HIGH --> monitor{"Condition Monitoring Feasible?"}
    monitor -- YES --> pred["Predictive Maintenance"]
    monitor -- NO --> pmp["Scheduled Preventive Plus Inspection"]
    pred --> optimize{"Failure Mechanism Known?"}
    optimize -- YES --> pro["Proactive Root Cause Elimination"]
    optimize -- NO --> rcm["RCM Study"]
    pro --> tpm["Integrate into TPM Culture"]

    classDef decision fill:#FFE599,stroke:#7F6000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef strategy fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef terminal fill:#D5A6BD,stroke:#741B47,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Reactive Maintenance. State any two of its advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**
Reactive maintenance (also called breakdown or run-to-failure maintenance) is a strategy in which **no maintenance action is taken until an asset actually fails**. The component is repaired or replaced after failure.
- *Advantages:* (i) zero planned maintenance cost; (ii) maximum component utilization since service life is fully consumed.
- *Disadvantages:* (i) unplanned downtime, which is typically 3–10 times costlier than planned downtime; (ii) high safety risk and possible cascade damage to surrounding equipment.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Calendar or usage interval | Condition parameter threshold |
| Information required | OEM recommendation, Weibull data | Vibration, thermography, oil analysis data |
| Cost profile | High routine cost | Lower long-term cost, high initial sensor cost |
| Over-maintenance | Yes, common | Minimized |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the six major maintenance strategies with a clear strategy-selection decision logic. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Maintenance strategies can be organized as a maturity hierarchy.

1. **Reactive Maintenance:** Service only after failure. Logic: choose when asset is **non-critical, low value, redundant** (e.g., light bulbs, backup units). Cost optimum when failure cost $C_f$ is small.

2. **Preventive Maintenance:** Service at **fixed intervals** (calendar or usage based). Logic: choose when **failure mode is wear-out** with known statistical life (e.g., rolling element bearings under steady load).

3. **Predictive Maintenance:** Service **only when monitored condition crosses a threshold**. Logic: choose when **failure is progressive and measurable** — vibration, temperature, wear-debris trends can give early warning (e.g., ISO 10816 zones).

4. **Proactive Maintenance:** Eliminate **root causes of failure**. Logic: choose when failures repeat from known mechanisms such as misalignment, imbalance, contamination, or resonance (e.g., high-speed rotating machinery).

5. **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM):** Apply **the seven-question logic tree** to determine the optimal task per failure mode. Logic: choose for **complex multi-mode assets** in regulated industries (aircraft, nuclear, petrochemical).

6. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM):** Organization-wide **autonomous maintenance culture** built on eight pillars. Logic: choose for **continuous-process industries** where OEE and operator ownership drive competitiveness.

The **decision logic** is: classify asset by **criticality** → classify failure mode by **detectability** → classify failure mechanism by **eliminability** → apply the strategy that minimizes *life-cycle cost* while satisfying *safety/environmental* constraints.

**(b)** A motor has MTBF = 2000 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. With spares logistics included, MDT = 20 hours. Compute (i) Inherent Availability, (ii) Operational Availability, (iii) downtime per year (8760 hours). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Inherent Availability:**

$$A_i = \frac{\mathrm{MTBF}}{\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MTTR}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \frac{2000}{2008} \approx 0.9960 \quad \text{or} \ 99.60\%$$

**(ii) Operational Availability:**

$$A_o = \frac{\mathrm{MTBF}}{\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MDT}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 20} = \frac{2000}{2020} \approx 0.9901 \quad \text{or} \ 99.01\%$$

**(iii) Downtime per year:**

$$\text{Operating time} = A_o \times 8760 = 0.9901 \times 8760 \approx 8673.3 \ \text{hours}$$

$$\text{Downtime per year} = 8760 - 8673.3 \approx 86.7 \ \text{hours}$$

This corresponds to roughly **3.6 days** of downtime per year.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** decision logic tree. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The RCM methodology, formalized in **SAE JA1011 / JA1012**, asks seven questions about every failure mode of every asset:

1. What are the functions and associated performance standards of the asset in its present operating context?
2. In what ways does it fail to fulfil its functions?
3. What causes each functional failure?
4. What happens when each failure occurs?
5. In what way does each failure matter? (Safety, Environmental, Operational, Non-operational consequences)
6. What can be done to prevent or predict each failure?
7. What should be done if a suitable preventive task cannot be found?

**Decision logic — using a tree (illustrated in Diagram 4.3):**

- **Step 1:** Identify failure modes.
- **Step 2:** Check safety/environmental consequence. If yes → mandatory scheduled PM or redesign.
- **Step 3:** Check if failure is hidden (no immediate warning). If yes → scheduled failure-finding task.
- **Step 4:** Check economic consequence. If significant → predictive or preventive task.
- **Step 5:** If none of the above → run-to-failure acceptable, but document.

RCM produces a **unique mix of tasks per failure mode**, avoiding the one-size-fits-all approach of pure preventive maintenance.

**(b)** A pump has failure rate $\lambda = 0.002$/hour. Cost of preventive action $C_p = \text{₹}20{,}000$. Cost of corrective action $C_f = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$. Find the optimum PM interval $T^*$ using $T^* \approx \sqrt{2 C_p / (\lambda^2 C_f)}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$\lambda = 0.002 \ \text{per hour}, \quad \lambda^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20000}{4 \times 10^{-6} \times 200000}}$$

$$T^* = \sqrt{\frac{40000}{0.8}} = \sqrt{50000}$$

$$T^* \approx 223.6 \ \text{hours}$$

So the optimum preventive maintenance interval is **approximately 224 hours** of operation.

**Verification of cost-effectiveness:** Without PM, expected failures per 224-hour block $= 1 - e^{-0.002 \times 224} = 1 - e^{-0.448} \approx 0.361$. Expected cost without PM in 224 hours $\approx 0.361 \times 200000 = \text{₹}72{,}200$, which exceeds the preventive cost of ₹20,000 — confirming PM is economically justified at this interval.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Maintenance Strategy Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating availability terms** — writing $A_i$ where the question clearly demands $A_o$ (or vice versa) costs 1–2 marks instantly. Always read the question carefully: *"with logistic delays"* $\Rightarrow A_o$; *"ideal support"* $\Rightarrow A_i$.
> 2. **Omitting units** — MTBF in hours, MTTR in hours, $T^*$ in hours. Examiners explicitly deduct for missing units.
> 3. **Listing strategies without decision logic** — KTU examiners expect you to **justify WHY** a strategy is chosen, not merely enumerate definitions.
> 4. **Forgetting the Weibull shape parameter interpretation** — questions on $\beta < 1$, $\beta = 1$, $\beta > 1$ are frequent. Memorize the three-region bathtub mapping.
> 5. **TPM answer without the eight pillars or eight losses** — a 7-mark TPM answer is considered incomplete without naming them.
> 6. **RCM answer without the seven classical questions** — same penalty.
> 7. **Cost-optimization numericals without verifying economic feasibility** — always check $C_p$ vs. expected $C_f \cdot F(T)$ before concluding.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maintenance Strategies**
>
> - **Six canonical strategies:** Reactive, Preventive, Predictive, Proactive, RCM, TPM — each with a specific decision trigger.
> - **Reactive** = no action until failure; cheap up-front, catastrophic on the day.
> - **Preventive** = fixed interval; ideal for **wear-out failure modes** with known statistical life.
> - **Predictive** = condition-triggered; needs sensors and analysts; **highest long-term ROI**.
> - **Proactive** = root-cause elimination; pillars are alignment, balancing, lubrication, vibration, operation.
> - **RCM** = seven-question logic tree; **per failure mode** strategy assignment.
> - **TPM** = eight pillars, eight losses; **operator-owned daily maintenance**; aims at **OEE $\geq$ 85%**.
> - **MTBF** = average UP-time between failures.
> - **MTTR** = average active repair time.
> - **MDT** = MTTR + logistic delay (spares, mobilization, permits).
> - **$A_i = $ MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)**, **$A_o = $ MTBF / (MTBF + MDT)**.
> - **OEE = $A \times P \times Q$** — world-class benchmark is **85%**.
> - **Weibull $\beta$**: < 1 (infant mortality), = 1 (random), > 1 (wear-out).
> - **Optimum PM interval**: $T^* \approx \sqrt{2 C_p / (\lambda^2 C_f)}$ for the exponential case.
> - **Strategy selection is asset-specific**, not plant-wide — apply **RCM logic** to choose.
> - **Modern plants use a hybrid mix**: predictive for critical, preventive for semi-critical, reactive for non-critical.
> - **Always include units** in numerical answers — marks are deducted otherwise.
> - **Always state assumptions** in derivations (e.g., constant $\lambda$, exponential reliability).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organization and Spare Parts Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Organization and Spare Parts Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Maintenance Organization

**Maintenance Organization** is the formally structured, hierarchical arrangement of human resources, technical personnel, support staff, infrastructure, and procedural frameworks established within an industrial enterprise to plan, execute, and control all activities related to preserving, restoring, and improving the operational availability of physical assets.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for *Condition Monitoring and Maintenance Management*, the maintenance organization is not merely a chart of names and titles; it is a **functional ecosystem** that integrates reliability engineering, condition monitoring, planning, scheduling, and execution under a unified managerial vision.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor**
> *A maintenance organization defines "who does what, when, with which tools, and under whose authority" — translating maintenance policy into measurable Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) such as **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** and **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large, modern hospital. The hospital does not just employ doctors; it has a Chief Medical Officer, specialists, nurses, lab technicians, pharmacists, ambulance drivers, and a billing department. Each plays a defined role so that a patient (the **machine**) gets the right treatment at the right time.

In the same way, a **Maintenance Organization** in a plant has:
- A **Maintenance Manager** (like the Chief Medical Officer) — overall strategy.
- **Engineers / Planners** (like specialists) — diagnostics and planning.
- **Technicians / Craftsmen** (like nurses) — hands-on repair.
- **Stores / Spare Parts Officers** (like pharmacists) — ensuring parts availability.
- **Condition Monitoring Engineers** (like lab technicians) — continuous health checks.

Without this structure, even a factory full of spare parts and skilled workers would descend into chaos — repairs would be delayed, parts misplaced, and failures unmanaged.

## 1.2 Spare Parts Management

**Spare Parts Management (SPM)** is the scientific discipline concerned with the planning, identification, classification, procurement, storage, issue, and replenishment of replacement components, assemblies, and consumables required to sustain the operational availability of equipment at minimum total cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Pillars of Spare Parts Management (as per KTU 2024 Module 1)**
> 1. **Classification** — ABC, VED, FSN, SDE, SOS analysis
> 2. **Forecasting** — demand estimation based on failure rates
> 3. **Procurement** — EOQ-based ordering and lead-time control
> 4. **Storage** — bin location, FIFO/FEFO, and environmental control
> 5. **Disposal** — write-off and scrapping of obsolete stock

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your kitchen pantry. You keep **salt, sugar, and oil** in large quantities (you use them daily), **spices** in moderate amounts (used often but in small doses), and **saffron or exotic herbs** in tiny containers (rarely used but very expensive). You would never buy 10 kg of saffron — it's wasteful — and you would never run out of salt — it's essential. Spare parts management applies this same **rational prioritization** to industrial components, balancing **availability** against **capital lock-up**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric**
> A well-managed plant typically holds spare parts inventory equivalent to **1.5% to 3%** of the **replacement value of installed equipment (RV)**. Excess indicates poor management; deficit indicates high risk of downtime.

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | KTU-Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Lead Time (LT)** | Time between placing a purchase order and receiving the part at stores |
| **Reorder Point (ROP)** | Inventory level at which a fresh order must be triggered |
| **Safety Stock (SS)** | Buffer inventory to absorb fluctuations in demand and lead time |
| **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** | Order size that minimizes **total cost** (ordering + holding) |
| **ABC Class** | Items classified by **Annual Consumption Value** (A = vital few, C = trivial many) |
| **VED Class** | Items classified by **criticality** (V = Vital, E = Essential, D = Desirable) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Maintenance Organization — Structural Levels

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes three structural levels of a maintenance organization:

### 2.1.1 Top Management Level (Strategic)
- **Maintenance Director / Head of Maintenance**
- Functions: Policy formulation, budget approval, KPI approval, capital planning.
- Reports to: **Plant Head / Vice President (Operations)**.

### 2.1.2 Middle Management Level (Tactical)
- **Maintenance Engineers, Planners, Section Heads, Condition Monitoring Engineers.**
- Functions: Job planning, scheduling, work-order preparation, root cause analysis, CM (Condition Monitoring) data interpretation.
- Reports to: Maintenance Director.

### 2.1.3 Lower / Supervisory Level (Operational)
- **Foremen, Chargehands, Technicians, Craftsmen, Helpers.**
- Functions: Hands-on execution of preventive, predictive, and corrective maintenance tasks.
- Reports to: Middle-level engineers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Classic Organizational Models (High-Yield for KTU)**
> 1. **Centralized Maintenance** — A single, plant-wide maintenance department serves all production units. *Advantages:* Specialized tooling, lower manpower redundancy, easier skill pooling. *Disadvantages:* Slow response, communication lag.
> 2. **Decentralized Maintenance** — Each production unit has its own maintenance team. *Advantages:* Fast response, unit-specific expertise. *Disadvantages:* Duplication of resources.
> 3. **Combined / Hybrid Model** — Routine, low-skill jobs handled by production operators; specialist jobs handled by central maintenance. *Most widely used in modern plants.*

## 2.2 Functions of a Maintenance Organization

1. **Planning & Scheduling** — converting maintenance needs into actionable work orders.
2. **Execution** — performing preventive, predictive, breakdown, and corrective tasks.
3. **Condition Monitoring** — vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis, ultrasonic testing.
4. **Spare Parts Management** — inventory control, classification, procurement.
5. **Documentation & History** — failure logs, MTBF/MTTR tracking, ISO 9001 / 55001 records.
6. **Training & Development** — upgrading technician skills.
7. **Budgeting & Cost Control** — maintenance budget as % of Replacement Asset Value (RAV).
8. **Contract Management** — handling outsourced maintenance services (AMC, O&M).

## 2.3 Spare Parts Classification Techniques

### 2.3.1 ABC Analysis (Always Better Control)

Based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**. Items are ranked by their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)**:

$$\text{ACV} = \text{Annual Usage Quantity} \times \text{Unit Price}$$

| Class | % of Items | % of Annual Value | Control Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | ~10–20% | ~70–80% | Tight control, frequent review, accurate records |
| **B** | ~20–30% | ~15–25% | Moderate control, periodic review |
| **C** | ~60–70% | ~5–10% | Loose control, bulk ordering |

### 2.3.2 VED Analysis (Vital, Essential, Desirable)

| Class | Meaning | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **V — Vital** | Breakdown stops plant, no substitute, high downtime cost | Must always stock |
| **E — Essential** | Causes significant production loss, substitute available with effort | Stock based on lead time |
| **D — Desirable** | Causes minor inconvenience, substitute easily found | Stock minimally or order as needed |

### 2.3.3 FSN Analysis (Fast, Slow, Non-moving)

- **F** — Fast moving: high issue frequency → maintain continuous stock.
- **S** — Slow moving: moderate issue frequency → periodic review.
- **N** — Non-moving: not issued for 12+ months → candidates for disposal / write-off.

### 2.3.4 SDE & SOS Analysis

- **SDE** — Scarce, Difficult, Easy (based on procurement difficulty).
- **SOS** — Seasonal, Off-Seasonal (used in industries with cyclical demand).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The syllabus specifically mentions **ABC, VED, and FSN**. SDE and SOS are auxiliary; expect them as 2- or 3-mark sub-parts.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Economic Order Quantity (Wilson's Formula) | units |
| $D$ | Annual demand | units/year |
| $S$ | Ordering cost per order | ₹/order |
| $H$ | Holding cost per unit per year | ₹/unit·year |
| $T_c = \dfrac{DS}{\text{EOQ}} + \dfrac{H \cdot \text{EOQ}}{2}$ | Total annual inventory cost | ₹/year |
| $\text{ROP} = d \cdot L + \text{SS}$ | Reorder Point | units |
| $d$ | Average daily demand | units/day |
| $L$ | Lead Time | days |
| $\text{SS} = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Safety Stock (variable lead time) | units |
| $Z$ | Service-level z-score (e.g., 1.65 for 95%) | dimensionless |
| $\sigma_d$ | Std. dev. of daily demand | units/day |
| $\text{ACV} = U \cdot P$ | Annual Consumption Value | ₹ |
| $U$ | Annual usage | units |
| $P$ | Unit price | ₹ |
| $N = \dfrac{D}{\text{EOQ}}$ | Number of orders per year | orders/year |
| $T = \dfrac{1}{N} \cdot 365$ | Time between orders | days |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the above table, absolute value or "divided by" has been written as `\dfrac` to ensure no vertical pipe `|` character is introduced, preserving the markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Power Plants:** Spare rotor, stator windings, bearing assemblies — managed via **VED + ABC** combined matrix.
- **Aerospace:** Every component is tracked via **S/N (serial number)** with **life-limited parts** replaced based on cycles/hours, not failure.
- **Manufacturing:** ABC analysis frees up working capital locked in C-class dust-collector spares.
- **Shipping:** FSN analysis identifies obsolete bunker spares occupying expensive onboard storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Wilson's Formula

The **EOQ model** assumes:
- Constant demand rate ($D$).
- Constant lead time ($L$).
- No stock-outs allowed.
- Instantaneous replenishment (infinite replenishment rate).
- Order cost ($S$) and holding cost ($H$) are independent of order size.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up Total Cost

The total annual inventory cost $T_c$ has two components:

**1. Annual Ordering Cost:**
$$T_o = \dfrac{D}{\text{EOQ}} \cdot S$$

*Logic:* If we order $Q$ units each time, we need $D / Q$ orders per year, each costing $S$.

**2. Annual Holding Cost:**
$$T_h = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

*Logic:* Average inventory is $Q / 2$ (linearly depletes from $Q$ to $0$), held for the full year at $H$ per unit.

**3. Total Cost:**
$$T_c = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}$$

### 3.1.2 Minimization by Calculus

We minimize $T_c$ with respect to $Q$ by setting the first derivative to zero:

$$\dfrac{dT_c}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$\dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{DS}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{2DS}{H}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{EOQ}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}}$$

### 3.1.3 Second-Derivative Verification

$$\dfrac{d^2 T_c}{dQ^2} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $T_c$ is a **minimum** at the critical point. ✔

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A plant consumes **4,000 units/year** of a critical bearing. The ordering cost is **₹500/order**, and the holding cost is **₹20/unit·year**. The lead time is **6 days**, and the safety stock policy is **100 units**. Find EOQ, number of orders, time between orders, and ROP. (Assume 300 working days/year.)

### Step 1: Compute EOQ

$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 4000 \times 500}{20}}$$

$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4{,}000{,}000}{20}}$$

$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{200{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EOQ} \approx 447.21 \text{ units} \approx 448 \text{ units}}$$

### Step 2: Number of Orders per Year

$$N = \dfrac{D}{\text{EOQ}} = \dfrac{4000}{448}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 8.93 \approx 9 \text{ orders/year}}$$

### Step 3: Time Between Orders

$$T = \dfrac{300}{9} \approx 33.3 \text{ days}$$

### Step 4: Average Daily Demand

$$d = \dfrac{D}{300} = \dfrac{4000}{300} = 13.33 \text{ units/day}$$

### Step 5: Reorder Point (ROP)

$$\text{ROP} = d \cdot L + \text{SS}$$

$$\text{ROP} = 13.33 \times 6 + 100$$

$$\text{ROP} = 79.98 + 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{ROP} \approx 180 \text{ units}}$$

### Step 6: Total Minimum Cost

$$T_c = \dfrac{DS}{\text{EOQ}} + \dfrac{H \cdot \text{EOQ}}{2}$$

$$T_c = \dfrac{4000 \times 500}{448} + \dfrac{20 \times 448}{2}$$

$$T_c = 4464.29 + 4480$$

$$\boxed{T_c \approx ₹8{,}944.29 \text{ /year}}$$

## 3.3 ABC Analysis — Step-by-Step Numerical Procedure

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| **1** | List all spare parts. |
| **2** | Determine annual usage $U$ for each. |
| **3** | Determine unit price $P$ for each. |
| **4** | Calculate ACV $= U \times P$ for each. |
| **5** | Sort items in **descending** order of ACV. |
| **6** | Compute cumulative ACV and cumulative % of total ACV. |
| **7** | Apply Pareto cut-offs: top 70–80% → **A**, next 15–25% → **B**, bottom 5–10% → **C**. |

### Quick Verification Example

Suppose a stores list has the following:

| Item | U (units) | P (₹) | ACV (₹) | Cum. ACV | Cum. % | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bearing-1 | 100 | 1000 | 100,000 | 100,000 | 50% | **A** |
| Bearing-2 | 200 | 250 | 50,000 | 150,000 | 75% | **A** |
| Bolt-M10 | 5000 | 5 | 25,000 | 175,000 | 87.5% | **B** |
| Gasket-X | 100 | 25 | 2,500 | 177,500 | 88.75% | **C** |
| Cover-Y | 50 | 50 | 2,500 | 180,000 | 90% | **C** |
| Others (500) | — | — | 20,000 | 200,000 | 100% | **C** |

Cumulative value: A-class 50% (2 items), B-class 12.5% (1 item), C-class 37.5% (503 items). ✔

## 3.4 Combined ABC-VED Matrix (Analytical Depth)

The intersection of ABC (consumption value) and VED (criticality) produces a **9-cell matrix** that drives stocking policy:

| | **V (Vital)** | **E (Essential)** | **D (Desirable)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Stock heavily; tight control | Stock well; review monthly | Stock moderately |
| **B** | Stock well; review quarterly | Stock moderately | Stock minimally |
| **C** | Stock one unit (insurance) | Order on demand | Do not stock |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Items in the **A-V** and **B-V** cells are the *crown jewels* — high value AND high criticality. These demand the most rigorous spares policy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Maintenance Organization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Head / VP Operations] --> B[Maintenance Director]
    B --> C1[Maintenance Planning Section]
    B --> C2[Execution Section]
    B --> C3[Condition Monitoring Section]
    B --> C4[Stores and Spare Parts Section]
    B --> C5[Contracts and Outsourcing Section]
    C1 --> D1[Maintenance Engineer]
    C1 --> D2[Scheduler]
    C2 --> E1[Mechanical Foreman]
    C2 --> E2[Electrical Foreman]
    C2 --> E3[Instrumentation Foreman]
    C3 --> F1[Vibration Analyst]
    C3 --> F2[Thermography Engineer]
    C3 --> F3[Oil Analysis Lab]
    C4 --> G1[Stores Officer]
    C4 --> G2[Inventory Controller]
    E1 --> H1[Mechanical Technicians]
    E2 --> H2[Electrical Technicians]
    E3 --> H3[Instrument Technicians]
```

> **Reading the chart:** Solid arrows denote *direct line-of-command* (reporting hierarchy). Each block represents a distinct functional unit, and information flows upward through KPIs (MTBF, MTTR, Availability %) while resources flow downward.

## 4.2 Spare Parts Management — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[Equipment Master Data]
        I2[Failure History and MTBF]
        I3[Bill of Materials BOM]
        I4[Lead Time Data]
    end

    subgraph CLASSIFY
        K1[ABC Analysis]
        K2[VED Analysis]
```
