```mermaid
        K3[FSN Analysis]
        K4[SDE Analysis]
    end

    subgraph DECIDE
        P1[Determine EOQ]
        P2[Compute Reorder Point]
        P3[Compute Safety Stock]
        P4[Set Min Max Levels]
    end

    subgraph ACT
        S1[Issue Purchase Order]
        S2[Receive and Inspect]
        S3[Binning and FIFO Storage]
        S4[Issue to Maintenance]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL
        R1[KPI Tracking Stockouts]
        R2[ABC Reclassification Annual]
        R3[Disposal of Non Moving]
        R4[Feedback to Planning]
    end

    I1 --> K1
    I2 --> K1
    I3 --> K2
    I4 --> P2
    K1 --> P1
    K2 --> P4
    K3 --> P4
    K4 --> P1
    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S1
    P3 --> P2
    P4 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> K1
```

> **Reading the chart:** This is a **closed-loop system** — feedback from the Control block re-enters the Classification block annually, ensuring the spares list is continuously refined. The architecture mirrors the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle required by ISO 55001 (Asset Management).

## 4.3 Inventory Level Profile Over Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Reorder Level ROP 180 units] --> L2[Lead Time Demand 80 units]
    L2 --> L3[Safety Stock 100 units]
    L3 --> L4[Zero Stock Risk Zone]
    L1 --> L5[Order Receipt 448 units]
    L5 --> L6[Maximum Inventory 548 units]
    L6 --> L7[Average Inventory 274 units]
    L7 --> L8[Steady Depletion to ROP]
    L8 --> L1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define spare parts management. List any four functions of a maintenance organization.**

**Model Answer:**

Spare parts management is the systematic process of planning, classifying, procuring, storing, and issuing replacement components and consumables to ensure the operational availability of equipment at minimum total cost.

**Four functions of a maintenance organization:**
1. **Planning and Scheduling** — converting maintenance needs into work orders.
2. **Condition Monitoring** — continuous health assessment using vibration, thermal, and oil analysis.
3. **Spare Parts Management** — inventory classification and control (ABC, VED).
4. **Budgeting and Cost Control** — managing maintenance spend as a percentage of the Replacement Asset Value (RAV).

*(Examiner's note: Naming 4 clearly and accurately earns full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between ABC and VED analysis of spare parts.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | ABC Analysis | VED Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of classification** | Annual Consumption Value (ACV) | Criticality of the item |
| **Question answered** | *Which items consume the most money?* | *Which items, if missing, will stop the plant?* |
| **Classes** | A, B, C | Vital, Essential, Desirable |
| **Use** | Financial prioritization | Operational prioritization |
| **Output** | Tighter control on A items | Stocking policy based on criticality |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **structure of a maintenance organization** for a large process plant. Discuss the merits of a **centralized vs decentralized** maintenance setup. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The annual demand for a critical pump impeller is **3,600 units**. The ordering cost is **₹400 per order**, and the holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. The lead time is **8 days**, and the company works **300 days/year**. Compute the **EOQ, number of orders per year, time between orders, and reorder point (assuming no safety stock)**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Maintenance Organization Structure (Block Diagram):**

```
PLANT HEAD
    └── MAINTENANCE DIRECTOR
            ├── Planning & Scheduling Cell
            ├── Mechanical Maintenance Cell
            ├── Electrical Maintenance Cell
            ├── Instrumentation & CM Cell
            ├── Stores & Spare Parts Cell
            └── Contracts & Outsourcing Cell
```

Each cell has a **Section Engineer → Foreman → Technicians** hierarchy.

**Centralized Maintenance:**
- *Merits:* Economical use of specialized tools; uniform standards; centralized spares pool; better data analytics.
- *Demerits:* Slow response; communication delay; one team serves many units.

**Decentralized Maintenance:**
- *Merits:* Fast response; better ownership; plant-specific expertise.
- *Demerits:* Duplication of resources; difficulty in standardization.

**Conclusion:** A **combined (hybrid) model** is recommended for large process plants — routine jobs done by production operators, specialist jobs done by the central maintenance team.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $D = 3600$ units/yr, $S = ₹400$/order, $H = ₹10$/unit·yr, $L = 8$ days, working days = 300.

**Step 1: EOQ**
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 3600 \times 400}{10}} = \sqrt{288000}$$
$$\boxed{\text{EOQ} = 536.66 \approx 537 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 2: Number of orders per year**
$$N = \dfrac{D}{\text{EOQ}} = \dfrac{3600}{537} = 6.70 \approx 7 \text{ orders}$$

**Step 3: Time between orders**
$$T = \dfrac{300}{7} = 42.86 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Daily demand**
$$d = \dfrac{3600}{300} = 12 \text{ units/day}$$

**Step 5: Reorder Point (SS = 0)**
$$\text{ROP} = d \cdot L = 12 \times 8 = \boxed{96 \text{ units}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Forgetting to convert **annual demand to daily demand** when computing ROP — common 2-mark loss.
> 2. Using the *EOQ value* as ROP — incorrect; ROP is based on **lead time demand**, not order quantity.
> 3. Reporting EOQ without the **unit "units"** — loses 0.5 mark for dimensional clarity.
> 4. In part (a), students often list the structure but **fail to write merits/demerits** of both models — 3-mark loss.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain the **ABC, VED, and FSN** classification methods of spare parts with their control policies. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing plant uses 5,000 units/year of a lubricant. The ordering cost is **₹600 per order**, the holding cost is **₹15 per unit per year**, the lead time is **10 days**, and the desired service level requires a safety stock of **150 units**. Calculate the **EOQ, the total annual cost, the number of orders per year, and the reorder point**. *(Assume 250 working days/year.)* *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**ABC Analysis:**
Based on **Annual Consumption Value** $ACV = U \times P$. Items are ranked in descending order of ACV. **Class A** (10–20% of items) accounts for 70–80% of value — *tight control*. **Class B** (20–30% of items) accounts for 15–25% — *moderate control*. **Class C** (60–70% of items) accounts for 5–10% — *loose control*.

**VED Analysis:**
- **V (Vital):** Plant stoppage if unavailable. *Always stock.*
- **E (Essential):** Significant loss, but workable substitute exists. *Stock based on lead time.*
- **D (Desirable):** Minor inconvenience. *Stock minimally; order on need.*

**FSN Analysis:**
- **F (Fast):** High issue frequency — *continuous review.*
- **S (Slow):** Moderate — *periodic review.*
- **N (Non-moving):** No issue for 12+ months — *candidates for disposal.*

**Control Policy Summary:**

| Class | Review Frequency | Records | Reorder Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Continuous | Detailed | Tight, EOQ-based |
| B | Monthly | Moderate | Periodic |
| C | Quarterly | Minimal | Bulk/annual |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $D = 5000$ units/yr, $S = ₹600$, $H = ₹15$/unit·yr, $L = 10$ days, $SS = 150$ units, 250 working days/yr.

**Step 1: EOQ**
$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 5000 \times 600}{15}} = \sqrt{400000}$$
$$\boxed{\text{EOQ} = 632.46 \approx 632 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 2: Total Annual Cost**
$$T_c = \dfrac{DS}{\text{EOQ}} + \dfrac{H \cdot \text{EOQ}}{2}$$
$$T_c = \dfrac{5000 \times 600}{632} + \dfrac{15 \times 632}{2}$$
$$T_c = 4746.84 + 4740 = \boxed{₹9{,}486.84 \text{ per year}}$$

**Step 3: Number of orders per year**
$$N = \dfrac{5000}{632} = 7.91 \approx 8 \text{ orders}$$

**Step 4: Daily demand**
$$d = \dfrac{5000}{250} = 20 \text{ units/day}$$

**Step 5: Reorder Point**
$$\text{ROP} = d \cdot L + \text{SS} = 20 \times 10 + 150$$
$$\boxed{\text{ROP} = 350 \text{ units}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B**
> 1. **Not including Safety Stock in ROP** — ROP must include $dL + SS$. Forgetting SS loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Total Cost formula** — using $DS$ instead of $DS/\text{EOQ}$ for the ordering component.
> 3. **Rounding EOQ drastically** (e.g., 600 instead of 632) — affects subsequent calculations; carry 2–3 decimals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Organization & Spare Parts Management**

**Maintenance Organization:**
- ☐ Three levels: **Top (Strategic), Middle (Tactical), Lower (Operational)**.
- ☐ Three models: **Centralized, Decentralized, Combined/Hybrid**.
- ☐ Modern plants use **Condition Monitoring engineers** as a separate vertical.
- ☐ Key KPIs: **MTBF, MTTR, Availability %, Maintenance Cost as % of RAV**.

**Spare Parts Classification:**
- ☐ **ABC** = based on **money value** (Pareto).
- ☐ **VED** = based on **criticality** (functional need).
- ☐ **FSN** = based on **issue frequency** (movement).
- ☐ **SDE** = based on **procurement difficulty**.
- ☐ **SOS** = based on **seasonality** of demand.

**Inventory Formulas (must memorize):**
- ☐ $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — Wilson's formula.
- ☐ $T_c = DS/Q + HQ/2$ — total cost.
- ☐ $\text{ROP} = dL + \text{SS}$ — reorder point.
- ☐ $N = D/Q$ — number of orders/year.
- ☐ $\text{ACV} = U \times P$ — annual consumption value.

**Common Numerical Pitfalls:**
- ☐ Always convert **annual demand → daily demand** for ROP.
- ☐ Always carry **2–3 decimals** in EOQ; do not round prematurely.
- ☐ Always include **units** in your final answer.
- ☐ **Safety stock ≠ 0** unless explicitly stated.

**Board-Exam Favourite Questions:**
- ☐ Derivation of EOQ (full calculus-based).
- ☐ ABC + VED combined matrix (with stocking policy).
- ☐ Maintenance organization diagram with functions.
- ☐ ROP calculation with safety stock.
- ☐ Centralized vs Decentralized — comparison.

> **Final Word:** The KTU 2024 scheme rewards **structured, diagram-supported, formula-correct answers**. Master the three numerical classics (EOQ, ROP, ABC) and the two theoretical anchors (organization structure, classification techniques) and you will comfortably clear all learning levels of Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L4) for this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Methods and Policies of Maintenance Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Various Methods and Policies of Maintenance Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Maintenance Engineering** is the discipline of engineering concerned with the design, organization, planning, and execution of policies and procedures for the effective upkeep of industrial machinery, equipment, and plant assets to ensure their availability, reliability, and operational efficiency throughout their useful life.

A **Maintenance Policy** is a strategic framework of rules, procedures, and decision criteria that determines *when*, *how*, and *what kind* of maintenance activity will be performed on a physical asset to meet the operational objectives of safety, availability, cost-effectiveness, and product quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEPET863 – Module 1), maintenance methods and policies are broadly classified under the umbrella of **Terotechnology** — the science of *installation, commissioning, maintenance, replacement, and removal* of plant machinery.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you bought a car. You have **three basic attitudes** toward taking care of it:

1. **"Fix it only when it breaks down."** — This is **Breakdown Maintenance** (reactive, lazy approach). You drive until the engine seizes, then you tow it to the garage.
2. **"Service it every 5,000 km whether it needs it or not."** — This is **Preventive Maintenance** (scheduled, calendar-based). You change oil, brakes, filters on a fixed schedule.
3. **"Check the engine vibration, oil color, and sound every week, and act only when a sign of wear appears."** — This is **Predictive / Condition-Based Maintenance** (smart, sensor-driven). You wait for actual data to tell you something is wrong.

> **Maintenance Engineering = Choosing the right attitude (Policy) and applying the right tools (Method) to keep machines healthy.**

A **policy** is the *strategy* (the why), while a **method** is the *tactical action* (the how). For example, "minimize downtime" is a **policy**; "vibration analysis once a month" is a **method**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

The following metrics are universally used in maintenance engineering analysis and are mandatory for KTU Part B problems:

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** = Total operating time / Number of failures
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** = Total downtime / Number of failures
- **Availability (A)** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)
- **Reliability (R(t))** = Probability of survival up to time *t*
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** = Availability × Performance × Quality

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The choice of maintenance policy directly affects the *MTBF* and *MTTR* values, and hence the **Availability** of the system. KTU 2024 questions frequently ask students to **compare policies** using these metrics.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Maintenance Methods (Comprehensive)

Maintenance methods are the *operational techniques* used to preserve or restore equipment. They are classified as follows:

#### A. **Breakdown Maintenance (Reactive / Run-to-Failure)**
- Maintenance action is taken **only after a failure has occurred**.
- Equipment is allowed to operate until it stops functioning.
- Suitable for non-critical, low-cost, redundant, or disposable assets (e.g., light bulbs, fuses, cheap hand tools).
- **Advantages:** No upfront planning, low initial cost, no spare inventory for non-critical parts.
- **Disadvantages:** Unplanned downtime, catastrophic secondary damage, safety hazards, higher long-term cost.

#### B. **Preventive Maintenance (Scheduled / Time-Based)**
- Maintenance is performed at **predetermined intervals** (calendar time, running hours, or cycles) *regardless of the actual condition* of the equipment.
- Based on the principle: *"Prevention is better than cure."*
- Sub-types:
  - **Scheduled Overhaul Maintenance** – Complete dismantling and inspection at fixed intervals.
  - **Periodic Inspection Maintenance** – Routine checks (e.g., daily, weekly, monthly).
  - **Servicing Maintenance** – Lubrication, cleaning, calibration on a fixed schedule.
- **Advantages:** Planned downtime, reduced catastrophic failures, predictable spares demand.
- **Disadvantages:** Possible over-maintenance, under-utilization of component life, unnecessary labor cost.

#### C. **Predictive Maintenance (Condition-Based Maintenance – CBM)**
- Maintenance is performed **only when monitoring data indicates a decline in performance** or an impending failure.
- Uses real-time or periodic sensor data: vibration, temperature, oil analysis, acoustic emission, ultrasonics.
- **Advantages:** Maximizes component life, minimizes unnecessary maintenance, optimal spares utilization.
- **Disadvantages:** High initial investment in sensors and monitoring systems, requires trained analysts.

#### D. **Corrective Maintenance**
- Maintenance performed **after a failure has occurred**, but with the intent of *restoring the system to its original design specifications* (not merely patching it).
- Includes repair, rework, and replacement of failed components.
- Often used synonymously with breakdown maintenance, but corrective maintenance has a *post-failure improvement* aspect.

#### E. **Planned Maintenance**
- A **superset** that combines preventive, predictive, and corrective actions under a coordinated schedule and budget plan.
- Includes pre-arranged labor, spares, tools, work permits, and shutdown windows.
- Considered the *professional standard* for modern industries.

#### F. **Opportunity Maintenance**
- Maintenance action is performed **opportunistically** when the equipment is already down for some other reason.
- Example: While the plant is shut down for a major process reason, the maintenance team uses that window to service non-critical equipment.

#### G. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**
- A **holistic Japanese-origin philosophy** (from Seiichi Nakajima, 1971) where **every employee — from operator to top management** — participates in maintenance.
- Aims for *zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents* (the "3 Zeros").
- Pillars: Autonomous Maintenance, Planned Maintenance, Quality Maintenance, Focused Improvement, Early Equipment Management, Training, Safety, Administration.

#### H. **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)**
- A systematic engineering framework (developed in the aviation industry by Nowlan & Heap, 1978) that determines the **most cost-effective maintenance strategy** for each physical asset based on its:
  - **Function** — what the asset must do
  - **Functional Failure** — how it can fail
  - **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
  - **Consequence of failure** — safety, environmental, operational, economic
- RCM selects from seven standard maintenance tasks (e.g., scheduled restoration, scheduled discard, failure-finding, on-condition task, etc.)

---

### 2.2 Maintenance Policies (Strategic Decision Framework)

A **policy** dictates the *decision rule* for triggering maintenance. The three primary policies are:

| **Policy** | **Trigger for Maintenance** | **Decision Rule** |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactive (Run-to-Failure)** | Failure occurs | Act **only after** breakdown |
| **Scheduled (Time-Based)** | Time or usage threshold | Act when **T ≥ T<sub>pm</sub>** |
| **Predictive (Condition-Based)** | Condition indicator crosses threshold | Act when **CI ≤ CI<sub>limit</sub>** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Worth Memorizing):** *"A maintenance policy is a set of rules which dictate the preventive replacement or repair of a system or its components. It defines the moments at which maintenance actions are to be performed, depending on the observable state of the system and the history of past interventions."*

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Expression** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability (steady-state) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{f}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_{f}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability (exponential distribution) | dimensionless |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate (constant) | failures/hour |
| $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | dimensionless |
| $C_{T} = C_{preventive} + C_{corrective} + C_{downtime}$ | Total maintenance cost | currency |
| $T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{f}}{C_{p} \cdot \lambda^{2}}}$ | Optimal PM interval (block replacement) | hours |
| $K(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln[R(t)]$ | Conditional failure rate / hazard function | failures/hour |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU board exam, students often confuse the variables inside equations like $\vert \lambda \vert$ or $\vert R(t) \vert$. In the formula sheet above, all vertical bars are written using LaTeX escaping to avoid markdown table corruption. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ syntax in your answer script.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Methods

- **Aviation Industry** uses **RCM** almost exclusively (FAA mandates RCM-based maintenance).
- **Power Plants** rely on **Preventive + Predictive** hybrid (scheduled outages + online condition monitoring).
- **Manufacturing (Toyota, Ford)** uses **TPM** to drive worker-level ownership.
- **Oil & Gas** combines **Predictive (vibration, thermography)** with **Opportunity Maintenance** during planned shutdowns.
- **Data Centers** use **Predictive + Redundancy** policies because downtime cost is enormous.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Availability (A)

The **availability** of a repairable system at steady state is the long-run fraction of time the system is operational. We derive it from the basic definitions.

**Step 1: Define the system cycle.**
A repairable system alternates between two states — **UP** (operating) and **DOWN** (under repair). One complete cycle consists of an *uptime* and a *downtime* segment.

$$T_{cycle} = T_{up} + T_{down}$$

where
- $T_{up}$ = total operating time before failure = **MTBF**
- $T_{down}$ = total time the system is being repaired = **MTTR**

**Step 2: Express the fraction of time the system is UP.**
By definition, the *fractional uptime* (or availability) is:

$$A = \dfrac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 3: Express the unavailability (downtime fraction).**
For problems asking for the *expected downtime per year*, we use the complement:

$$U = 1 - A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 4: Numerical example (KTU-style).**
Given: MTBF = 800 hours, MTTR = 8 hours.

$$A = \dfrac{800}{800 + 8} = \dfrac{800}{808} = 0.9901$$

$$U = 1 - 0.9901 = 0.0099$$

If the system runs 24 × 365 = 8760 hours per year:

$$T_{down, year} = 0.0099 \times 8760 = 86.72 \text{ hours/year}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimal Preventive Maintenance Interval (Age Replacement Model)

This derivation answers: *"Given a system with Weibull-distributed failures, when should we replace it preventively to minimize cost per unit time?"*

**Step 1: Define cost components.**
- $C_{p}$ = cost of a **preventive replacement** (planned, cheaper)
- $C_{f}$ = cost of a **failure replacement** (unplanned, expensive, includes downtime penalty)
- $T$ = preventive replacement interval
- $\lambda(t)$ = age-dependent failure rate

**Step 2: Write expected cost per unit time.**
During one renewal cycle of length $T$:

- If the system **fails before** $T$, it is replaced at failure with cost $C_{f}$.
- If it **survives** until $T$, it is replaced preventively with cost $C_{p}$.

For a constant failure rate $\lambda$ (exponential reliability, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$):

$$E[\text{Cost/cycle}] = C_{f}\int_{0}^{T} \lambda \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{dF}{dt} dt + C_{p} \cdot R(T)$$

**Step 3: Simplify (for constant failure rate).**

$$E[\text{Cost/cycle}] = C_{f}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda T}\right) + C_{p} \cdot e^{-\lambda T}$$

**Step 4: Minimize cost per unit time.**
Take the derivative of total cost per unit time and set to zero. After algebraic simplification:

$$T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{f}}{C_{p} \cdot \lambda^{2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **classical Barlow–Hunter (1960) Age Replacement Model**, and it is a high-yield KTU question. Expect a 7-mark problem asking you to derive or apply this formula.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Methods vs. Policies

| **Parameter** | **Breakdown** | **Preventive** | **Predictive (CBM)** | **Corrective** | **RCM** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Failure occurrence | Time / usage | Condition threshold | Failure | Risk-based |
| Downtime | Unplanned, high | Planned, moderate | Minimal, planned | Unplanned, high | Variable |
| Cost (initial) | Low | Moderate | High | Low | High (engineering effort) |
| Cost (long-term) | Highest (damage) | Moderate | Lowest | High | Lowest |
| Skill required | Repair-level | Technician | Analyst + Technician | Repair-level | Systems Engineer |
| Spare inventory | Reactive (large buffer) | Pre-calculated | Optimized (just-in-time) | Reactive | Optimized |
| Equipment life utilization | Poor (run to death) | Under-utilized | Optimal | Poor | Optimal |
| Best suited for | Non-critical assets | Critical, well-known assets | Critical, instrumented assets | All post-failure | High-consequence, regulated assets |
| KTU weightage | High | High | Very High | Moderate | High |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Question:** A pump has MTBF = 2000 hours and MTTR = 10 hours. If the pump runs continuously, calculate:
(a) The steady-state availability.
(b) The expected downtime per year.
(c) The expected number of failures per year.

**Solution:**

(a)

$$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{2000}{2000 + 10} = \dfrac{2000}{2010} = 0.9950$$

(b) Operating hours per year = 8760.

$$T_{down, year} = (1 - 0.9950) \times 8760 = 0.0050 \times 8760 = 43.8 \text{ hours/year}$$

(c)

$$N_{failures/year} = \dfrac{8760}{MTBF} = \dfrac{8760}{2000} = 4.38 \text{ failures/year}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Maintenance Methods

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision-flow logic** for choosing a maintenance method based on equipment criticality and failure data. This is a safe, KTU-board-friendly alternative to a physical diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Asset Identified] --> B{Is the Asset Critical?}
    B -- No --> C[Breakdown Maintenance]
    B -- Yes --> D{Failure Data Available?}
    D -- No --> E[Time-Based Preventive Maintenance]
    D -- Yes --> F{Sensors / Monitoring Feasible?}
    F -- No --> G[Scheduled Preventive + Inspection]
    F -- Yes --> H[Condition-Based / Predictive Maintenance]
    H --> H1[Vibration Analysis]
    H --> H2[Thermography]
    H --> H3[Oil / Wear Debris Analysis]
    H --> H4[Ultrasonic / Acoustic Emission]
    C --> I[Corrective Repair After Failure]
    E --> J[PM Schedule: T pm interval]
    G --> K[PM Schedule with FMEA]
    I --> L[Restore to Design Spec]
    J --> M[Time-Based Replacement]
    K --> N[Risk-Based Decision]
    L --> O{Record Failure Data}
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Update Maintenance Policy Database]
    P --> Q[Continuous Improvement Loop]
```

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Maintenance Policies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MP[Maintenance Policy Framework] --> R[Reactive Policy]
    MP --> S[Scheduled Policy]
    MP --> P[Predictive Policy]
    MP --> RC[RCM Policy]
    MP --> TPM[TPM Policy]

    R --> R1[Run to Failure]
    R --> R2[Operate until breakdown]

    S --> S1[Calendar-Based PM]
    S --> S2[Usage-Based PM]
    S --> S3[Block Replacement]

    P --> P1[On-Condition Task]
    P --> P2[Continuous Monitoring]
    P --> P3[Periodic Diagnostic]

    RC --> RC1[Seven RCM Task Types]
    RC1 --> RC1A[Scheduled Restoration]
    RC1 --> RC1B[Scheduled Discard]
    RC1 --> RC1C[Failure Finding]
    RC1 --> RC1D[On-Condition Task]
    RC1 --> RC1E[Redesign / One-Time Change]
    RC1 --> RC1F[No Maintenance Operate to Failure]

    TPM --> TPM1[Autonomous Maintenance]
    TPM --> TPM2[Planned Maintenance]
    TPM --> TPM3[Focused Improvement]
    TPM --> TPM4[Training and Education]
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing a Maintenance Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the failure consequence?] --> Q2A[Minor: No safety risk]
    Q1 --> Q2B[Major: Safety/Environmental risk]
    Q1 --> Q2C[Severe: Production Loss]

    Q2A --> P1[Use Breakdown Maintenance]
    Q2B --> P2[Use Predictive CBM with RCM logic]
    Q2C --> P3{Is downtime cost high?}

    P3 -- Yes --> P3A[Predictive CBM]
    P3 -- No --> P3B[Scheduled Preventive]

    P1 --> END1[Low cost, simple]
    P2 --> END2[High reliability, regulated]
    P3A --> END3[Optimal cost-availability]
    P3B --> END4[Predictable but higher PM cost]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
**"Differentiate between Breakdown Maintenance and Preventive Maintenance. State two situations where each is most suitable."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis** | **Breakdown Maintenance** | **Preventive Maintenance** |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Performed **after failure** | Performed **at fixed intervals**, before failure |
| Planning | Unplanned | Pre-planned with schedule |
| Cost pattern | Low initial, high long-term (downtime + damage) | Moderate recurring, low long-term |
| Suitable for | Non-critical, low-cost, redundant assets (e.g., lighting, hand tools) | Critical, expensive, high-utilization machinery (e.g., turbines, pumps) |

*(Award 2 marks for the differentiation table and 1 mark for suitable situations.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**"What is Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)? List any four condition monitoring techniques used in CBM."**

**Model Answer:**
CBM is a maintenance strategy where maintenance actions are performed **only when monitored parameters indicate a drop in the operating condition** of the equipment below a pre-set threshold.

Four condition monitoring techniques:
1. **Vibration Analysis** – for rotating machinery (bearings, gears).
2. **Thermography (Infrared Imaging)** – for electrical panels, bearings, insulation.
3. **Oil / Wear Debris Analysis (LNF, Spectrographic)** – for engines, gearboxes, hydraulics.
4. **Ultrasonic / Acoustic Emission Testing** – for pressure vessels, valves, leak detection.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for technique list, 1 mark for relevance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often **forget to state assumptions** (e.g., constant failure rate) when applying formulas like the Barlow-Hunter model. This is a 1-mark deduction every time.
> 2. Avoid mixing the *method* and the *policy* in a single answer. Always clarify the distinction.
> 3. In numerical problems, **always carry units** in the final answer. A correct number without units loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. When defining TPM or RCM, do **not paraphrase loosely**. Use the standard terminologies (Autonomous Maintenance, OEE, FMEA, RCM Decision Tree, etc.).

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain in detail the various types of preventive maintenance strategies. Compare their advantages and disadvantages with a suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

Preventive Maintenance (PM) refers to **planned, periodic maintenance** performed to retain an asset in operating condition and prevent unscheduled downtime. The major types are:

1. **Scheduled Overhaul Maintenance**
   - The equipment is **completely dismantled, inspected, and reassembled** at fixed intervals (e.g., every 4000 hours or annually).
   - Example: Aircraft engine overhaul every 5000 flight hours.
   - Advantage: Comprehensive coverage, very low probability of in-service failure.
   - Disadvantage: Very high labor cost, long downtime, possible over-maintenance.

2. **Periodic Inspection Maintenance**
   - Routine **visual, functional, and instrumental inspections** at regular intervals (daily, weekly, monthly).
   - Example: Daily walk-through inspection of a CNC machine; monthly vibration check on a motor.
   - Advantage: Early detection, low cost, no production interruption.
   - Disadvantage: Cannot detect internal wear without dismantling.

3. **Servicing / Routine Maintenance**
   - Includes **lubrication, cleaning, adjustment, calibration, and minor part replacement** on a fixed schedule.
   - Example: Greasing bearings every 200 hours, oil change every 500 hours.
   - Advantage: Extends equipment life, very economical.
   - Disadvantage: Possible over-lubrication or under-lubrication if not data-driven.

4. **Scheduled Replacement (Block Replacement)**
   - Specific components are **replaced wholesale at a pre-determined age**, regardless of their actual condition.
   - Example: Replacing all fluorescent tubes in a plant every 12 months.
   - Advantage: Administrative simplicity, predictable spares demand.
   - Disadvantage: Discards components that may still have useful life.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| **Type** | **Frequency** | **Cost** | **Best For** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overhaul | Very low (years) | Highest | Critical, complex machinery |
| Inspection | Daily–monthly | Lowest | All equipment |
| Servicing | High (hours) | Low | Lubricated parts, sensors |
| Block replacement | Medium | Moderate | Consumables, low-cost parts |

*(Award 7 marks: 1 mark for definition, 4 marks for explanation of four types, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for the comparison table.)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *A machine has a constant failure rate of 0.001 failures/hour. The cost of preventive replacement is ₹5000 and the cost of failure replacement is ₹25,000 (including downtime). Using the Age Replacement Model, determine the optimal preventive replacement interval.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given data:**
- Failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour
- Preventive replacement cost $C_{p} = ₹5000$
- Failure replacement cost $C_{f} = ₹25,000$

**Step 1: State the Age Replacement Model formula (Barlow-Hunter).**

$$T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{f}}{C_{p} \cdot \lambda^{2}}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 25{,}000}{5000 \times (0.001)^{2}}}$$

$$T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{50{,}000}{5000 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{50{,}000}{0.005}}$$

$$T_{opt} = \sqrt{10^{7}}$$

$$T_{opt} = 3162.28 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**
The machine should be preventively replaced approximately every **3162 operating hours** (≈ 4.4 months of continuous operation) to minimize the long-run cost per unit time.

**Verification (optional):**
Expected number of PMs per 10,000 hours = 10000 / 3162 ≈ 3.16 PMs, cost = 3.16 × 5000 = ₹15,811
Expected number of failures per 10,000 hours = 0.001 × 10000 = 10 failures (in the absence of PM, but in PM regime fewer will occur), cost savings are visible.

*(Award 7 marks: 1 mark for stating formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for units check, 1 mark for numerical evaluation, 1 mark for final answer with units, 1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for verification/assumptions.)*

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Describe the seven standard maintenance task types used in Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) as per the Nowlan & Heap framework. Use a suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

RCM, developed by **Stanley Nowlan and Howard Heap (United Airlines, 1978)**, prescribes a systematic seven-task decision logic. They are:

1. **Scheduled Restoration Task**
   - The component is **restored to a like-new condition** at fixed intervals.
   - Example: Overhauling a gas turbine combustor every 10,000 hours.

2. **Scheduled Discard Task**
   - The component is **discarded and replaced** at a fixed age, regardless of its visible condition.
   - Example: Replacing aircraft emergency oxygen generators at expiry date.

3. **On-Condition Task (Predictive)**
   - The component is **monitored continuously or periodically**, and replaced only when its condition indicator crosses a threshold.
   - Example: Vibration monitoring of a pump bearing; replacement when RMS velocity > 7.1 mm/s.

4. **Failure-Finding Task**
   - Used for **hidden/protective functions** (e.g., emergency shutdown valves, fire alarms) that only operate on demand.
   - Example: Quarterly test of an emergency diesel generator.

5. **Combination of Above Tasks**
   - Multiple strategies applied in parallel for different failure modes of the same component.

6. **Operate-to-Failure (No Scheduled Task)**
   - For **non-critical, low-consequence, economically justified** components.
   - Example: Indicator lamps, nameplates.

7. **Redesign / One-Time Change**
   - When no scheduled task can effectively manage a failure, the component is **redesigned or replaced with a better one**.
   - Example: Replacing a brittle brass fitting with a stainless-steel forged part after repeated leaks.

**RCM Decision Flow:**
The classic RCM question tree asks, in order:
- Is the loss of function evident to the operating crew?
- Does loss of function cause safety/environmental hazard?
- Does loss of function cause major economic loss?

Depending on answers, the appropriate task from the seven is selected.

*(Award 7 marks: 1 mark for context, 5 marks for the seven tasks with examples, 1 mark for the decision-flow summary.)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *For a CNC machining center, the following data are recorded over one year of continuous operation (8760 hours): total number of failures = 12, total downtime = 144 hours. Calculate (i) MTBF, (ii) MTTR, (iii) Steady-state availability, (iv) Expected number of failures per 1000 operating hours.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_{total} = 8760$ hours
- $N_{f} = 12$ failures
- $T_{down} = 144$ hours

**(i) MTBF:**

$$MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{f}} = \dfrac{8760}{12} = 730 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) MTTR:**

$$MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_{f}} = \dfrac{144}{12} = 12 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Steady-state Availability:**

$$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{730}{730 + 12} = \dfrac{730}{742} = 0.9838$$

$$A \approx 98.38\%$$

**(iv) Expected failures per 1000 hours:**

$$N_{f, 1000h} = \dfrac{1000}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1000}{730} = 1.369 \text{ failures per 1000 hours}$$

Or alternatively, from one-year data:

$$N_{f, 1000h} = \dfrac{12 \text{ failures}}{8760 \text{ hours}} \times 1000 = 1.369 \text{ failures/1000h}$$

*(Award 7 marks: 1 mark for each of the four sub-parts, 1 mark for correct units, 1 mark for cross-check consistency.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maintenance Method** = the *tactical action* (e.g., oil change, vibration check). **Maintenance Policy** = the *strategic rule* (e.g., "change oil every 500 hours").
- The **three primary policies** are: **Reactive**, **Scheduled (Time-Based)**, and **Predictive (Condition-Based)**. Every other policy is a hybrid or refinement of these.
- **MTBF** measures reliability; **MTTR** measures maintainability. **Availability** combines both.
- **Barlow–Hunter Age Replacement Model** is the standard optimization tool for preventive intervals: $T_{opt} = \sqrt{2 C_{f} / (C_{p} \lambda^{2})}$. Always state the assumption of *constant failure rate*.
- **TPM** emphasizes *operator-driven* maintenance; **RCM** emphasizes *risk-driven* decision logic.
- The **seven RCM task types** are a board favorite — memorize the categories, not just the names.
- For numerical problems, **always include units** (hours, ₹, failures/year) in the final answer.
- A **higher availability** is achieved by either increasing MTBF (better design / better PM) or decreasing MTTR (faster repair / better spares).
- **Opportunity Maintenance** is a "free" maintenance window — use it strategically to reduce future planned shutdowns.
- **FMEA** (Failure Modes and Effects Analysis) is the foundational data input for RCM decisions.
- The **OEE** metric is what production managers track; **Availability** is what maintenance engineers optimize.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Which maintenance policy is best?"*, **never** answer with a single policy. Always state: *"The optimal choice depends on (a) consequence of failure, (b) cost of monitoring, (c) failure data availability, and (d) equipment criticality. In practice, a hybrid of preventive and predictive (CBM) is most common in modern industries."* This is the textbook answer that scores full marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condition Based Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Condition Based Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)** is a proactive maintenance philosophy in which maintenance interventions are scheduled and executed *only when* predefined indicators show signs of degradation or impending failure in an asset. Unlike fixed-interval preventive maintenance, CBM relies on the actual *real-time health* of equipment — measured through periodic or continuous monitoring of parameters such as vibration, temperature, lubricant condition, acoustic emission, electrical signature, and ultrasonic signatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"Condition Based Maintenance is a maintenance strategy that initiates maintenance actions based on the actual condition of the equipment, obtained from continuous or periodic monitoring, rather than on a fixed schedule or runtime."*

The governing principle of CBM can be formally expressed as:

$$
A_{m} = f(P_{m},\, T_{h},\, H(t),\, \lambda(t))
$$

where:
- $A_{m}$ = Maintenance Action Trigger
- $P_{m}$ = Monitored Performance Parameters (vibration, temperature, etc.)
- $T_{h}$ = Predefined Health Threshold
- $H(t)$ = Current Asset Health Index
- $\lambda(t)$ = Time-varying Failure Rate Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CBM vs. PdM vs. PM — Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **PM (Preventive Maintenance):** Time-based or usage-based (e.g., change oil every 5000 hrs).
> - **CBM (Condition Based Maintenance):** Based on observed condition indicators.
> - **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Uses forecasting models on CBM data to predict remaining useful life (RUL).
> - In the KTU syllabus, **CBM and PdM are often used interchangeably**, but academically CBM is the *trigger*, while PdM is the *prediction engine*.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Human Body Annual Check-up" Model

Imagine you own a car. The **traditional (preventive) approach** says, *"Change your brake pads every 20,000 km — no matter what."* This is safe but inefficient — you may replace good pads, or worse, miss a worn pad at 19,999 km.

Now consider the **CBM approach**: you install a sensor on the brake pad. Every time you brake, the sensor measures pad thickness. The car's computer beeps: *"Brake pad is at 3 mm — replace within 500 km."*

**This is exactly how CBM works in industry.**

- The **sensor** = Vibration / Temperature / Oil debris monitor
- The **beeping computer** = Data acquisition + threshold logic
- The **"replace within 500 km"** = Maintenance trigger based on real condition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** CBM is like listening to your machinery's heartbeat. When the heartbeat changes (unusual vibration, rising temperature, dirty oil), you send the doctor (maintenance crew) — not on a calendar date, but because the patient *told you something is wrong*.

---

### 1.3 Core Pillars of CBM

CBM rests on **four foundational pillars** that KTU examiners frequently test:

| Pillar | Description | Typical Metric |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **1. Sensing** | Acquiring raw physical signals from the asset | Sensor type, sampling rate |
| **2. Diagnostics** | Interpreting signals to detect faults | Vibration RMS, crest factor |
| **3. Prognostics** | Forecasting remaining useful life | RUL, P-F curve interval |
| **4. Decision & Action** | Triggering optimal maintenance action | Work order generation |

---

### 1.4 The P-F Curve (Potential Failure Curve) — The Heart of CBM

The **P-F Curve** is the *single most important concept* in CBM for KTU exams. It models the degradation of an asset from a healthy "Potential Failure (PF) point" to a functional "Failure (F) point."

```
Asset Health
   ▲
   │
   │  ●──────● (P) Potential Failure Detectable
   │   \      \
   │    \      \
   │     \      \
   │      \      \
   │       \      \
   │        \      \
   │         \      \
   │          \      ● (F) Functional Failure
   └──────────────────────────► Time
              P    F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **P-F Interval:** The time window between when a failure *can* be detected (P) and when it *actually* causes functional breakdown (F). **The goal of CBM is to detect failures well within this P-F interval** so maintenance can be planned.

**Industry Standard Rule:** The inspection interval should be ≤ **0.5 × P-F interval** to ensure detection with safety margin.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Operational Logic of CBM

CBM follows a **closed-loop, five-stage logical cycle**. Each stage has a specific engineering function and produces a defined output:

1. **Stage 1 — Data Acquisition:** Sensors (accelerometers, thermocouples, pressure transducers) capture raw physical signals from the asset. The sampling rate must obey the **Nyquist criterion**: $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$.

2. **Stage 2 — Signal Preprocessing:** Raw signals are filtered (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) and amplified. Noise is removed using techniques like FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) and time-synchronous averaging.

3. **Stage 3 — Feature Extraction:** Diagnostic features are computed. Examples include:
   - **Vibration:** RMS, Peak, Crest Factor (CF), Kurtosis
   - **Temperature:** Rate of change (dT/dt), Absolute value
   - **Oil:** Particle count, viscosity, Ferrography index
   - **Acoustic Emission:** RMS, Energy, Ring-down count

4. **Stage 4 — Condition Assessment:** Extracted features are compared against baseline values and alarm thresholds. Modern systems use AI/ML models (neural networks, fuzzy logic) to classify the fault.

5. **Stage 5 — Maintenance Decision & Action:** Based on the assessment, the system triggers one of four actions:
   - **No Action** (asset is healthy)
   - **Monitor Closely** (warning zone)
   - **Schedule Maintenance** (alarm zone)
   - **Immediate Shutdown** (critical zone)

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet for Condition Based Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU UNIVERSITY EXAM — MEMORIZE THESE:**

| Formula No. | Equation | Description | Units / Boundary |
|:-----------:|:--------|:------------|:-----------------|
| **F1** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System Availability (0 ≤ A ≤ 1) | Dimensionless ratio |
| **F2** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure Rate (constant failure region) | Failures per hour |
| **F3** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential) | 0 ≤ R(t) ≤ 1 |
| **F4** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum t_{r,i}}{n}$ | Mean Time To Repair (arithmetic mean) | Hours |
| **F5** | $CF = \dfrac{x_{peak}}{x_{rms}}$ | Crest Factor (bearing fault indicator) | Typically 3–6 for healthy bearings |
| **F6** | $k = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^4}{\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2\right)^2}$ | Kurtosis (4th moment statistic) | Healthy ≈ 3; Bearing fault > 3 |
| **F7** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | 0 ≤ OEE ≤ 1 (World class ≥ 0.85) |
| **F8** | $T_{insp} \leq 0.5 \times T_{PF}$ | Inspection Interval (P-F rule) | Hours or cycles |
| **F9** | $C_{CBM} = C_{insp} + C_{M(\text{planned})}$ | Total CBM Cost | Currency units |
| **F10** | $RUL = T_{F} - t_{current}$ | Remaining Useful Life | Hours or cycles |

> **Legend (with no pipe symbols used in math):**
> - **A** = Availability
> - **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
> - **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (hours)
> - **λ** = Failure rate
> - **R(t)** = Reliability at time t
> - **CF** = Crest Factor
> - **k** = Kurtosis
> - **OEE** = Overall Equipment Effectiveness
> - **P** = Performance efficiency, **Q** = Quality rate
> - **C_insp** = Cost of inspection/monitoring
> - **T_PF** = P-F Interval duration

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of CBM

CBM is the *backbone* of Industry 4.0 and Smart Manufacturing. Its engineering applications include:

- **Rotating Machinery:** Monitoring of motors, pumps, gearboxes, turbines, and compressors in power plants, oil & gas refineries, and aerospace engines.
- **Civil Structures:** Structural health monitoring (SHM) of bridges, dams, and high-rise buildings using embedded sensors.
- **Manufacturing Lines:** Detecting tool wear in CNC machines to prevent defective products.
- **Fleet Management:** Real-time monitoring of aircraft engines (Airbus, Boeing), railway bogies, and naval vessels.
- **Energy Sector:** Wind turbine gearbox monitoring to avoid costly downtime in offshore installations.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In 14-mark questions, students often lose marks by failing to mention the *economic* benefit of CBM (reduced downtime cost, lower spare inventory, extended asset life). Always quantify with **MTBF improvement** and **OEE uplift**.

---

### 2.4 Types of Maintenance Strategies — Comparative Hierarchy

| Strategy | Trigger Basis | Data Required | Cost (Long Term) | Downtime Risk |
|:---------|:--------------|:--------------|:------------------|:--------------|
| **Reactive (Run-to-Failure)** | After failure | None | Highest (catastrophic) | Highest |
| **Scheduled Preventive** | Calendar / runtime | Maintenance log | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Predictive (CBM)** | Real condition sensors | Continuous | Medium (lowest ROI loss) | Lowest |
| **Prescriptive** | CBM + AI optimization | Continuous + historical | Lowest (with AI) | Negligible |
| **Opportunistic** | Adjacent downtime | Inspection logs | Variable | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> The strategic evolution in industry follows: **Reactive → Preventive → Predictive (CBM) → Prescriptive (AI-driven CBM)**. KTU questions often ask students to *justify* the migration from preventive to CBM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the System Availability Function (F1)

**Starting Premise:** Availability is the fraction of time an asset is in an *operational (up)* state versus the total time required (up + down).

**Step 1 — Define the two states:**

Let $T_{up}$ = total time asset is operational, $T_{down}$ = total time asset is non-operational (under repair).

$$
T_{total} = T_{up} + T_{down}
$$

**Step 2 — Express as statistical averages over N cycles:**

For $N$ failure-repair cycles:

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{T_{up}}{N}, \quad MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Availability Definition:**

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}} = \dfrac{N \cdot MTBF}{N \cdot MTBF + N \cdot MTTR}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel N from numerator and denominator:**

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical Interpretation:**

- If $MTBF = 900$ hrs and $MTTR = 100$ hrs:

$$
A = \dfrac{900}{900 + 100} = \dfrac{900}{1000} = 0.90 \; (90\%)
$$

This means the asset is operational **90% of the time** — a critical KPI in KTU's Maintenance Engineering module.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reliability Function (F3)

**Step 1 — Define Failure Rate λ:**

The failure rate is the conditional probability of failure per unit time, given survival up to time $t$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

where $f(t)$ = probability density of failure, $R(t)$ = reliability.

**Step 2 — Assume Constant Failure Rate (Useful Life Period):**

In the *useful life* region of the bathtub curve, $\lambda$ is constant. Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda R(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables:**

$$
\dfrac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides:**

$$
\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \dfrac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**

Since $R(0) = 1$ (asset is certainly working at start):

$$
\ln(R(t)) - \ln(1) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate:**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 7 — Practical Example:**

A pump has $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour. Reliability at $t = 1000$ hrs:

$$
R(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187
$$

So there is an **81.87% probability** the pump survives 1000 hours without failure.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fault Diagnosis Using CBM Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU Practical / Viva Questions — Fault-to-Parameter Mapping:**

| Machine Component | Primary CBM Technique | Diagnostic Feature | Threshold (Typical) | Symptom on Deviation |
|:------------------|:----------------------|:-------------------|:--------------------|:---------------------|
| **Rolling Bearing** | Vibration analysis | High-frequency enveloping (g, ENV) | ENV > 4 g RMS | Inner race spall, outer race spall, ball defect |
| **Rolling Bearing** | Vibration analysis | Kurtosis (k) | k > 3.5 | Lubrication failure, early fatigue |
| **Gearbox** | Vibration analysis | Sideband amplitude near mesh frequency | Sidebands > 0.5 × gear mesh amplitude | Tooth crack, misalignment, wear |
| **Shaft** | Vibration analysis | 1× RPM amplitude (unbalance) | > 6 mm/s RMS | Imbalance, bent shaft |
| **Shaft** | Vibration analysis | 2× RPM amplitude | > 50% of 1× amplitude | Misalignment |
| **Electric Motor** | Current signature analysis | Rotor bar sidebands | > 45 dB below fundamental | Broken rotor bar |
| **Hydraulic System** | Oil analysis | Particle count (ISO 4406) | > 18/16/13 | Wear debris in fluid |
| **Steam Turbine** | Thermography | Bearing housing temperature | > 80°C (warning), > 90°C (alarm) | Lubrication failure |
| **Pressure Vessel** | Acoustic emission | RMS voltage | > 50 dB above baseline | Crack propagation |
| **Pipeline** | Ultrasonic testing | Wall thickness reading | Below design thickness | Corrosion, erosion |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Optimizing Inspection Interval

**Problem Statement:**
A motor bearing has a P-F interval of $T_{PF} = 60$ days. Determine the maximum permissible inspection interval.

**Solution Using F8:**

$$
T_{insp} \leq 0.5 \times T_{PF}
$$

$$
T_{insp} \leq 0.5 \times 60 \text{ days} = 30 \text{ days}
$$

**Engineering Judgment:** We must inspect **at least once every 30 days** to ensure the failure is caught in time with a 50% safety margin. In high-criticality applications, KTU problems may further reduce this to $0.33 \times T_{PF}$.

---

### 3.5 Cost-Benefit Derivation of CBM vs. Preventive Maintenance

**Total Cost of Maintenance (TCM):**

$$
TCM = C_{down} \cdot T_{down} + C_{M} \cdot N_{M} + C_{insp} \cdot N_{insp}
$$

where:
- $C_{down}$ = Downtime cost per hour
- $T_{down}$ = Total downtime hours per year
- $C_{M}$ = Cost per maintenance action
- $N_{M}$ = Number of maintenance actions per year
- $C_{insp}$ = Cost per inspection
- $N_{insp}$ = Number of inspections per year

**For CBM to be economically justified:**

$$
TCM_{CBM} < TCM_{PM}
$$

$$
\left(C_{down} \cdot T_{down}^{CBM} + C_M \cdot N_M^{CBM} + C_{insp} \cdot N_{insp}\right) < \left(C_{down} \cdot T_{down}^{PM} + C_M \cdot N_M^{PM}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> CBM typically **increases** $C_{insp}$ (more inspections) but **drastically decreases** $T_{down}$ and $N_M$ (fewer failures and fewer unnecessary replacements).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CBM Closed-Loop Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete **closed-loop architecture of a CBM system**, from sensor acquisition to maintenance action and feedback:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ASSET UNDER MONITORING] --> A2[SENSOR LAYER: Accelerometer, Thermocouple, Pressure Transducer]
    A2 --> A3[SIGNAL CONDITIONING: Amplification, Filtering, Anti-Aliasing]
    A3 --> A4[DATA ACQUISITION: ADC, Sampling at Nyquist Rate]
    A4 --> A5[FEATURE EXTRACTION: RMS, Peak, Kurtosis, FFT, Envelope]
    A5 --> A6[DIAGNOSTIC ENGINE: Compare to Baseline and Threshold]
    A6 --> A7{FAULT CLASSIFIED?}
    A7 -- NO FAULT --> A8[HEALTHY: Continue Monitoring]
    A7 -- WARNING --> A9[ALERT: Schedule Maintenance Window]
    A7 -- CRITICAL --> A10[EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN: Immediate Action]
    A8 --> A2
    A9 --> B1[MAINTENANCE PLANNING MODULE]
    A10 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[WORK ORDER GENERATION]
    B2 --> B3[MAINTENANCE EXECUTION]
    B3 --> B4[POST-MAINTENANCE VERIFICATION]
    B4 --> B5[UPDATE BASELINE AND ML MODEL]
    B5 --> A5
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Selecting Maintenance Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[NEW ASSET] --> S2{CRITICALITY OF ASSET?}
    S2 -- LOW --> S3[REACTIVE: Run-to-Failure]
    S2 -- MEDIUM --> S4[PREVENTIVE: Scheduled PM]
    S2 -- HIGH --> S5{FAULT PREDICTABLE?}
    S5 -- NO --> S4
    S5 -- YES --> S6{CBM INFRASTRUCTURE AVAILABLE?}
    S6 -- NO --> S4
    S6 -- YES --> S7[CONDITION BASED MAINTENANCE: CBM]
    S7 --> S8{MACHINE LEARNING MODELS DEPLOYED?}
    S8 -- NO --> S9[STANDARD CBM: Threshold Based]
    S8 -- YES --> S10[PRESCRIPTIVE MAINTENANCE: AI Driven CBM]
```

---

### 4.3 P-F Curve with CBM Detection Bands

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[HEALTHY STATE] --> P2[P POINT: Potential Failure Begins - Subtle Signal]
    P2 --> P3[WARNING ZONE: Trend Monitoring Begins]
    P3 --> P4[ALARM ZONE: Maintenance Scheduled]
    P4 --> P5[F POINT: Functional Failure - Asset Down]
    P5 --> P6[EMERGENCY REPAIR]
    P6 --> P1
    style P3 fill:#fff4cc
    style P4 fill:#ffd6cc
    style P5 fill:#ff8080
    style P1 fill:#ccffcc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Green = safe operation. Yellow = warning band where trend monitoring triggers. Orange = alarm band where maintenance is scheduled. Red = functional failure (downtime). The **P-F interval** is the horizontal distance between node `P2` and node `P5`.

---

### 4.4 Maintenance Strategy Evolution Map (Industry 4.0)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[GENERATION 1: REACTIVE MAINTENANCE] --> M2[GENERATION 2: PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE]
    M2 --> M3[GENERATION 3: CONDITION BASED MAINTENANCE]
    M3 --> M4[GENERATION 4: PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE]
    M4 --> M5[GENERATION 5: PRESCRIPTIVE MAINTENANCE AI DRIVEN]
    style M1 fill:#ffe0e0
    style M2 fill:#fff4cc
    style M3 fill:#e0f0ff
    style M4 fill:#d0e8ff
    style M5 fill:#c0d8ff
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Discuss the evolution of maintenance strategies,"* draw a horizontal timeline like above and explain each generation with a one-line defining feature. Always end with the Industry 4.0 connection.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> Define Condition Based Maintenance. List any **four** techniques used for condition monitoring.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Condition Based Maintenance is a maintenance strategy where maintenance actions are initiated based on the actual measured condition of the equipment, rather than on predetermined schedules or runtime hours. Maintenance is performed only when monitored parameters indicate that the asset's condition has degraded to a predefined threshold.

**Four CBM Techniques (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Vibration Analysis** — Most widely used for rotating machinery; detects imbalance, misalignment, bearing faults, and gear defects.
2. **Oil / Lubricant Analysis** — Spectrometric and ferrographic analysis of wear debris in lubricating oil.
3. **Thermography (Infrared Imaging)** — Detects overheating in electrical equipment, bearings, and insulation.
4. **Acoustic Emission Monitoring** — Detects high-frequency stress waves from crack propagation in pressure vessels and pipelines.
5. *(Optional)* Ultrasonic Testing, Motor Current Signature Analysis, Performance trending.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> Explain the **P-F Curve** and state the rule used to determine the inspection interval.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**P-F Curve Explanation (2 Marks):** The P-F (Potential Failure to Functional Failure) curve is a graphical representation of an asset's degradation from the point where a failure mode becomes detectable (P-point) to the point where the asset can no longer perform its intended function (F-point). The time elapsed between these two points is called the **P-F interval**. The curve typically shows accelerating degradation — slow initially, then rapidly worsening as failure approaches.

**Inspection Interval Rule (1 Mark):**

$$
T_{insp} \leq 0.5 \times T_{PF}
$$

This ensures that any developing fault is detected with a **50% safety margin** before functional failure occurs, allowing time for maintenance planning.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **steps involved in implementing a Condition Based Maintenance program** in an industrial plant. Mention the role of the P-F curve in the planning of inspection intervals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a CBM system**. Discuss how **vibration analysis** is used as a condition monitoring tool for rotating machinery. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Steps for Implementing CBM — 7 Marks:**

1. **Asset Criticality Analysis (1 Mark):** Identify and rank critical assets using methods like Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA). Only critical assets justify the cost of CBM sensors and infrastructure.

2. **Selection of Condition Monitoring Techniques (1 Mark):** Based on failure modes identified, choose appropriate techniques — e.g., vibration for bearings, thermography for electrical panels, oil analysis for gearboxes.

3. **Sensor Installation and Calibration (1 Mark):** Install accelerometers, thermocouples, and pressure transducers at strategic locations. Calibrate against known baselines (ISO standards such as ISO 10816 for vibration).

4. **Establishing Baseline and Alarm Thresholds (1 Mark):** Record healthy-state readings. Define alarm zones (warning, critical) using statistical thresholds (e.g., 2σ and 3σ limits).

5. **Data Acquisition and Trend Analysis (1 Mark):** Set sampling frequency per Nyquist criterion. Plot trends of features like RMS, kurtosis, crest factor over time.

6. **P-F Interval Analysis and Inspection Scheduling (1 Mark):** Determine the P-F interval for each failure mode. Set inspection interval as $T_{insp} \leq 0.5 \times T_{PF}$.

7. **Maintenance Action and Feedback Loop (1 Mark):** Trigger maintenance work orders based on alarm state. Update the baseline after repair and use machine learning to refine thresholds over time.

---

**(b) CBM Architecture and Vibration Analysis — 7 Marks:**

**Functional Architecture (Block Diagram — 3 Marks):**

```
Asset → Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC/DAQ → Feature Extraction
     → Diagnostic Engine → Decision Module → Maintenance Action
     → Feedback to Baseline Database
```

**Vibration Analysis Explanation (4 Marks):**

- **Domain of Measurement:** Vibration signals are analyzed in both **time domain** and **frequency domain**.
- **Time Domain Features:**
  - **RMS Velocity:** Indicates overall vibration severity. Per ISO 10816, machines > 7.1 mm/s RMS are in alarm zone.
  - **Crest Factor:** $CF = x_{peak} / x_{rms}$. Healthy bearings: $CF \approx 3$–$6$. Bearing spall: $CF > 10$.
  - **Kurtosis:** $k > 3.5$ indicates impulsive faults (early bearing fatigue).
- **Frequency Domain Features (FFT):**
  - **1× RPM amplitude** → Imbalance
  - **2× RPM amplitude** → Misalignment
  - **Shaft frequency sidebands around bearing race frequencies (BPFO, BPFI)** → Bearing defects
  - **Gear mesh frequency and sidebands** → Gear tooth faults
- **Advanced Technique — Envelope Analysis (Demodulation):** Used for detecting bearing faults at early stages by demodulating high-frequency resonance excited by bearing impacts.
- **Application:** In a 1000 kW induction motor driving a centrifugal pump, vibration monitoring at the bearing housings detects inner race spalling up to **3 months before functional failure**.

---

#### **QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare **Preventive Maintenance** and **Condition Based Maintenance**. Discuss **four advantages and two limitations** of CBM. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The failure rate of a critical pump is $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour. Calculate:
- (i) The MTBF of the pump.
- (ii) The reliability at $t = 500$ hours and $t = 2000$ hours.
- (iii) The probability of failure in the interval from $t = 500$ to $t = 1000$ hours. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table + Advantages/Limitations — 7 Marks:**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Condition Based Maintenance (CBM) |
|:----------|:----------------------------|:-----------------------------------|
| **Trigger** | Time-based / runtime-based | Real-time condition indicators |
| **Data Source** | Maintenance schedule | Sensor data (vibration, temp, oil) |
| **Cost of Inspection** | Lower | Higher (sensor infrastructure) |
| **Cost of Unexpected Failure** | Higher | Significantly lower |
| **Spare Inventory** | Higher (proactive stocking) | Lower (just-in-time spares) |
| **Asset Life** | Often shortened (over-maintenance) | Extended (intervention only when needed) |
| **Applicability** | Non-critical assets | Critical, high-downtime-cost assets |
| **Skill Required** | Standard mechanical skills | Skilled analysts + data interpretation |

**Four Advantages of CBM (4 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Reduced Unplanned Downtime:** Failures are detected in advance, allowing planned shutdowns.
2. **Lower Life-Cycle Cost:** Although inspection cost is higher, savings from avoided failures outweigh it.
3. **Increased Equipment Reliability and Life:** Maintenance is performed when actually needed — no over- or under-maintenance.
4. **Improved Safety:** Critical faults are identified before catastrophic failure (e.g., turbine blade crack).

**Two Limitations of CBM (2 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **High Initial Investment:** Sensors, data acquisition systems, and trained analysts require significant capital.
2. **Requires Skilled Workforce:** Data interpretation (especially FFT spectra, envelope analysis) needs trained engineers and may be prone to false positives/negatives if not handled correctly.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour

**(i) MTBF Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Reliability at t = 500 hours (2 Marks):**

$$
R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0005 \times 500} = e^{-0.25}
$$

$$
R(500) \approx 0.7788 \; (77.88\%)
$$

**Reliability at t = 2000 hours (1 Mark):**

$$
R(2000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 2000} = e^{-1}
$$

$$
R(2000) \approx 0.3679 \; (36.79\%)
$$

**(iii) Probability of failure in interval [500, 1000] hours (2 Marks):**

The probability of failure in interval $[t_1, t_2]$ is:

$$
P(500 < T < 1000) = R(500) - R(1000)
$$

First compute $R(1000)$:

$$
R(1000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(500 < T < 1000) = 0.7788 - 0.6065 = 0.1723 \; (17.23\%)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse MTBF and MTTR** — MTBF is *between* failures (operational time); MTTR is *to repair* (downtime). Many students swap them in the availability formula and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Always specify the time interval** when computing reliability — $R(t)$ is a function of $t$, not a constant.
> 3. **In the P-F curve question**, students often draw the curve but forget to label the **P point, F point, and P-F interval explicitly**. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unlabeled diagrams.
> 4. **Numerical questions** require units in the final answer. Always write "2000 hours" not just "2000".
> 5. **Do not write "CBM = PdM" blindly.** State the distinction: CBM is the trigger mechanism, PdM is the predictive forecasting on CBM data.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — READ BEFORE EXAM:**

- **CBM** = Maintenance based on real condition, NOT schedule. **(Definition must be memorized verbatim.)**
- **P-F Curve** — plot from P (Potential Failure) to F (Functional Failure). Curve is concave upward (accelerating degradation).
- **Inspection Interval Rule:** $T_{insp} \leq 0.5 \times T_{PF}$
- **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
- **Reliability Formula (Exponential, Useful Life):** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- **Failure Rate Formula:** $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$
- **Crest Factor (CF)** — Healthy bearing: 3–6; Faulty: > 10. **Formula:** $CF = x_{peak} / x_{rms}$
- **Kurtosis (k)** — Healthy ≈ 3; Bearing fault > 3.5. **Formula:** $k = \mu_4 / \sigma^4$
- **Four Core CBM Techniques to remember:**
  1. Vibration Analysis (most common for rotating equipment)
  2. Oil / Lubricant Analysis (wear debris detection)
  3. Thermography (electrical + mechanical)
  4. Acoustic Emission (cracks in pressure vessels)
- **Five Stages of CBM:** Sensing → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Diagnosis → Maintenance Action
- **Cost-Benefit Equation:** CBM is justified when Total Cost (Downtime + Maintenance + Inspection) of CBM < Total Cost of PM.
- **World-Class OEE Benchmark:** $\geq 85\%$. CBM directly improves OEE by reducing unplanned downtime.
- **Industry Evolution:** Reactive → Preventive → CBM → Predictive (PdM) → Prescriptive (AI-driven).
- **Nyquist Criterion** (for sampling): $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **ISO 10816** is the international standard for mechanical vibration severity evaluation — cite it in answers.
- **Reliability at t = MTBF is always 36.79%** = $e^{-1}$ — a common numerical in KTU exams.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark Part B questions on CBM, structure your answer as: (1) Definition, (2) Block Diagram, (3) Key Techniques with Features, (4) P-F Curve with Diagram, (5) Numerical Example, (6) Advantages/Limitations, (7) Industry Application. This 7-part structure covers all KTU CO mapping requirements and ensures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condition Checking and Inspection and Trend Monitoring Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Condition Checking and Inspection and Trend Monitoring Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Condition Checking and Inspection** in the KTU 2024 syllabus framework refers to the systematic, scheduled, and non-disruptive examination of an asset's physical state, performance parameters, and structural integrity using standardized techniques. These activities constitute the **first operational pillar** of any Condition Monitoring (CM) program and are formally classified under the **Scheduled/Preventive Maintenance** and **Predictive Maintenance** sub-domains of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM).

**Trend Monitoring** is the continuous acquisition, recording, and analytical processing of condition data over successive time intervals to identify **degradation patterns**, **deviations from baseline**, and to forecast the **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** of equipment. It represents the **intelligence layer** that transforms raw inspection data into actionable maintenance decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** *Condition Checking + Inspection = Periodic/Snapshot View of Asset Health.* *Trend Monitoring = Continuous Time-Series View of Asset Health.* Both feed into the **CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System)** as separate data streams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **owner of a car**:

- **Condition Checking & Inspection** is what happens when you take your car to a mechanic **once every 6 months**. The mechanic visually checks the brake pads, measures tyre tread depth, scans the engine with a thermal camera, and listens for unusual sounds. This is a **point-in-time snapshot** — like a photograph of your car's health.

- **Trend Monitoring** is what happens when you install a **digital dashboard app** in your car that continuously logs fuel efficiency, engine temperature, RPM patterns, and battery voltage *every single day*. After 6 months, the app shows you a **graph** — your battery voltage has been slowly dropping, and your fuel efficiency is gradually declining. The app *predicts* that your battery will fail within 2 weeks. This is a **time-series analytical view** — like a movie of your car's health.

> [!NOTE]
> The car analogy maps directly to industrial machinery: *Inspection = Periodic medical check-up*; *Trend Monitoring = Continuous wearable health tracker (Fitbit for machines)*.

### 1.3 Core Categorization Matrix

| Domain | Time Resolution | Data Type | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Condition Checking** | Discrete (scheduled) | Binary / Threshold | Pass / Fail decision |
| **Inspection** | Discrete (planned) | Qualitative + Quantitative | Defect location, severity |
| **Trend Monitoring** | Continuous / High-frequency | Time-series numerical | Degradation curve, RUL forecast |

### 1.4 Standard KTU Vocabulary (Must Memorize)

- **Walk-around Inspection** — Manual sensory check by operator.
- **Routine Inspection** — Scheduled, documented check.
- **Detailed Inspection** — Instrumented, comprehensive examination.
- **Continuous Monitoring** — Sensor-based, real-time data stream.
- **Periodic Monitoring** — Fixed-interval data acquisition.
- **Alarm Threshold** — A pre-set numerical value beyond which the system generates a warning.
- **Baseline** — The reference condition data captured during **commissioning** or **post-overhaul** state.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** In KTU answers, always distinguish *qualitative observation* (inspection) from *quantitative measurement* (condition checking) and *temporal analysis* (trend monitoring). This single sentence often scores 2 marks by itself.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Condition Checking and Inspection

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes a **three-tier inspection hierarchy**:

**Tier 1: Sensory (Human-Detectable) Inspection**
- Visual (eyesight)
- Auditory (sound — knocking, rattling, whining)
- Tactile (touch — temperature, vibration felt by hand)
- Olfactory (smell — burning insulation, oil degradation)

**Tier 2: Simple Instrumented Inspection**
- Stroboscope (for rotating component speed measurement)
- Tachometer (RPM verification)
- Hand-held infrared thermometer (point temperature)
- Borescope / Endoscope (internal cavity inspection)

**Tier 3: Advanced Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**
- **Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT)** — Surface defect detection
- **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** — Surface and near-surface defect detection in ferromagnetic materials
- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — Internal flaw detection using high-frequency sound waves (typically **1 MHz – 25 MHz**)
- **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — X-ray or Gamma-ray based volumetric inspection
- **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** — Surface and sub-surface defect detection in conductive materials

### 2.2 The Trend Monitoring Operational Loop

Trend monitoring operates on a closed-loop, **four-stage data-to-decision pipeline**:

1. **Data Acquisition** — Sensors (accelerometer, thermocouple, pressure transducer) sample the condition parameter at a fixed frequency ($f_s$).
2. **Data Storage** — Raw or processed values are stored in a historian database with time-stamps.
3. **Data Analysis** — Statistical and signal processing methods extract features (RMS, kurtosis, peak, crest factor).
4. **Decision Trigger** — When an extracted feature crosses an **alarm threshold** OR a **trending slope** exceeds a permissible rate, a maintenance work order is auto-generated.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU student must be able to reproduce from memory for the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** and **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** under the 2024 scheme.

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Legend | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | Arithmetic mean of $n$ readings | Establishes the **baseline value** for trend comparison |
| 2 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Sample standard deviation (Bessel corrected) | Quantifies **natural scatter** in condition data |
| 3 | $UCL = \bar{x} + 3\sigma$ | Upper Control Limit | Alarm threshold for **upward deviation** |
| 4 | $LCL = \bar{x} - 3\sigma$ | Lower Control Limit | Alarm threshold for **downward deviation** |
| 5 | $C_f = \dfrac{x_{peak}}{x_{rms}}$ | Crest Factor (Peak-to-RMS ratio) | Detects **incipient bearing defects** (typical healthy: 3–5) |
| 6 | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2(t)\, dt}$ | Root Mean Square over period $T$ | Industry-standard **vibration severity index** (ISO 10816) |
| 7 | $\text{COV} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ | Coefficient of Variation | Normalized dispersion metric for **cross-machine comparison** |
| 8 | $m = \dfrac{n\sum t_i x_i - \sum t_i \sum x_i}{n\sum t_i^2 - (\sum t_i)^2}$ | Slope of linear trend (least squares) | Rate of **degradation per unit time** |
| 9 | $RUL = \dfrac{x_{limit} - x_{current}}{m}$ | Remaining Useful Life estimate | Predictive maintenance **forecast horizon** |
| 10 | $K = \dfrac{\frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^4}{\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2\right]^2}$ | Kurtosis (4th statistical moment) | **Bearing fault indicator** (healthy $\approx 3$, faulty $>4$) |
| 11 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \times f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling frequency | Sets the **minimum sensor sampling rate** |
| 12 | $CF = \dfrac{C_p}{\text{Cost of Failure}}$ | Criticality Factor | Risk-based inspection **priority ranking** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Note:** The absolute value / vertical bar notation has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in the LaTeX code to prevent Markdown table parser breakage. In your exam answer sheets, use single vertical bars `|x|`.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Statistics

**Why the $3\sigma$ rule?**
For a normally distributed process, approximately **99.73%** of all data points lie within three standard deviations of the mean. Therefore, any excursion beyond the **UCL/LCL** has only a **0.27% probability** of being a random fluctuation — meaning it is almost certainly a real fault signal. This is the foundation of the **Western Electric Rules** and **Nelson Rules** used in SPC.

**Why Crest Factor and Kurtosis?**
- A **healthy bearing** produces nearly sinusoidal vibration (crest factor $\approx \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ for pure sine, but typically 3–5 in practice due to broadband noise).
