- A **faulty bearing** produces **periodic impacts** — sharp spikes that dramatically elevate both the **crest factor** and the **kurtosis** value.
- These are **early indicators** — they rise **months before** the RMS vibration crosses ISO 10816 alarm limits.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Power Generation:** Trending of turbine bearing temperature and vibration predicts bearing failure 4–8 weeks in advance, avoiding catastrophic downtime worth **₹50 lakhs/day**.
- **Aviation (MRO):** Walk-around inspection + ultrasonic inspection of fuselage skin is mandated by **DGCA CAR Section 2 Series I Part V**.
- **Oil & Gas:** Radiographic inspection of pressure vessels during scheduled shutdowns.
- **Manufacturing:** Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts on CNC spindle vibration to maintain ISO 9001 quality conformance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Trend Slope (Least Squares Method)

**Problem Context:** A maintenance engineer has recorded the following **vibration RMS** values for a motor bearing over 6 successive monthly inspections. Derive the **linear trend equation** and predict the vibration value at the **7th month**, given the alarm threshold is **7.10 mm/s**.

| Month ($t_i$) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vibration ($x_i$) in mm/s | 3.20 | 3.55 | 3.90 | 4.30 | 4.75 | 5.25 |

**Step 1: List the necessary summations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 6 \\
\sum t_i &= 1+2+3+4+5+6 = 21 \\
\sum x_i &= 3.20+3.55+3.90+4.30+4.75+5.25 = 24.95 \\
\sum t_i^2 &= 1+4+9+16+25+36 = 91 \\
\sum t_i x_i &= (1)(3.20)+(2)(3.55)+(3)(3.90)+(4)(4.30)+(5)(4.75)+(6)(5.25) \\
&= 3.20 + 7.10 + 11.70 + 17.20 + 23.75 + 31.50 = 94.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the linear regression slope formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{n \sum t_i x_i - \sum t_i \sum x_i}{n \sum t_i^2 - (\sum t_i)^2} \\
m &= \frac{(6)(94.45) - (21)(24.95)}{(6)(91) - (21)^2} \\
m &= \frac{566.70 - 523.95}{546 - 441} \\
m &= \frac{42.75}{105} \\
m &= 0.4071 \text{ mm/s per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the intercept $b$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \bar{x} - m \bar{t} \\
\bar{x} &= \frac{24.95}{6} = 4.1583 \\
\bar{t} &= \frac{21}{6} = 3.5 \\
b &= 4.1583 - (0.4071)(3.5) \\
b &= 4.1583 - 1.4250 \\
b &= 2.7333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Write the trend equation.**

$$
x(t) = 0.4071\,t + 2.7333
$$

**Step 5: Predict the value at $t = 7$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(7) &= (0.4071)(7) + 2.7333 \\
x(7) &= 2.8497 + 2.7333 \\
x(7) &= 5.583 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Estimate Remaining Useful Life (RUL).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RUL &= \frac{x_{limit} - x_{current}}{m} \\
RUL &= \frac{7.10 - 5.25}{0.4071} \\
RUL &= \frac{1.85}{0.4071} \\
RUL &= 4.544 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The bearing vibration is increasing at **0.4071 mm/s per month** and will cross the **7.10 mm/s threshold** in approximately **4.5 months**. A corrective replacement should be planned within this window.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Inspection Method Selection Logic

| Defect Type | Material | Defect Location | Recommended NDT Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface crack | Steel | Surface | Liquid Penetrant (PT) | Capillary action draws penetrant into crack |
| Surface crack | Steel | Surface or near-surface | Magnetic Particle (MT) | Magnetic flux leakage at discontinuity |
| Internal void | Aluminum casting | Sub-surface / volumetric | Radiographic (RT) | X-rays penetrate and reveal internal density variation |
| Internal flaw | Steel forging | Deep sub-surface | Ultrasonic (UT) | Sound waves reflect off internal interfaces |
| Surface fatigue crack | Copper tube | Surface | Eddy Current (ET) | Induced currents disturbed by surface defects |
| Delamination | Composite panel | Sub-surface | Ultrasonic (UT) phased array | High-frequency waves detect bond-line separation |
| Corrosion thinning | Pipe wall | Through-thickness | Ultrasonic thickness gauging | Measures wall thickness from one side |

### 3.3 Statistical Control Chart Construction (Worked Example)

**Data:** 10 successive samples of motor current drawn (in Amperes) measured at 8-hour intervals:
**8.1, 8.3, 8.0, 8.2, 8.4, 8.1, 8.3, 8.2, 8.5, 8.2**

**Step 1: Compute the mean.**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{8.1+8.3+8.0+8.2+8.4+8.1+8.3+8.2+8.5+8.2}{10} = \frac{82.3}{10} = 8.23 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2: Compute sample standard deviation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}} \\
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 &= (0.13)^2+(0.07)^2+(-0.23)^2+(-0.03)^2+(0.17)^2+(-0.13)^2+(0.07)^2+(-0.03)^2+(0.27)^2+(-0.03)^2 \\
&= 0.0169+0.0049+0.0529+0.0009+0.0289+0.0169+0.0049+0.0009+0.0729+0.0009 \\
&= 0.2009 \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{0.2009}{9}} = \sqrt{0.02232} = 0.1494 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute control limits.**

$$
UCL = 8.23 + 3(0.1494) = 8.23 + 0.4482 = 8.678 \text{ A}
$$

$$
LCL = 8.23 - 3(0.1494) = 8.23 - 0.4482 = 7.782 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4: Decision.** All 10 readings lie between **7.782 A and 8.678 A**. The process is **in statistical control**. No alarm is triggered.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Condition Monitoring & Trend Monitoring Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ASSET UNDER MONITORING] --> B[PHYSICAL PARAMETERS]
    B --> C[Vibration]
    B --> D[Temperature]
    B --> E[Pressure]
    B --> F[Acoustic Emission]
    B --> G[Lubricant Condition]

    C --> H[SENSOR LAYER]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I[Accelerometer]
    H --> J[Thermocouple]
    H --> K[Pressure Transducer]
    H --> L[AE Sensor]
    H --> M[Oil Spectrometer]

    I --> N[DATA ACQUISITION UNIT]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N

    N --> O[EDGE PROCESSING]

    O --> P[FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    P --> P1[RMS Calculation]
    P --> P2[Peak Detection]
    P --> P3[Kurtosis Analysis]
    P --> P4[Spectral FFT]

    P1 --> Q[STORAGE HISTORIAN]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    P4 --> Q

    Q --> R{ANALYSIS ENGINE}

    R --> S[Threshold Comparison]
    R --> T[Statistical SPC Charts]
    R --> U[Trend Slope RUL Forecast]
    R --> V[Machine Learning Model]

    S --> W{DECISION TRIGGER}
    T --> W
    U --> W
    V --> W

    W --> X[Normal Operation]
    W --> Y[Alarm Generated]
    W --> Z[Work Order in CMMS]

    Z --> AA[MAINTENANCE ACTION]
    AA --> AB[Inspection Feedback Loop]
    AB --> A
```

### 4.2 Inspection Method Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Defect Suspected] --> q1{Defect Location?}

    q1 --> surf[Surface] --> q2{Material Type?}
    q1 --> subs[Near Surface] --> q3{Ferromagnetic?}
    q1 --> vol[Volumetric Internal] --> q4{Access From Both Sides?}

    q2 --> cond[Conductive] --> m1[Eddy Current Testing ET]
    q2 --> noncond[Non Conductive] --> m2[Liquid Penetrant PT]

    q3 --> yes[Yes] --> m3[Magnetic Particle MT]
    q3 --> no[No] --> m4[Ultrasonic UT]

    q4 --> yes2[Yes] --> m5[Radiographic RT]
    q4 --> no2[No One Side Only] --> m6[Ultrasonic UT]
```

### 4.3 Trend Monitoring Life Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Baseline Capture] --> B[Periodic Data Sampling]
    B --> C[Statistical Analysis]
    C --> D{Trend Within Limits?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Continue Monitoring]
    D -->|No| F[Investigate Anomaly]
    F --> G[Root Cause Diagnosis]
    G --> H[Predict RUL]
    H --> I{Action Required?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Schedule Maintenance]
    I -->|No| E
    J --> K[Maintenance Action]
    K --> L[Post Maintenance Verification]
    L --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the condition monitoring cycle" in a 7-mark question, the above **Trend Monitoring Life Cycle** diagram is the expected answer structure. The closed-loop returning to *Baseline Capture* after maintenance is the **key visual element** examiners look for.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between Condition Checking and Trend Monitoring in the context of predictive maintenance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Condition Checking | Trend Monitoring |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | Periodic, discrete intervals | Continuous or high-frequency |
| **Data Type** | Snapshot / instantaneous | Time-series / longitudinal |
| **Output** | Pass/Fail, defect map | Degradation curve, RUL prediction |
| **Decision Logic** | Threshold comparison at one instant | Slope, pattern, statistical deviation analysis |
| **Example** | Quarterly ultrasonic thickness check | Daily vibration RMS logging into SCADA |

*Conclusion:* Condition checking answers *"What is the condition NOW?"* while trend monitoring answers *"What will the condition BE, and WHEN will it fail?"* — the latter is a *predictive* capability that the former cannot provide. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*List any four Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods commonly used during condition inspection of industrial equipment and state one specific defect each method can detect.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT)** — Detects surface-breaking cracks in non-porous metals. *(0.75 Mark)*
2. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — Detects internal flaws such as voids, inclusions, and hydrogen-induced cracking in thick sections. *(0.75 Mark)*
3. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** — Detects surface and near-surface discontinuities in ferromagnetic components like gears and shafts. *(0.75 Mark)*
4. **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** — Detects surface cracks, corrosion thinning, and conductivity variation in tubes and heat exchanger surfaces. *(0.75 Mark)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You are required to answer **ONE full question** from a Module choice of two. Below, both alternatives are fully solved.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain in detail the various categories of Condition Checking and Inspection activities used in a modern maintenance program. Use examples for each category.*

**Model Solution:**

Condition checking and inspection activities can be classified into **three progressive tiers** based on complexity and instrumentation:

**1. Sensory Inspection (Tier 1) [2 Marks]**
Conducted by trained operators using the five senses.
- **Visual:** Look for oil leaks, corrosion deposits, loose fasteners, cracks, discoloration.
- **Auditory:** Listen for unusual whining (bearing), knocking (loose component), hissing (steam leak).
- **Tactile:** Touch the housing to detect abnormal heat or excessive vibration.
- *Example:* A machine operator doing a pre-shift walk-around.

**2. Instrumented Inspection (Tier 2) [2 Marks]**
Uses simple portable instruments for quantitative measurement.
- **Stroboscope** to check fan blade speed and observe frozen motion.
- **Hand-held IR thermometer** to detect hot spots in switchgear.
- **Borescope** to inspect the internal bore of a cylinder without disassembly.
- *Example:* Quarterly thermography scan of an electrical panel.

**3. Non-Destructive Testing (Tier 3) [3 Marks]**
Sophisticated methods requiring certified NDT Level II/III personnel.
- **PT** for turbine blade leading-edge cracks.
- **MT** for welded joint surface crack detection.
- **UT** for boiler tube wall thickness measurement.
- **RT** for critical casting volumetric integrity.
- **ET** for aircraft wing skin inspection.
- *Example:* Annual shutdown NDT campaign in a petrochemical plant.

*Conclusion:* The three tiers are deployed in a **layered strategy** — Tier 1 daily, Tier 2 monthly, Tier 3 yearly or based on Tier 1/2 findings. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A centrifugal pump bearing shows the following monthly vibration RMS readings (in mm/s): 2.8, 3.1, 3.5, 3.8, 4.2, 4.7. The alarm threshold is 6.0 mm/s. Using linear regression, determine (i) the trend equation, (ii) the vibration at the 10th month, and (iii) the Remaining Useful Life in months.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

| Month $t_i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vibration $x_i$ (mm/s) | 2.8 | 3.1 | 3.5 | 3.8 | 4.2 | 4.7 |

**Step 1: Compute the summations.** [1 Mark]

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 6 \\
\sum t_i &= 21 \\
\sum x_i &= 2.8+3.1+3.5+3.8+4.2+4.7 = 22.1 \\
\sum t_i^2 &= 91 \\
\sum t_i x_i &= (1)(2.8)+(2)(3.1)+(3)(3.5)+(4)(3.8)+(5)(4.2)+(6)(4.7) \\
&= 2.8 + 6.2 + 10.5 + 15.2 + 21.0 + 28.2 = 83.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the slope $m$.** [2 Marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{n\sum t_i x_i - \sum t_i \sum x_i}{n\sum t_i^2 - (\sum t_i)^2} \\
m &= \frac{6(83.9) - (21)(22.1)}{6(91) - (21)^2} \\
m &= \frac{503.4 - 464.1}{546 - 441} \\
m &= \frac{39.3}{105} \\
m &= 0.3743 \text{ mm/s per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the intercept $b$.** [1 Mark]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{22.1}{6} = 3.6833 \\
\bar{t} &= 3.5 \\
b &= 3.6833 - (0.3743)(3.5) \\
b &= 3.6833 - 1.3100 \\
b &= 2.3733
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Trend Equation [0.5 Mark]:**

$$
x(t) = 0.3743\,t + 2.3733
$$

**(ii) Vibration at the 10th month [1 Mark]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(10) &= (0.3743)(10) + 2.3733 \\
x(10) &= 3.743 + 2.3733 \\
x(10) &= 6.1163 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Remaining Useful Life [1.5 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RUL &= \frac{x_{limit} - x_{current}}{m} = \frac{6.0 - 4.7}{0.3743} \\
RUL &= \frac{1.3}{0.3743} \\
RUL &= 3.473 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conclusion:* The vibration will cross the 6.0 mm/s alarm threshold between the **9th and 10th month**, and a **proactive bearing replacement must be scheduled within 3.5 months**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a neat sketch, describe the four stages of the trend monitoring life cycle. Explain the role of the baseline in this process.*

**Model Solution:**

**The Four Stages of Trend Monitoring Life Cycle:** [4 Marks for explanation + 3 Marks for diagram description]

**Stage 1 — Baseline Establishment**
Immediately after commissioning, overhaul, or major repair, the asset is run under **known normal operating conditions** and a comprehensive set of condition parameters (vibration, temperature, pressure, current, lubrication quality) is recorded. This dataset forms the **Baseline Reference Vector**. Without a valid baseline, no trend comparison is mathematically possible.

**Stage 2 — Periodic Data Sampling**
At regular intervals (or continuously), the same set of parameters is re-measured under similar operating conditions. Data is time-stamped and stored in a historian database.

**Stage 3 — Statistical Analysis and Comparison**
New data is compared with the baseline using:
- **Threshold comparison** (UCL/LCL on $3\sigma$ control charts)
- **Trend slope analysis** (linear regression)
- **Advanced pattern recognition** (machine learning models)

**Stage 4 — Decision and Action**
If a deviation is detected:
- **Within warning band:** Increase monitoring frequency.
- **At alarm threshold:** Schedule corrective work.
- **Post-maintenance:** The cycle restarts from Stage 1 with a new baseline.

**Diagram Description (for answer sheet):** Draw four boxes labelled *Baseline → Sampling → Analysis → Decision* connected by arrows in a **closed loop** returning from Decision back to Baseline. Mark the baseline as a *"Reference Vector"* feeding into the Analysis stage as a dashed input arrow. **[3 Marks for diagram]**

*Conclusion:* The **closed-loop nature** is the defining feature — the cycle restarts after every maintenance intervention, ensuring that trend analysis always references the **current known-good state** of the asset. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A motor's bearing housing temperature readings (in °C) taken at the start of each shift for 8 days are: 62, 63, 64, 65, 66, 68, 71, 75. Establish a control chart (assume $\bar{R} = 2.0°C$ for control limit construction) and identify any points that breach the upper control limit. Comment on the trend.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the mean and standard deviation.** [2 Marks]

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{62+63+64+65+66+68+71+75}{8} = \frac{534}{8} = 66.75°C
$$

Since the problem provides $\bar{R} = 2.0°C$, the standard deviation is estimated using:

$$
\sigma \approx \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{2.0}{1.128} = 1.773°C
$$

(where $d_2 = 1.128$ for subgroup size $n = 2$, a standard SPC constant)

**Step 2: Compute control limits.** [1 Mark]

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL &= 66.75 + 3(1.773) = 66.75 + 5.319 = 72.07°C \\
LCL &= 66.75 - 3(1.773) = 66.75 - 5.319 = 61.43°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Plot / tabulate and check each point.** [2 Marks]

| Day | Temp (°C) | Within [LCL, UCL]? | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 62 | Yes | OK |
| 2 | 63 | Yes | OK |
| 3 | 64 | Yes | OK |
| 4 | 65 | Yes | OK |
| 5 | 66 | Yes | OK |
| 6 | 68 | Yes | OK |
| 7 | 71 | Yes | OK |
| 8 | **75** | **No (75 > 72.07)** | **UCL BREACH** |

**Step 4: Comment on the trend.** [2 Marks]

The data exhibits a **clear monotonic upward trend** with an apparent **acceleration in the last 3 days** (68 → 71 → 75, i.e., +3, +4, +4). Although only the 8th day formally breaches the UCL statistically, the **pattern of progressively larger increments** is itself a **Western Electric Rule violation** (specifically, 7 consecutive points trending upward). This is a strong indicator of **progressive bearing failure** — likely due to lubricant degradation, raceway spalling, or improper alignment causing frictional heat buildup.

*Conclusion:* The bearing should be **inspected immediately** (vibration analysis + oil sampling) and likely replaced before catastrophic seizure occurs. The 3-$\sigma$ rule is necessary but not sufficient — **pattern-based rules** provide earlier warnings. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing condition checking with trend monitoring** — they are NOT synonyms. Saying *"trend monitoring is just inspection done more often"* loses 1–2 marks. Use the exact phrase *"continuous time-series analysis versus discrete snapshot evaluation."*
> 2. **Forgetting units in the slope** — The slope $m$ in vibration trend must be reported as **mm/s per month** (or the equivalent), not as a bare number.
> 3. **Misapplying the $3\sigma$ rule** — Students often write $2\sigma$ (which gives 95% confidence, used in Six Sigma DMAIC at "warning" level) instead of $3\sigma$ (99.73% confidence, used for "action" level). The KTU syllabus specifies $3\sigma$ as the standard.
> 4. **Skipping the baseline discussion** — Any trend monitoring answer that does not mention the role of the **baseline** will lose at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing RUL formula direction** — RUL uses $(x_{limit} - x_{current})$, NOT $(x_{current} - x_{limit})$. Getting the sign wrong gives a negative RUL, which is a red-flag mistake.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark inspection questions** — A question worth 7 marks almost always expects a labelled diagram. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print This Section):**
>
> - **Condition Checking** = Point-in-time, **Inspection** = Scheduled examination, **Trend Monitoring** = Continuous time-series. These three are NOT interchangeable.
> - **Five senses inspection** = cheapest, fastest, done daily by operators. Called *Tier 1*.
> - **NDT methods** to memorize: **P-M-U-R-E** — **P**enetrant, **M**agnetic, **U**ltrasonic, **R**adiographic, **E**ddy Current.
> - **Baseline** is the **soul of trend monitoring** — no baseline means no trend.
> - **UCL = Mean + 3σ** ; **LCL = Mean − 3σ** ; this gives **99.73% confidence**.
> - **Crest Factor = Peak / RMS** ; **Kurtosis** is the 4th moment — both rise sharply in faulty bearings.
> - **RUL = (Threshold − Current Reading) / Slope** — always positive by construction.
> - **Least squares slope formula** must be reproduced from memory: $m = \dfrac{n\sum t_i x_i - \sum t_i \sum x_i}{n\sum t_i^2 - (\sum t_i)^2}$
> - **ISO 10816** is the vibration severity standard — know the four zones (A, B, C, D) conceptually.
> - **Sampling rate** must be at least $2 \times f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion) — relevant for vibration data acquisition.
> - **Western Electric Rules** include the famous *"7 points in a row trending up or down"* — beyond the 3-σ check.
> - **Closed-loop** trend monitoring: every maintenance action restarts the baseline — this is the answer to any *"draw the cycle"* question.
> - **Application domains to mention** in answers: Power, Aviation (DGCA), Oil & Gas, Manufacturing — examiners reward industry-context awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
