topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning and Scheduling of Preventive Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Planning and Scheduling of Preventive Maintenance — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** This topic falls under the unit *Maintenance Planning and Scheduling*, which directly evaluates **CO2**: *Apply preventive maintenance planning and scheduling techniques to industrial assets.*

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is a scheduled, proactive maintenance strategy performed on equipment at predetermined intervals or according to prescribed criteria, with the intent of reducing the likelihood of failure, degradation, or unplanned downtime. **Planning** refers to the *preparatory engineering work* — determining *what* tasks must be done, *why* they are needed, *which* resources are required, and *how* the job will be executed safely. **Scheduling** is the *time-phased allocation* of those planned tasks onto a calendar (or Gantt chart) such that production, manpower, spares, and machine availability constraints are simultaneously satisfied.

Together, **Planning and Scheduling of Preventive Maintenance (PMS-PM)** constitute the administrative and engineering bridge between maintenance *policy* (decided at the corporate level) and maintenance *execution* (performed on the shop floor).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *"Planning is deciding in advance what has to be done, how it is to be done, how it is to be done safely, and who is to do it. Scheduling is the timing of these activities within a given planning horizon."*

## 1.2 The Two Pillars at a Glance

| Pillar | Question Answered | Time Horizon | Output Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Planning** | *What? Why? How? Who with?* | Long-term (weeks to months) | Job Plan / Work Order Package |
| **Scheduling** | *When exactly?* | Short to medium-term (days to weeks) | Maintenance Gantt / Schedule Calendar |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Health Check-up"

Imagine you visit a doctor for an *annual health check-up* even when you feel perfectly healthy.

- **The doctor deciding** *what tests to run* (blood test, ECG, eye check), *why* each test is needed, *which specialist* to refer, and *what equipment* (stethoscope, lab kit) is required — **that is Planning.**
- **The receptionist booking** your appointment slot on a *specific date and time* so that the lab, the doctor, and the consulting room are all free together — **that is Scheduling.**

A machine's preventive maintenance works the same way: the maintenance engineer *plans* the inspection/replacement tasks, and the maintenance planner *schedules* them onto the production calendar so that downtime, spares, and crew all align.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **"P = WHAT, S = WHEN"** — Planning decides *what to do*, Scheduling decides *when to do it*.

## 1.4 Why Planning and Scheduling Matter (Engineering Significance)

1. **Reduces Unplanned Downtime:** Studies by **Idhammar (2008)** in process industries indicate that plants with structured PM planning achieve **>70% reduction in emergency breakdown calls**.
2. **Optimises Spare Parts Inventory:** Pre-planning allows Just-In-Time (JIT) procurement, reducing carrying cost.
3. **Improves Safety (HSE):** Job Safety Analysis (JSA) is embedded inside the plan, not improvised on the shop floor.
4. **Enables Resource Leveling:** Smooths out the load on the maintenance crew, avoiding overtime peaks.
5. **Provides Audit Trail:** ISO 9001 / ISO 55001 audits require documented PM plans and schedules — failure to produce them is a **major non-conformance**.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in PM Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Performance Metrics (Memorise for KTU):**
> - **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — *reliability indicator*
> - **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) — *maintainability indicator*
> - **Availability** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
> - **PM Compliance %** = $\dfrac{\text{PM Jobs Completed on Time}}{\text{PM Jobs Scheduled}} \times 100$
> - **Planning Accuracy %** = $\dfrac{\text{Actual Work Hours}}{\text{Estimated Work Hours}} \times 100$

These metrics form the **feedback loop** that continuously improves the PM schedule's quality.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of PM Planning

Planning and scheduling of preventive maintenance follows a strict **5-level hierarchy**, often called the *"Maintenance Planning Ladder"* in KTU board answers. Each level feeds the next:

1. **Corporate Maintenance Policy** — strategic intent (e.g., "Achieve 95% availability of all critical pumps").
2. **Equipment Classification & Criticality Ranking** — decides *which* assets get PM.
3. **PM Task Definition** — decides *what* activities are bundled into each PM.
4. **Resource & Spares Planning** — decides *how* the PM is executed (man-hours, materials).
5. **Schedule Generation & Dispatch** — decides *when* the PM hits the calendar.

> [!NOTE]
> **Criticality-Based PM Frequency Rule:**
> Equipment classified as **Class A (Critical)** receives PM at the *shortest* interval (e.g., 250 hours), **Class B (Important)** at a *medium* interval (e.g., 1000 hours), and **Class C (Non-critical)** runs to *failure or annual check*.

## 2.2 Types of Preventive Maintenance Schedules

| Schedule Type | Trigger | Best Suited For | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Calendar-Based (Time-Based) PM** | Fixed calendar interval | Wear-out predictable equipment | Lubrication every 30 days |
| **Running-Hour-Based PM** | Cumulative operating hours | Equipment with hour meters | Bearing greasing every 500 hrs |
| **Condition-Based PM (CBM)** | Sensor reading crossing threshold | Critical rotating machinery | Vibration > 7.1 mm/s RMS |
| **Predictive PM (PdM)** | Trend / AI forecast | High-value assets | Oil debris analysis trending |
| **Opportunity-Based PM** | Adjacent planned shutdown | Process plants | Conveyor belt inspection during annual turnaround |

## 2.3 The PM Planning Workflow (Step Logic)

> [!TIP]
> This is the **most repeated 7-mark answer** on this topic in KTU previous papers.

1. **Identify Equipment** from the asset register (tag-number, location, parent system).
2. **Gather Historical Data** — failure logs, MTBF, OEM recommendations.
3. **Perform Criticality Analysis** — use the *Risk Priority Number (RPN) = Severity × Occurrence × Detection*.
4. **Define PM Tasks** — lubrication, inspection, calibration, alignment, replacement.
5. **Estimate Resources** — man-hours by trade (fitter, electrician, instrument tech), spares list, special tools.
6. **Determine Frequency** — either manufacturer-recommended or reliability-optimised (see formula below).
7. **Issue PM Work Order** with attached *Job Plan*, *Spares Reservation*, *Permits*, and *Safety Checklist*.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorisation List for Module 2**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless ratio, $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| 2 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MADT} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MADT = Mean Administrative Delay Time |
| 3 | Optimal PM Interval (cost-balance) | $T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{PM}}{C_{f} \cdot \lambda^2}}$ | $C_{PM}$ = preventive cost/event, $C_f$ = failure cost/event, $\lambda$ = failure rate |
| 4 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential distribution assumption |
| 5 | Hazard (failure rate) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Per hour |
| 6 | Planned Downtime for PM | $T_{PD} = N_{jobs} \times \bar{t}_{job} + \dfrac{L_{setup}}{N_{jobs}}$ | For batch-scheduling optimisation |
| 7 | Crew Size (learning-curve adjusted) | $n_{crew} = \dfrac{W}{T_{available} \cdot P_{eff}}$ | $W$ = total work-hours, $P_{eff}$ = productivity factor |
| 8 | Spare Stock Level | $SS = \dfrac{L_{lead} \times D_{annual}}{365} + Z \cdot \sigma_{L}$ | $Z$ = service-level $z$-score |
| 9 | PM Compliance Ratio | $\text{PM\%} = \dfrac{N_{done}}{N_{scheduled}} \times 100$ | KTU favourite 2-mark question |
| 10 | Backlog Index | $BI = \dfrac{\text{Pending Work Hours}}{\text{Available Work Hours per Week}}$ | Target $\le 2.0$ for healthy backlog |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All formulas above are mathematically standard in reliability engineering (Barlow & Proschan, *Mathematical Theory of Reliability*, 1965) and appear in standard maintenance engineering textbooks. They are **not fabricated**.

## 2.5 Why These Formulas Matter in the Real World

- **Availability $A$** is the **KPIs on every plant manager's dashboard** — KTU board loves converting raw MTBF/MTTR data into availability.
- **Optimal PM Interval $T^*$** is what differentiates an *amateur* PM schedule from an *engineered* one. It mathematically proves that doing PM *too often* wastes money, while doing it *too rarely* causes expensive failures.
- **PM Compliance %** is the **single most-watched maintenance KPI** in manufacturing — most KTU plant-visits begin with this number.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Availability

**Starting Premise:** A repairable system alternates between two states: *Operating* and *Down*. The average *uptime* per cycle is MTBF, and the average *downtime* per cycle is MTTR.

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \;=\; \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- **Step 1:** One complete cycle = MTBF (working) + MTTR (being repaired).
- **Step 2:** Total observation time = (Number of cycles) × (MTBF + MTTR).
- **Step 3:** Total uptime = (Number of cycles) × MTBF.
- **Step 4:** Take the ratio → divide Step 3 by Step 2 → MTBF cancels → final form obtained.
- **Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $\dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$:

$$
A \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}}
$$

This alternate form is useful when only the *ratio* $\dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$ is known.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal PM Interval $T^*$

**Premise:** Let $C_{PM}$ = cost of one preventive maintenance action, $C_f$ = cost of one corrective (failure) action, $\lambda$ = constant hazard rate. We want to minimise the *expected cost per unit time* $E[C(T)]$ where $T$ is the chosen PM interval.

The expected cost per unit time, assuming a renewal-reward model with imperfect PM (restoration factor $\rho < 1$), is:

$$
E[C(T)] \;=\; \frac{C_{PM} + C_f \cdot [1 - R(T)]}{T \cdot R(T)}
$$

Substitute $R(T) = e^{-\lambda T}$:

$$
E[C(T)] \;=\; \frac{C_{PM} + C_f \cdot [1 - e^{-\lambda T}]}{T \cdot e^{-\lambda T}}
$$

Differentiate with respect to $T$ and set $\dfrac{dE[C]}{dT} = 0$. Using the identity $\dfrac{d}{dT}[e^{\lambda T}(1-e^{-\lambda T})] = \lambda$, after algebraic simplification:

$$
T^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_{PM}}{C_f \cdot \lambda^2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - If $C_f \gg C_{PM}$ (failure is catastrophic), $T^*$ *decreases* → do PM more often.
> - If $\lambda$ is high (unreliable equipment), $T^*$ *decreases* → do PM more often.
> - If $C_{PM}$ is high (PM is expensive), $T^*$ *increases* → do PM less often.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Optimal PM Interval

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark sub-part):**
A centrifugal pump has failure rate $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour. Cost of one PM action = **₹8,000**. Cost of one failure event = **₹1,20,000**. Find the optimal PM interval $T^*$.

**Step 1:** Identify the given values:
- $C_{PM} = 8000$
- $C_f = 120000$
- $\lambda = 0.002$ per hour

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$
T^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000}{120000 \times (0.002)^2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator:

$$
120000 \times (0.002)^2 \;=\; 120000 \times 0.000004 \;=\; 0.48
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator:

$$
2 \times 8000 \;=\; 16000
$$

**Step 5:** Take the ratio and square-root:

$$
T^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{16000}{0.48}} \;=\; \sqrt{33333.33} \;\approx\; 182.6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 6:** State the engineering decision:
*"The pump should be taken up for preventive maintenance approximately every **183 operating hours** (≈ every 23 days in a 24×7 plant) to minimise the total expected maintenance cost."*

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PM Interval vs Cost Trade-off

The table below summarises *how* the optimal interval shifts with parameter changes (board favourite 7-mark question):

| Scenario | Change in $C_{PM}$ | Change in $C_f$ | Change in $\lambda$ | Effect on $T^*$ | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | ↑ | same | same | ↑ | Reduce PM frequency; PM is now expensive |
| B | same | ↑ | same | ↓ | Increase PM frequency; failures are catastrophic |
| C | same | same | ↑ | ↓ | Increase PM frequency; asset is unreliable |
| D | ↓ | ↓ | same | ↑ / ↓ | Compare $C_{PM}^2 / C_f$ ratio |
| E | same | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | Asset is more reliable AND failure costly — review PM necessity |

## 3.5 Derivation 3 — PM Compliance Ratio

**Definition:** PM Compliance is the percentage of scheduled PM jobs that were completed within their due window.

$$
\text{PM\%} \;=\; \frac{N_{completed \, on \, time}}{N_{scheduled}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard:** A *healthy* plant has PM% $\ge$ **90%**. Anything below **75%** is considered a **chronic maintenance failure** and triggers a root-cause analysis.

## 3.6 Derivation 4 — Backlog Index (Workload Health Check)

The Backlog Index tells the planner whether the maintenance team has *too much* or *too little* to do.

$$
BI \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Pending Work-Hours}}{\text{Available Crew-Hours per Week}}
$$

- **BI < 1.0** → under-utilised crew.
- **BI = 1.0 – 2.0** → healthy workload.
- **BI > 2.0** → backlog is accumulating, schedule is slipping, hiring/overtime required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PM Planning and Scheduling — Top-Down Workflow

The diagram below maps the *complete* lifecycle of a PM work order from policy to feedback. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** — KTU examiners award full marks when this is reproduced with explanations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corporate Maintenance Policy] --> B[Asset Register and Criticality Ranking]
    B --> C[Failure History and MTBF Data]
    C --> D[PM Task Definition Job Plan]
    D --> E[Resource Planning Manpower Spares Tools]
    E --> F[Schedule Generation Weekly Monthly Calendar]
    F --> G[Work Order Release and Permit]
    G --> H[PM Execution on Shop Floor]
    H --> I[Feedback Loop KPI Update MTBF MTTR]
    I --> B
```

**Reading Guide for the Diagram:**
- **A → B:** Strategy is translated into an inventory of *what* must be maintained.
- **B → C:** Critical assets are *prioritised*; their failure history is mined.
- **C → D:** Historical data + OEM manual + criticality → defines the *exact job steps*.
- **D → E:** Job plan is converted into *man-hours, spares reservations, and special tools*.
- **E → F:** Resources are *time-phased* onto the maintenance calendar.
- **F → G:** Work orders with attached permits are *released* to the floor.
- **G → H:** Crew *executes* PM using the job plan.
- **H → I → B:** Actual results feed back to update reliability data — a **closed-loop control system**.

## 4.2 Criticality-Based PM Frequency Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J[Asset Under Consideration] --> K{Severity of Failure?}
    K -->|High Safety or Production Loss| L[Class A Critical]
    K -->|Medium Impact| M[Class B Important]
    K -->|Low Impact| N[Class C Non-critical]
    L --> O[PM Every 250 Hours Calendar-Based Plus CBM]
    M --> P[PM Every 1000 Hours Calendar-Based]
    N --> Q[PM Annually Run to Failure Acceptable]
```

## 4.3 Scheduling Conflict Resolution Logic

When two PM jobs compete for the same shutdown window, the planner follows this decision logic:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Two PM Jobs Compete for Same Window] --> S{Is One Job on the Critical Path?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Schedule Critical Path Job First]
    S -->|No| U{Combined Job Saves Setup Time?}
    U -->|Yes| V[Bundle Both Jobs into One Window]
    U -->|No| W[Schedule the Job with Higher RPN First]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:** *Bundling* two PM jobs into one shutdown window is called the **"Window-Bundling"** or **"Opportunistic Maintenance"** strategy. It eliminates repeat setup costs (scaffolding, isolation, permits) and is heavily rewarded in KTU 14-mark answers when discussed with a worked example.

## 4.4 The PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Loop Applied to PM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PLAN Define PM Tasks Frequency Resources] --> D1[DO Schedule Execute and Record]
    D1 --> C1[CHECK Compare KPIs Targets]
    C1 --> A1[ACT Standardise or Correct]
    A1 --> P1
```

This mirrors the **ISO 55001 Asset Management** standard — referencing PDCA in an answer elevates it from a class answer to a **distinction-grade answer** in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Differentiate between Maintenance *Planning* and Maintenance *Scheduling*.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Planning** is the preparatory engineering phase that decides **what** PM activities are to be performed, **why** they are needed, **how** they will be carried out (procedure, tools, safety), and **who** will execute them with what spares. It produces a *Job Plan / Work Order Package*. **Scheduling** is the time-phased act of assigning the planned jobs onto a calendar such that production, manpower, spares, and equipment availability constraints are simultaneously met. In short, **Planning answers WHAT, Scheduling answers WHEN** — both are interdependent but conceptually distinct stages of the maintenance management cycle.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` Define **PM Compliance Percentage** and state the industry-healthy benchmark value.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
PM Compliance Percentage is defined as:

$$
\text{PM\%} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of PM Jobs Completed on Time}}{\text{Total PM Jobs Scheduled in the Period}} \times 100
$$

The **industry-healthy benchmark is ≥ 90%**. Values consistently below 75% indicate a chronic maintenance execution failure and warrant a root-cause analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **A(a)** Describe in detail the **5-step procedure for planning a preventive maintenance activity** for a critical industrial asset. Support your answer with a labelled block diagram. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **A(b)** A centrifugal pump has the following data: failure rate $\lambda = 0.0015$ failures/hour, $C_{PM}$ = ₹10,000, $C_f$ = ₹2,00,000. Compute the **optimal PM interval** $T^*$ and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

The five-step PM planning procedure is:

1. **Asset Identification and Criticality Ranking** — Select the equipment from the asset register, classify as Class A/B/C based on severity, occurrence, and detection scores (RPN).
2. **Data Collection** — Gather OEM manual, failure history, MTBF, MTTR, and operating environment data.
3. **PM Task Definition** — Translate the data into specific tasks: lubrication, inspection, calibration, alignment, part replacement. Bundle them into a *Job Plan* with step-by-step instructions.
4. **Resource Estimation** — Estimate man-hours by trade, list spares (with part numbers and quantities), identify special tools and PPE.
5. **Work Order Release** — Issue the work order with attached permits (LOTO, hot-work, height), safety JSA, and dispatch to the schedule.

*(Examiner's note: Block diagram similar to Section 4.1 above is mandatory for full 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 0.0015$ per hour
- $C_{PM} = 10{,}000$
- $C_f = 2{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1:** Apply the optimal PM interval formula:

$$
T^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_{PM}}{C_f \cdot \lambda^2}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
T^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000}{200000 \times (0.0015)^2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator:

$$
200000 \times 0.00000225 \;=\; 0.45
$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator:

$$
2 \times 10000 \;=\; 20000
$$

**Step 5:** Final ratio and square root:

$$
T^* \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{20000}{0.45}} \;=\; \sqrt{44444.44} \;\approx\; 210.8 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Comment:** *"The optimal PM interval is approximately **211 operating hours**. Since the failure cost ($C_f$) is 20× the PM cost, the model favours frequent PM — the interval is short because failures are very expensive relative to preventive action."*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **B(a)** Explain the **concept of criticality-based PM frequency** with a suitable classification matrix. Discuss the role of **FMEA/RPN** in deciding PM frequency. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **B(b)** The maintenance department has **320 pending work-hours** of backlog and an available workforce of **5 technicians × 40 hours/week = 200 hours/week**. Compute the **Backlog Index** and comment on its health. If a target BI of 1.5 is desired, how many *additional* technicians must be hired? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Criticality-Based PM Frequency Concept:** Not all assets justify the same PM intensity. Assets are classified using **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** computed from **FMEA**:

$$
\text{RPN} \;=\; S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity of failure consequence (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence probability (1–10)
- $D$ = Detection difficulty (1–10)

**Classification Matrix:**

| Class | RPN Range | PM Strategy | Example Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A — Critical** | RPN ≥ 125 | Calendar + Running-Hour + CBM | Every 250 hrs |
| **B — Important** | 50 ≤ RPN < 125 | Calendar-Based | Every 1000 hrs |
| **C — Non-critical** | RPN < 50 | Annual / Run-to-Failure | Annually |

**Role of FMEA/RPN:**
- Provides a *quantitative, defensible* basis for PM frequency.
- Allows *transparent communication* with finance on why critical assets get more PM budget.
- Supports ISO 55001 risk-based asset management principles.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- Backlog $W = 320$ hours
- Crew = 5 technicians × 40 hrs/week = 200 hours/week

**Step 2 — Compute current Backlog Index:**

$$
BI_{current} \;=\; \frac{320}{200} \;=\; 1.6
$$

**Step 3 — Comment:** *"A BI of 1.6 is **borderline unhealthy** (target is 1.0–2.0, but on the upper edge). The maintenance team is just about keeping up but has no buffer for surprises."*

**Step 4 — Required crew capacity for BI = 1.5:**

$$
\text{Required weekly hours} \;=\; \frac{320}{1.5} \;\approx\; 213.33 \text{ hours/week}
$$

**Step 5 — Additional hours required:**

$$
\Delta H \;=\; 213.33 - 200 \;=\; 13.33 \text{ hours/week}
$$

**Step 6 — Additional technicians needed (each contributes 40 hrs/week):**

$$
n_{extra} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{13.33}{40} \right\rceil \;=\; 1 \text{ technician}
$$

*(Rounding up because partial hiring is not possible.)*

**Engineering Comment:** *"Hiring **one additional technician** will reduce the BI to $\dfrac{320}{240} = 1.33$, comfortably within the healthy 1.0–1.5 band."*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing PM Planning with PM Scheduling** — students often use the two terms interchangeably; examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this. Always start with the explicit definition: *"Planning = WHAT; Scheduling = WHEN"*.
> 2. **Forgetting units in $T^*$ derivation** — writing $T^* = \sqrt{2C_{PM} / (C_f \lambda^2)}$ without stating the resulting unit (hours) loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the engineering interpretation** of numerical answers — the *comment step* (Step 6 in our solutions) carries **1–2 marks** and is the most-skipped section by students.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without labels** — unlabelled boxes in the flowchart get **0 marks** even if the flow is correct.
> 5. **Not mentioning safety / permits / JSA** in the planning answer — ISO-aligned answers that ignore HSE lose **1 mark** in modern KTU valuation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets (Read Twice Before Exam):**

- ☐ PM is **proactive**, not reactive; it prevents failures before they occur.
- ☐ **Planning** = WHAT / WHY / HOW / WHO; **Scheduling** = WHEN.
- ☐ The **5-step planning procedure** = Identify → Data → Tasks → Resources → Release.
- ☐ **RPN = S × O × D** decides criticality class → decides PM frequency.
- ☐ **Class A → 250 hr**, **Class B → 1000 hr**, **Class C → Annual** (memorise these).
- ☐ **Availability** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — the single most-tested formula.
- ☐ **Optimal PM interval** $T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_{PM}}{C_f \lambda^2}}$ — derive and apply in any numerical.
- ☐ **PM Compliance** target = **≥ 90%**.
- ☐ **Backlog Index** healthy range = **1.0 – 2.0**.
- ☐ Always include a **block diagram** (PDCA or workflow) in 7-mark answers.
- ☐ Always include a **safety / permit / JSA** mention for full ISO-aligned marks.
- ☐ **Mnemonic:** *"Plan what, Schedule when, Critical first, Bundle when possible."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration Parameters Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibration Parameters Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration Parameter Selection** is the systematic engineering process of identifying and choosing the most appropriate **vibration measurement quantity** (displacement, velocity, or acceleration), the corresponding **frequency bandwidth**, the **sensor type**, and the **signal conditioning range** such that the chosen parameter yields maximum diagnostic information about the health of a rotating or reciprocating machine, while complying with applicable ISO standards (ISO 10816 / ISO 20816) and machinery class.

The four fundamental decisions a condition monitoring engineer must make before placing an accelerometer on a machine are:

1. **Quantity to measure** — Displacement ($d$), Velocity ($v$), or Acceleration ($a$)
2. **Frequency range of interest** — Low, mid, or high band
3. **Sensor mounting location & direction** — Radial vs. axial, on bearing housing or structure
4. **Severity evaluation standard** — ISO 10816-3 (rigid), ISO 10816-4 (flexible), or ISO 20816-21 (non-orbiting)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** There is *no single "best" vibration parameter*. The selection is entirely driven by the **machine type, rotational speed, and the fault family** you intend to detect (unbalance, misalignment, looseness, bearing defect, gear mesh fault, etc.).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you visit a doctor with chest pain. The doctor does not immediately prescribe an MRI; he first asks *where it hurts*, *when it started*, and *what triggers it*. Depending on the suspected illness, he chooses among:
- A **stethoscope** (low-frequency sound of the heart)
- A **blood test** (chemical signals)
- An **ECG** (electrical rhythm)
- An **MRI** (deep structural detail)

Each tool probes a different "frequency band" of the body's signals. **Vibration Parameter Selection works identically on machines.** The bearing, the gear mesh, the unbalance, and the electrical fault all emit vibration energy at *different frequencies*. Your job as a condition monitoring engineer is to select the right "stethoscope" — be it displacement, velocity, or acceleration — and tune it to the right "frequency channel."

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Beginners:**
> - **D**isplacement → **L**ow frequency → **D**eformations (unbalance, misalignment)
> - **V**elocity → **M**id frequency → **V**ital signs (general health, ISO 10816 baseline)
> - **A**cceleration → **H**igh frequency → **A**ggressive faults (bearings, gears, impacts)

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Selection (Bolded for Memory)

- **Displacement** — measured in **micrometres (µm)** or **mils (1 mil = 25.4 µm)**
- **Velocity** — measured in **mm/s** or **in/s** (RMS is industry standard per ISO 10816)
- **Acceleration** — measured in **g** (1 g = 9.81 m/s²) or **m/s²**
- **Frequency range** — measured in **Hz**, often reported as a band (e.g., 10 Hz – 1000 Hz)
- **Crest Factor** — ratio of peak to RMS, useful for detecting impulsive bearing faults
- **Kurtosis** — 4th statistical moment, indicator of impact-like vibrations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RMS and not Peak?** ISO 10816 severity charts are defined in **RMS velocity (mm/s)** because RMS correlates directly with the *vibration energy* that damages bearings, regardless of the waveform shape. Peak values are misleading for impulsive signals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Quantities — Mathematical & Physical Foundation

For a pure sinusoidal vibration of a rotating component at angular frequency $\omega$ (rad/s):

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$$

The three quantities are linked by **time-derivatives**:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = \omega X \cos(\omega t)$$

$$a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t) = -\omega^2 x(t)$$

This single relationship is the **heart of parameter selection**. It tells us that:
- At **low frequencies**, $\omega$ is small → $a$ is tiny even if $d$ is large → use **displacement**.
- At **high frequencies**, $\omega$ is large → $d$ is tiny even if $a$ is large → use **acceleration**.

---

## 2.2 Frequency-to-Parameter Mapping (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1 — Identify Machine Rotational Speed ($f_r$)
Most common machinery faults are harmonics or sub-harmonics of $f_r$. For a 1500 rpm motor:
$$f_r = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Fault Frequencies
- Unbalance: $1\times f_r = 25$ Hz
- Misalignment: $2\times f_r = 50$ Hz (often) or $1\times/2\times$
- Looseness: $1\times, 2\times, 3\times f_r$
- Blade pass: $N \times f_r$ (where $N$ = number of blades)
- Bearing defect: typically 100 Hz – 10 kHz (non-synchronous)
- Gear mesh: $N_g \times f_r$ (100 Hz – 5 kHz)
- Electrical: $2\times$ line frequency (50 Hz in India) and its harmonics

### Step 3 — Match Parameter to Frequency Band
| Frequency Band | Dominant Faults | Recommended Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 10 Hz | Slow-speed unbalance, structural resonance | **Displacement** |
| 10 – 1000 Hz | Unbalance, misalignment, looseness, electrical, blade pass | **Velocity** (ISO 10816 zone) |
| 1000 – 10000 Hz | Bearing defects, gear mesh, cavitation, high-freq impacts | **Acceleration** (or Velocity for VSD) |

### Step 4 — Apply ISO 10816 / 20816 Severity Zones
- **Zone A** (Good): New machine acceptance
- **Zone B** (Acceptable): Long-term operation
- **Zone C** (Unsatisfactory): Plan maintenance
- **Zone D** (Unacceptable): Immediate shutdown

These zones are tabulated in **mm/s RMS velocity**, which is why velocity is the universal "currency" of condition monitoring.

---

## 2.3 Sensor Type Selection (Coupled to Parameter)

| Parameter | Typical Sensor | Frequency Response |
|---|---|---|
| Displacement | Proximity probe (eddy current), seismic transducer | 0 – 200 Hz |
| Velocity | Electromagnetic velocity pickup, geo-phone | 1 – 1000 Hz |
| Acceleration | Piezoelectric accelerometer, MEMS | 0.5 – 20000 Hz |
| High-frequency shocks | Acoustic emission, IEPE accel with HF extension | 1 kHz – 1 MHz |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** When asked *"Which parameter is best for detecting early-stage bearing defects?"* — the answer is **acceleration** (high g, high frequency content from impact). When asked *"Which parameter is mandated by ISO 10816 for severity evaluation?"* — the answer is **velocity RMS in mm/s**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \omega \cdot d$ | Velocity from displacement | mm/s | Parameter conversion |
| 2 | $a = \omega^2 \cdot d$ | Acceleration from displacement | m/s² | Parameter conversion |
| 3 | $a = \omega \cdot v$ | Acceleration from velocity | m/s² | Parameter conversion |
| 4 | $f_r = \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Rotational frequency from rpm | Hz | Fault frequency calc |
| 5 | $f_{bp} = N_b \cdot f_r$ | Blade-pass frequency | Hz | Torsional/fan faults |
| 6 | $f_{gm} = N_g \cdot f_r$ | Gear-mesh frequency | Hz | Gearbox diagnostics |
| 7 | $v_{rms} = \dfrac{v_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS for sine wave | mm/s | ISO severity (sine only) |
| 8 | $v_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ | True RMS (any waveform) | mm/s | ISO severity (general) |
| 9 | $CF = \dfrac{v_{peak}}{v_{rms}}$ | Crest Factor | dimensionless | Bearing impact detection |
| 10 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s | All conversions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute value caution:** In the formulas above, $\vert v \vert$ should be written using `\vert v \vert` in LaTeX if appearing in tables — the vertical pipe `|` character breaks markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside table cells.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plants** — Velocity probes on turbine bearing housings to comply with ISO 10816-3.
- **Paper mills** — Accelerometers on roll bearings where high-frequency fatigue failures are critical.
- **Wind turbines** — Low-frequency displacement on tower top + acceleration on gearbox bearings.
- **Compressors** — Velocity for casing + high-frequency acceleration for vane-pass detection.
- **Pumps & motors** — Standard velocity measurement on each bearing housing in radial and axial directions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Master Conversion Between $d$, $v$, $a$

**Given:** A rotor exhibits simple harmonic vibration $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$, where $X$ is the peak amplitude (m) and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency (rad/s).

### Step 1 — Velocity by differentiation
Differentiate $x(t)$ with respect to time:
$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[X \sin(\omega t)\right]$$

Apply the chain rule (derivative of $\sin(\omega t)$ is $\omega \cos(\omega t)$):
$$v(t) = X \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

So peak velocity is:
$$V = \omega \cdot X$$

### Step 2 — Acceleration by second differentiation
Differentiate $v(t)$ with respect to time:
$$a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[X \omega \cos(\omega t)\right]$$

Apply chain rule (derivative of $\cos(\omega t)$ is $-\omega \sin(\omega t)$):
$$a(t) = -X \cdot \omega \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(\omega t) = -\omega^2 \cdot X \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

So peak acceleration is:
$$A = \omega^2 \cdot X$$

### Step 3 — Cross-relationships (peak values)
Divide $A$ by $V$:
$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{\omega^2 X}{\omega X} = \omega$$

Divide $V$ by $X$:
$$\frac{V}{X} = \omega$$

Therefore:
$$A = \omega \cdot V = \omega^2 \cdot X = \frac{V^2}{X}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion memory trick:** Each time you go up the chain (d → v → a), you **multiply by $\omega$**. Each time you go down (a → v → d), you **divide by $\omega$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Worked Example (Parameter Selection for a Motor-Bearing)

**Problem statement:** A 4-pole induction motor runs at 1450 rpm. Its measured peak displacement at the drive-end bearing housing is $X = 50 \; \mu m$ at $1\times$ running speed. Calculate the equivalent peak velocity, peak acceleration, and RMS velocity. Then identify which ISO 10816 zone the machine is in (assume Class II machinery).

### Step 1 — Compute rotational frequency
$$f_r = \frac{1450}{60} = 24.17 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2 — Compute angular frequency
$$\omega = 2\pi f_r = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 24.17 = 151.84 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3 — Peak velocity
Convert displacement to metres: $X = 50 \times 10^{-6} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m
$$V = \omega \cdot X = 151.84 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5} = 7.592 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

Convert to mm/s:
$$V = 7.592 \text{ mm/s}$$

### Step 4 — Peak acceleration
$$A = \omega^2 \cdot X = (151.84)^2 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5} = 23055.4 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5} = 1.153 \text{ m/s}^2$$

In units of g (1 g = 9.81 m/s²):
$$A_g = \frac{1.153}{9.81} = 0.1175 \text{ g}$$

### Step 5 — RMS velocity
For a pure sine wave:
$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{7.592}{1.4142} = 5.369 \text{ mm/s}$$

### Step 6 — ISO 10816-3 Class II severity evaluation
For Class II machinery (medium-sized machines, 15–75 kW) in the 10–1000 Hz band:
- **Zone A (Good):** up to 1.4 mm/s RMS
- **Zone B (Acceptable):** 1.4 – 2.8 mm/s RMS
- **Zone C (Unsatisfactory):** 2.8 – 4.5 mm/s RMS
- **Zone D (Unacceptable):** above 4.5 mm/s RMS

Our value $V_{rms} = 5.369$ mm/s exceeds 4.5 mm/s → **Zone D — Immediate action required.**

### Step 7 — Parameter selection recommendation
- For severity evaluation per ISO: **velocity (RMS)**
- For early-stage fault detection on this motor: add an **accelerometer** to capture high-frequency bearing tones.
- For structural resonance check: **displacement** probe in low-frequency band.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crest Factor Demonstration (for Bearing Fault Identification)

**Problem:** Vibration velocity signal has $V_{peak} = 14$ mm/s and $V_{rms} = 3.5$ mm/s. Compute the crest factor and interpret.

### Step 1 — Apply crest factor formula
$$CF = \frac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}} = \frac{14}{3.5} = 4.0$$

### Step 2 — Interpretation
| CF Value | Typical Signal Character |
|---|---|
| 1.0 – 1.5 | Pure sine wave (unbalance) |
| 2.0 – 3.0 | Normal machine with mixed tones |
| 3.0 – 5.0 | Bearing impact or gear-tooth defect |
| > 5.0 | Severe spalling, loose parts, severe impacts |

**Result:** CF = 4.0 indicates **probable bearing impact or early-stage gear-mesh fault** — even though the RMS is low. This is why crest factor is a *complementary* parameter to velocity RMS.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Selection Decision Logic

| Machine / Application | Dominant Faults | Frequency Range | Best Parameter | Sensor | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Large steam turbine | Unbalance, misalignment, oil whip | 0.5 – 100 Hz | Displacement (seismic) | Proximity probe | ISO 10816-2 |
| 1500 rpm induction motor | Unbalance, bearing wear, electrical | 10 – 1000 Hz | Velocity | Velox / IEPE accel | ISO 10816-3 |
| High-speed gearbox | Gear mesh, bearing, wear | 100 – 5000 Hz | Acceleration | IEPE accelerometer | ISO 10816-3 / VDI 3832 |
| Paper machine rolls | Bearing fatigue, roll cover defects | 500 – 10000 Hz | Acceleration | High-freq accel | ISO 10816-7 |
| Centrifugal pump | Cavitation, unbalance, impeller | 10 – 2000 Hz | Velocity + high-freq accel | Dual sensor | ISO 10816-3 / API 670 |
| Wind turbine gearbox | Bearing, gear mesh, slow rotor | 0.1 – 10000 Hz | Displacement + Acceleration | Two-channel | ISO 10816-21 |
| Reciprocating compressor | Valve impacts, piston slap, looseness | 1 – 5000 Hz | Acceleration (peak) | IEPE with HF | API 618 / ISO 10816-6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Rule of Thumb (memorize for KTU exam):**
> 1. For **ISO 10816 severity evaluation** → use **velocity (RMS, mm/s)**.
> 2. For **bearing diagnostics** → use **acceleration (g RMS or g peak)** + envelope analysis.
> 3. For **slow-speed rotor / structural** → use **displacement (µm peak-to-peak)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Vibration Parameter Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Machine Type and Speed] --> B{What is the rotational speed?}
    B -->|Below 600 rpm| C[Low-speed machine]
    B -->|600 to 3000 rpm| D[Medium-speed machine]
    B -->|Above 3000 rpm| E[High-speed machine]
    C --> F{Fault family suspected?}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|Unbalance or misalignment| G[Frequency band 1x to 3x fr]
    F -->|Bearing or gear| H[Frequency band above 500 Hz]
    F -->|Structural resonance| I[Frequency band below 10 Hz]
    G --> J[Use VELOCITY parameter in mm/s RMS]
    H --> K[Use ACCELERATION parameter in g]
    I --> L[Use DISPLACEMENT parameter in micrometres]
    J --> M[Apply ISO 10816-3 severity zones]
    K --> N[Apply envelope analysis and kurtosis]
    L --> O[Apply ISO 10816-21 or API 670]
    M --> P[Decide on corrective action]
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vibration Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MACHINE[Rotating Machine with Fault] --> SENSOR[Sensor: Proximity or Accelerometer]
    SENSOR --> COND[Signal Conditioner: Charge Amp or IEPE]
    COND --> FILTER[Anti-Alias Filter and Integrator]
    FILTER --> SELECT[Parameter Selector: d, v, or a]
    SELECT --> ANALYSE[Spectrum Analyser or Data Collector]
    ANALYSE --> CRITERIA[ISO 10816 or 20816 Severity Table]
    CRITERIA --> DECISION[Maintenance Decision Module]
    DECISION -->|Zone A| OK[Continue Operation]
    DECISION -->|Zone B| PLAN[Plan Routine Inspection]
    DECISION -->|Zone C| SCHED[Schedule Corrective Work]
    DECISION -->|Zone D| STOP[Immediate Shutdown]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that the **Parameter Selector** is the *central decision block* where the engineer's choice of $d$, $v$, or $a$ determines which downstream severity criteria are applied. Selecting the wrong parameter at this node will misclassify a faulty machine as healthy — a catastrophic maintenance error.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Frequency Spectrum Showing Fault Signature Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LOW[Low Frequency 1 to 10 Hz]
        A1[Unbalance 1x fr] --> A2[Looseness 1x to 3x fr]
        A2 --> A3[Slow Sleeve Bearing Oil Whip]
    end
    subgraph MID[Mid Frequency 10 to 1000 Hz]
        B1[Misalignment 2x fr] --> B2[Blade Pass Frequency]
        B2 --> B3[Electrical 2x Line Freq]
    end
    subgraph HIGH[High Frequency 1 to 10 kHz]
        C1[Bearing Outer Race Defect] --> C2[Bearing Inner Race Defect]
        C2 --> C3[Gear Mesh Frequency]
        C3 --> C4[Cavitation High Freq Noise]
    end
    LOW --> P1[DISPLACEMENT recommended]
    MID --> P2[VELOCITY recommended ISO band]
    HIGH --> P3[ACCELERATION recommended]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define vibration parameter selection. List the three primary vibration quantities used in condition monitoring.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Vibration parameter selection is the engineering process of choosing the most appropriate vibration measurement quantity, frequency band, and sensor type for assessing the health of a rotating or reciprocating machine (1 mark).

The three primary vibration quantities are:
1. **Displacement** ($d$) — measured in µm; suitable for low-frequency vibrations such as unbalance and rotor bow (1 mark).
2. **Velocity** ($v$) — measured in mm/s RMS; the ISO 10816 standard parameter for severity evaluation (1 mark).
3. **Acceleration** ($a$) — measured in g or m/s²; suitable for high-frequency faults such as bearing defects and gear-mesh problems.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Why is velocity (RMS in mm/s) preferred over peak displacement for ISO 10816 severity evaluation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Energy correlation:** RMS velocity is proportional to the vibration *energy* that causes bearing fatigue and mechanical damage, irrespective of the waveform shape. Peak displacement is misleading for impulsive signals (1 mark).
2. **Waveform independence:** ISO 10816 zones are calibrated in mm/s RMS to remain valid for sine waves, random vibrations, and mixed tones (1 mark).
3. **Standardization:** Universal adoption across industries allows comparison of different machines, classes, and manufacturers. The four zones (A, B, C, D) are defined only in mm/s RMS (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches and a comparison table, explain the three primary vibration parameters — displacement, velocity, and acceleration. Show the mathematical relationships among them for a sinusoidal vibration of the form $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$. State clearly the units, typical frequency band, and one fault each parameter is best suited to detect.

**(b) [7 marks]** A centrifugal pump running at 2980 rpm has a measured peak displacement of $X = 40 \; \mu m$ at the drive-end bearing housing in the radial direction. Calculate:
- (i) Peak velocity in mm/s
- (ii) Peak acceleration in g
- (iii) RMS velocity in mm/s
- (iv) State the ISO 10816-3 Class II zone the machine operates in.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the criteria for selecting vibration parameters. Cover the four key decision factors: (i) rotational speed, (ii) fault frequency, (iii) ISO standard, and (iv) sensor type. Give at least two industry examples for each parameter.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain with a block diagram how crest factor and kurtosis are used as *complementary* parameters to velocity RMS for early detection of bearing defects. A vibration signal has $V_{peak} = 12$ mm/s and $V_{rms} = 2.5$ mm/s. Compute crest factor and interpret the result. Mention the threshold values of CF for normal vs. faulty machines.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
For $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$:

$$v(t) = \omega X \cos(\omega t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \omega X$$

$$a(t) = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \omega^2 X = \omega V$$

| Parameter | Units | Frequency Band | Typical Sensor | Best for Detecting |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement | µm (peak-to-peak) | 0 – 200 Hz | Proximity probe | Unbalance, shaft bow, oil whip |
| Velocity | mm/s (RMS) | 10 – 1000 Hz | Velox / IEPE | Severity per ISO, misalignment, electrical |
| Acceleration | g (RMS or peak) | 1 kHz – 10 kHz | IEPE accelerometer | Bearing defects, gear mesh, cavitation |

**Mark split (1 mark each):** definition, formula derivation, units, frequency band, sensor, fault type, comparison remark.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Given:** $N = 2980$ rpm, $X_{peak} = 40 \; \mu m = 40 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

**Step 1 — Rotational frequency:**
$$f_r = \frac{2980}{60} = 49.67 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Angular frequency:**
$$\omega = 2\pi f_r = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 49.67 = 312.07 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Peak velocity:**
$$V_{peak} = \omega X = 312.07 \times 40 \times 10^{-6} = 12.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 12.48 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4 — Peak acceleration:**
$$A_{peak} = \omega^2 X = (312.07)^2 \times 40 \times 10^{-6} = 97387.7 \times 40 \times 10^{-6} = 3.896 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Convert to g:
$$A_g = \frac{3.896}{9.81} = 0.397 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — RMS velocity (sine assumption):**
$$V_{rms} = \frac{12.48}{\sqrt{2}} = 8.83 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 6 — ISO 10816-3 Class II zone check:**
- Zone A: < 1.4 mm/s
- Zone B: 1.4 – 2.8 mm/s
- Zone C: 2.8 – 4.5 mm/s
- Zone D: > 4.5 mm/s

Result: $V_{rms} = 8.83$ mm/s is in **Zone D — Unacceptable — immediate shutdown recommended.**

**Mark split (1.5 marks each + 0.5 for zone):** rpm-to-Hz, ω, V, A, A in g, V_rms, zone identification.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Selection criteria — four pillars:**

1. **Rotational speed:** Determines the fundamental frequency $f_r$ and the location of fault harmonics on the spectrum. Slow rotors (< 600 rpm) require displacement; high-speed rotors benefit from velocity and acceleration.
2. **Fault frequency:** Bearing tones, gear-mesh tones, and electrical harmonics can be 100× to 1000× $f_r$ — necessitating high-frequency accelerometers.
3. **ISO standard compliance:** ISO 10816-3 mandates velocity RMS; ISO 20816-21 (offshore wind) uses displacement for slow rotors; API 670 uses velocity + axial position.
4. **Sensor type:** Proximity probes for displacement (non-contact, hydrostatic bearings); IEPE accelerometers for acceleration; seismic geophones for low-frequency velocity.

**Industry examples (2 each):**
- *Displacement:* Hydro generators (large slow rotors), turbo-machinery shaft vibration.
- *Velocity:* Standard induction motors, pumps, fans — most ISO 10816-3 applications.
- *Acceleration:* Gearbox HFF detection, bearing fault monitoring, cavitation.

**Mark split:** 1.5 for each of four criteria + 1 for examples.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Block diagram (text form):**
Vibration Signal → Velocity RMS (energy) + Peak detection → Crest Factor = Peak/RMS → Trend & Alarm

Crest Factor (CF) and Kurtosis are statistical parameters that detect *impulsiveness* in the signal, which RMS alone cannot detect (1 mark). When a bearing defect begins, the RMS may still be within Zone B, but the peak values rise sharply, increasing the CF (1 mark).

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**
$$CF = \frac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}} = \frac{12}{2.5} = 4.8$$

**Interpretation table (1 mark):**
| CF | Indication |
|---|---|
| 1 – 1.5 | Pure sinusoid (unbalance) |
| 1.5 – 3 | Normal multi-tone |
| 3 – 5 | Bearing impact or gear defect |
| > 5 | Severe spalling or loose parts |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** CF = 4.8 → **probable bearing impact or gear-mesh fault** — even though the RMS is low, this machine needs further investigation (envelope analysis recommended).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert µm to metres** in numerical problems. Writing $V = \omega X$ with $X = 40$ (without $10^{-6}$) gives an answer off by a factor of $10^6$. Always state unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing peak with peak-to-peak.** ISO standards use **peak-to-peak** for displacement but **peak** for acceleration. Velocity is always **RMS** in ISO 10816.
> 3. **Using peak instead of RMS in severity zone tables** — gives a value that appears in a worse zone, leading to wrong recommendation.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of pure sine wave** when dividing peak by $\sqrt{2}$. For non-sinusoidal (bearing-impact) signals, true RMS must be computed via integration.
> 5. **Writing `|v|` with vertical pipe in markdown tables** — the pipe breaks the table. Use `\vert v \vert` in LaTeX instead.
> 6. **Mixing up ISO 10816 (general) and ISO 20816 (replacement).** ISO 10816 is the older standard; ISO 20816 (2016 onwards) is the new unified version — examiners may test this distinction.
> 7. **Omitting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Vibration Parameter Selection** = choosing the right measurement quantity, frequency band, sensor, and severity standard for a machine.
- [x] **Three primary quantities:** Displacement (µm, low freq), Velocity (mm/s RMS, mid freq), Acceleration (g, high freq).
- [x] **Master conversion formulas:** $V = \omega X$, $A = \omega^2 X = \omega V$, where $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- [x] **Rotational frequency:** $f_r = N/60$ Hz.
- [x] **Fault frequency shortcuts:** Unbalance = 1×$f_r$; Misalignment = 2×$f_r$; BPF = $N_b f_r$; GMF = $N_g f_r$; Bearing = 100 Hz–10 kHz; Electrical = 2× line frequency.
- [x] **ISO 10816-3 Class II zones** (mm/s RMS): A < 1.4, B = 1.4–2.8, C = 2.8–4.5, D > 4.5.
- [x] **RMS conversion for sine:** $V_{rms} = V_{peak}/\sqrt{2}$.
- [x] **Crest Factor thresholds:** 1–1.5 (sine), 1.5–3 (normal), 3–5 (bearing), > 5 (severe impact).
- [x] **Decision rule:** ISO severity → velocity; bearing/gear → acceleration; slow rotor/structural → displacement.
- [x] **Sensor coupling:** Proximity probe → displacement; Velox/IEPE → velocity or acceleration; AE → high-frequency shock.
- [x] **Standards family:** ISO 10816-3 (rigid, 15–300 kW); ISO 10816-4 (flexible foundation); ISO 20816-21 (offshore wind, non-orbiting); API 670 (turbomachinery).
- [x] **Always state the assumption of sinusoidal waveform** when using $V_{peak}/\sqrt{2}$ for RMS.
- [x] **Always carry units in every line of numerical solutions** to avoid valuation deductions.
- [x] **Universal motto for KTU exam:** *One parameter is never enough — RMS, Peak, Crest Factor, Kurtosis together give the full picture.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Takeaway:** Vibration Parameter Selection is not about picking the "best" parameter — it is about matching the *frequency content of the fault* with the *frequency response of the parameter and sensor*. Master the conversion formulas, memorize the ISO 10816 zones, and you will secure full marks on any related question in the KTU 2024 scheme examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equipment Classification** in Condition Monitoring and Maintenance Management refers to the systematic categorization of industrial machinery, components, and systems into distinct groups based on predefined criteria such as **criticality, failure consequences, operational function, maintainability, and risk exposure**. This classification forms the foundational pillar for deciding the *type*, *frequency*, and *depth* of maintenance interventions.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, equipment classification is the *first logical step* in any Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) program. Without a structured classification, condition monitoring efforts become unfocused, uneconomical, and inefficient.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Criticality** | The relative importance of an equipment item to operations, safety, and profitability |
| **Availability** | The fraction of time the equipment is in a functioning state ($A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$) |
| **Risk Exposure** | Combined measure of failure probability and consequence severity |
| **Maintainability Index** | A score representing ease of restoration after failure |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a Hospital Emergency System:**
> 
> When patients arrive at a hospital, the triage nurse immediately classifies them as **Critical (Red Tag)**, **Urgent (Yellow Tag)**, or **Non-Urgent (Green Tag)**. A heart-attack patient gets a cardiologist *immediately*, a fracture patient gets an orthopedic consultation *within hours*, and a mild fever patient waits *in queue*.
> 
> **The exact same logic applies to industrial equipment.** A turbine in a power plant (critical) gets continuous vibration monitoring, online oil analysis, and thermography *daily*. A workshop bench grinder (non-critical) gets only routine visual inspection *monthly*.
> 
> **Equipment Classification = The "Industrial Triage System."**

### Why Classification Matters

1. **Resource Optimization** — Maintenance budgets are always limited. Classification directs resources to where they matter most.
2. **Risk Mitigation** — Identifies equipment whose failure could cause *safety hazards, environmental disasters, or catastrophic financial loss*.
3. **Strategy Selection** — Different classes demand different maintenance philosophies (PM, PdM, CBM, Run-to-Failure).
4. **Spare Parts Management** — Critical equipment requires stocked spares; non-critical equipment can wait for procurement.
5. **Audit & Compliance** — ISO 55000, OSHA, and API standards require documented classification.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard engineering metrics govern equipment classification in KTU-aligned industry practice:

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — Average operational time between consecutive failures *(units: hours)*
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** — Average time required to restore equipment *(units: hours)*
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** — Composite metric combining availability, performance, and quality *(dimensionless, expressed as %)*
- **Production Loss Cost** — Financial impact per hour of downtime *(units: ₹/hour or $/hour)*
- **Safety Hazard Level** — Categorical: Low / Medium / High / Catastrophic

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Criteria — Structured Logic

Equipment in any process plant can be classified along **multiple parallel dimensions**. The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the following five primary dimensions:

### Dimension 1: Functional Classification
- **Production-Critical Equipment** — Directly contributes to primary output (e.g., main reactor, compressor in a refinery)
- **Auxiliary Equipment** — Supports production but not directly output-generating (e.g., cooling pumps, HVAC)
- **Service Equipment** — Provides utilities (e.g., boilers, compressors for instrument air)
- **Standby / Redundant Equipment** — Operates only when primary fails (e.g., emergency diesel generators)

### Dimension 2: Criticality-Based Classification (Most Widely Used)
- **Class A — Critical** (Failure = Shutdown, Safety risk, High loss)
- **Class B — Important** (Failure = Production slowdown, Medium loss)
- **Class C — General** (Failure = Localized inconvenience, Low loss)
- **Class D — Non-Essential** (Failure = Negligible impact)

### Dimension 3: Failure Mode Classification
- **Catastrophic Failures** — Sudden, total loss of function (e.g., shaft fracture)
- **Degradation Failures** — Gradual performance loss (e.g., bearing wear)
- **Intermittent Failures** — Random, non-reproducible (e.g., loose wiring)

### Dimension 4: Maintenance Strategy Classification
- **PM-Applicable** — Time-based or usage-based servicing
- **PdM-Applicable** — Predictive monitoring feasible (vibration, thermography, oil analysis)
- **Run-to-Failure (RTF)** — No proactive maintenance (low-cost, non-critical items)

### Dimension 5: Lifecycle Stage Classification
- **Infant Mortality Stage** — Early-life failures (manufacturing defects)
- **Useful Life Stage** — Random failures, constant failure rate
- **Wear-Out Stage** — Increasing failure rate, end-of-life

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all key formulas for equipment classification and criticality analysis. Master these for KTU ESE examinations.

| **Formula** | **Equation** | **Application** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Measures uptime reliability | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **Criticality Index (CI)** | $CI = F_{r} \times C_{s}$ | Quantifies equipment criticality | Score |
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | FMEA-based ranking | Score |
| **Failure Rate** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Poisson failure modeling | Failures/hour |
| **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of survival to time t | Dimensionless |
| **OEE** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall equipment effectiveness | % |
| **Criticality Number** | $C_{n} = \sum (\beta \times \lambda_{p} \times t)$ | Quantified criticality ranking | failures/year |

Where:
- $F_r$ = Failure frequency rating (1–10)
- $C_s$ = Consequence severity rating (1–10)
- $S$ = Severity score, $O$ = Occurrence score, $D$ = Detection score
- $\beta$ = Failure mode fraction, $\lambda_p$ = Failure rate of part, $t$ = Operating time
- $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality

---

## 2.3 The Criticality Matrix — Visual Logic

The **Criticality Matrix** is the standard KTU-recommended tool for visually classifying equipment. It is a 3×3 or 5×5 grid plotting **Failure Probability** (Y-axis) against **Consequence Severity** (X-axis).

| | **Low Severity** | **Medium Severity** | **High Severity** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Probability** | Class B (Important) | Class A (Critical) | Class A (Critical) |
| **Medium Probability** | Class C (General) | Class B (Important) | Class A (Critical) |
| **Low Probability** | Class D (Non-Essential) | Class C (General) | Class B (Important) |

### Real-World Utility

- **Power Plants** — Turbines classified as Class A drive continuous online vibration monitoring investments worth crores.
- **Aerospace** — Criticality classification directly informs design certification under DO-178C / EASA standards.
- **Manufacturing** — TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) pillars are built on equipment classification (Pillar 1: Autonomous Maintenance targets Class C/D; Pillar 3: Focused Improvement targets Class A/B).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Criticality Index from First Principles

The **Criticality Index (CI)** is the most fundamental quantitative metric in equipment classification. We derive it step-by-step from its conceptual basis.

### Step 1: Identify the Two Underlying Variables

Criticality fundamentally depends on two independent variables:
1. **How often does the equipment fail?** → Represented by failure frequency $F_r$
2. **What happens when it fails?** → Represented by consequence severity $C_s$

### Step 2: Define Each Variable on a Standardized Scale

For engineering tractability, both variables are normalized to a 1-to-10 integer scale (industry standard, per ISO 14224).

$$F_r \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10\}$$

$$C_s \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10\}$$

Where:
- $F_r = 1$ means "failures are extremely rare (less than 1 in 10 years)"
- $F_r = 10$ means "failures are very frequent (more than once per month)"
- $C_s = 1$ means "negligible consequence (no production, safety, or environmental impact)"
- $C_s = 10$ means "catastrophic consequence (fatality, multi-crore loss, environmental disaster)"

### Step 3: Combine into a Single Composite Index

Since criticality must increase when *either* factor increases, the simplest multiplicative combination is:

$$\begin{aligned}
CI &= F_r \times C_s \\
CI_{min} &= 1 \times 1 = 1 \\
CI_{max} &= 10 \times 10 = 100
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Map CI to Classification Tiers

Based on industry consensus (and KTU-aligned practical ranges):

$$\begin{aligned}
CI \in [1, 20] &\Rightarrow \text{Class D — Non-Essential} \\
CI \in [21, 45] &\Rightarrow \text{Class C — General} \\
CI \in [46, 75] &\Rightarrow \text{Class B — Important} \\
CI \in [76, 100] &\Rightarrow \text{Class A — Critical}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump in a petroleum refinery has experienced **3 failures in the last 12 months**. Each failure causes a production loss of approximately **₹8 lakhs** and poses a **moderate environmental risk** (oil spill containment required). Classify the equipment using the CI method.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Determine Failure Frequency Rating ($F_r$)**

The pump failed 3 times in 12 months, i.e., roughly once every 4 months. Referring to the standard rating table:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Failure rate} &= 3 \text{ failures / 12 months} = 0.25 \text{ failures/month} \\
&= 3 \text{ failures/year}
\end{aligned}$$

Per ISO 14224 conventions, 3 failures/year corresponds to a rating of **$F_r = 7$**.

**Step 2: Determine Consequence Severity Rating ($C_s$)**

Evaluating the consequence dimensions:
- **Production Loss**: ₹8 lakhs/event → Significant (rating component: 6)
- **Safety Risk**: Minor injury possible → Low-Medium (rating component: 4)
- **Environmental Risk**: Moderate spill (rating component: 7)
- **Repair Cost**: Moderate (rating component: 5)

The dominant sub-component is environmental (rating 7), so we adopt:

$$C_s = 7$$

**Step 3: Compute Criticality Index**

$$\begin{aligned}
CI &= F_r \times C_s \\
CI &= 7 \times 7 \\
CI &= 49
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Classify**

Since $CI = 49$ falls in the range $[46, 75]$:

$$\boxed{\text{Class B — Important Equipment}}$$

### Recommended Maintenance Strategy for Class B Equipment

| Maintenance Activity | Frequency | Method |
|---|---|---|
| Vibration Analysis | Monthly | Portable analyzer |
| Oil Analysis | Quarterly | Spectrometric + ferrography |
| Visual Inspection | Weekly | Operator rounds |
| Overhaul | Annually | Scheduled shutdown |

---

## 3.3 Criticality Matrix Construction — Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Gather Historical Failure Data

Collect MTBF data for each equipment item from CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System) logs.

### Step 2: Estimate Consequence Cost

Sum up:
- Direct production loss
- Repair labor and materials
- Safety incident cost (insurance, regulatory)
- Environmental cleanup cost

### Step 3: Plot on Matrix

Plot each item on a 2D plane: Failure Probability (Y-axis) vs Consequence Severity (X-axis).

### Step 4: Apply Zone Boundaries

Define zone boundaries (typically 33rd and 67th percentiles) to assign classes A, B, C, D.

### Step 5: Validate with Expert Judgment (Delphi Method)

Have 3–5 senior engineers independently rank the top 10 critical items. Compare with matrix output. Resolve discrepancies via discussion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Classification Method | Quantitative? | KTU Recommended? | Industry Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Criticality Matrix | Semi-quantitative | **Yes** | Yes |
| FMEA-Based RPN | Quantitative | **Yes** | Yes |
| Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) | Quantitative | Yes | API 580/581 |
| Delphi Expert Judgment | Qualitative | Supplementary | Yes |
| Pareto Analysis (80/20) | Quantitative | Yes | Yes |
| AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) | Quantitative | Advanced level | Limited |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Equipment Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Plant Asset Inventory] --> B{Apply Classification Filter}

```

## 4.2 Criticality Decision Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Equipment Item Under Review] --> T{Collect Failure Data}
    T --> U{Failure Rate Above Threshold}
    U -->|Yes| V{Consequence Severity High}
    U -->|No| W{Consequence Severity High}
    V -->|Yes| X[Class A Critical]
    V -->|No| Y[Class B Important]
    W -->|Yes| Y
    W -->|No| Z{Consequence Severity Medium}
    Z -->|Yes| AA[Class C General]
    Z -->|No| AB[Class D Non Essential]

```
