# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define equipment classification. Why is it considered the foundational step in any maintenance management program?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Equipment classification is the systematic categorization of industrial assets based on criteria such as criticality, failure consequences, operational function, and maintainability. **(1 mark)**

It is foundational because it enables: **(2 marks)**
1. **Resource Optimization** — Directing limited maintenance budget to highest-impact equipment
2. **Strategy Selection** — Choosing appropriate maintenance philosophy (PM, PdM, RTF) for each class
3. **Risk Mitigation** — Prioritizing equipment whose failure poses safety or environmental hazards
4. **Spare Parts Planning** — Stocking decisions aligned with criticality
5. **Audit Compliance** — Documented evidence for ISO 55000 and regulatory audits

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between Class A and Class D equipment with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Class A (Critical) | Class D (Non-Essential) |
|---|---|---|
| **Failure Impact** | Shutdown, safety risk, high loss | Negligible impact |
| **Maintenance Strategy** | Continuous PdM + PM | Run-to-failure |
| **Spare Stocking** | Dedicated stock maintained | No stocking |
| **Example 1** | Main turbine in power plant | Workshop bench grinder |
| **Example 2** | Primary reactor in petrochemical unit | Office air conditioner |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition difference, 1 for strategy difference, 1 for examples)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE full question from each module. Each question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark question options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: A process plant contains the following five equipment items. Perform a complete criticality-based classification using the Criticality Index method.**

| Equipment | Failures/Year | Production Loss/Event (₹) | Safety Risk | Environmental Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal Compressor | 2 | 15,00,000 | High | Medium |
| Cooling Water Pump | 6 | 80,000 | Low | Low |
| Heat Exchanger | 1 | 5,00,000 | Low | Low |
| Distillation Column | 0.5 | 50,00,000 | High | High |
| Workshop Lathe | 0.2 | 5,000 | Negligible | Negligible |

**Use the rating table: Failure rate 0–1/yr → Fr=2; 1–3/yr → Fr=5; 3–6/yr → Fr=7; >6/yr → Fr=9. Severity rating by dominant consequence: Low=3, Medium=6, High=9.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compute CI for all five items and classify into A/B/C/D.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Assign Failure Frequency Ratings**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressor: } 2/\text{yr} &\Rightarrow F_r = 5 \\
\text{Cooling Pump: } 6/\text{yr} &\Rightarrow F_r = 7 \\
\text{Heat Exchanger: } 1/\text{yr} &\Rightarrow F_r = 2 \\
\text{Distillation Column: } 0.5/\text{yr} &\Rightarrow F_r = 2 \\
\text{Lathe: } 0.2/\text{yr} &\Rightarrow F_r = 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Assign Consequence Severity Ratings (based on dominant risk)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressor: High safety} &\Rightarrow C_s = 9 \\
\text{Cooling Pump: Low both} &\Rightarrow C_s = 3 \\
\text{Heat Exchanger: Low both} &\Rightarrow C_s = 3 \\
\text{Distillation Column: High both} &\Rightarrow C_s = 9 \\
\text{Lathe: Negligible both} &\Rightarrow C_s = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute Criticality Index**

$$\begin{aligned}
CI_{compressor} &= 5 \times 9 = 45 \\
CI_{cooling pump} &= 7 \times 3 = 21 \\
CI_{heat exchanger} &= 2 \times 3 = 6 \\
CI_{distillation column} &= 2 \times 9 = 18 \\
CI_{lathe} &= 2 \times 1 = 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Classify using tier boundaries [1–20]=D, [21–45]=C, [46–75]=B, [76–100]=A**

| Equipment | CI | Class |
|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal Compressor | 45 | Class C (borderline B) |
| Cooling Water Pump | 21 | Class C |
| Heat Exchanger | 6 | Class D |
| Distillation Column | 18 | Class D (borderline C) |
| Workshop Lathe | 2 | Class D |

**(7 marks — 2 for ratings, 2 for computation, 2 for classification, 1 for tabulation)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Recommend suitable maintenance strategies for each classified item and justify.

**Model Solution:**

**(7 marks distributed as: 1 mark per equipment + integrated strategy justification)**

| Equipment | Class | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal Compressor | C (borderline B) | Monthly vibration + quarterly oil analysis + semi-annual overhaul | High consequence (₹15L/event) warrants predictive monitoring |
| Cooling Water Pump | C | Quarterly vibration check + annual seal replacement | High failure rate (6/yr) but low consequence justifies PM focus |
| Heat Exchanger | D | Annual visual + cleaning during shutdown | Low CI, degradation predictable, no need for PdM |
| Distillation Column | D (borderline C) | Continuous pressure/temperature monitoring + annual NDT inspection | Very low failure rate; high consequence but rare event |
| Workshop Lathe | D | Reactive (run-to-failure) | Negligible CI, no proactive intervention warranted |

**Strategic Justification (1 mark):** Resources should be concentrated on the Compressor and Cooling Pump (combined failures = 8/year), as these consume the most maintenance hours. The Distillation Column, despite high consequence, fails rarely (0.5/year) and thus does not warrant continuous monitoring.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Discuss the various dimensions along which industrial equipment can be classified. Explain the Criticality Matrix method with a suitable diagram and describe how it differs from the FMEA-based RPN method.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the five classification dimensions with examples.

**Model Solution:**

The five standard dimensions of equipment classification are:

**1. Functional Classification (1.5 marks)**
Categorizes equipment by its role in the production system:
- **Production Equipment**: Generates the primary product (e.g., main reactor, compressor)
- **Auxiliary Equipment**: Supports production (e.g., feedwater pump, condenser)
- **Service Equipment**: Provides utilities (e.g., boiler, instrument air compressor)
- **Standby Equipment**: Activates only during primary failure (e.g., emergency DG set)

**2. Criticality Classification (1.5 marks)**
Based on failure consequence severity:
- **Class A**: Failure causes plant shutdown, safety incident, or loss > ₹10L/event
- **Class B**: Failure causes significant production loss (₹1L–₹10L)
- **Class C**: Failure causes moderate loss (< ₹1L)
- **Class D**: Failure has negligible impact

**3. Failure Mode Classification (1 mark)**
- **Catastrophic**: Sudden, total loss (shaft fracture)
- **Degradation**: Gradual decline (bearing wear)
- **Intermittent**: Random occurrences (loose electrical connection)

**4. Maintenance Strategy Classification (1.5 marks)**
- **PM-Suitable**: Time/usage-based servicing feasible
- **PdM-Suitable**: Continuous condition monitoring viable
- **RTF-Acceptable**: Low cost, non-critical, no proactive maintenance

**5. Lifecycle Stage Classification (1.5 marks)**
- **Infant Mortality**: Early-life failures from manufacturing defects
- **Useful Life**: Constant random failure rate
- **Wear-Out**: Increasing failure rate approaching end-of-life

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Draw the Criticality Matrix, explain its logic, and compare with FMEA-based RPN.

**Model Solution:**

**Criticality Matrix Diagram (3 marks):**

| | **Low Severity** | **Medium Severity** | **High Severity** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Probability** | Class B | **Class A** | **Class A** |
| **Medium Probability** | Class C | Class B | **Class A** |
| **Low Probability** | Class D | Class C | Class B |

**Logic (2 marks):**
The matrix is a 2D grid where:
- **X-axis**: Consequence Severity (low → high)
- **Y-axis**: Failure Probability (low → high)
- Each cell is pre-assigned a class based on combined risk
- Equipment is plotted and class assigned by zone

**Comparison with FMEA-based RPN (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Criticality Matrix | FMEA-RPN |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Failure probability × consequence | Severity × Occurrence × Detection |
| **Granularity** | Coarse (3–4 classes) | Fine-grained (numeric score) |
| **Detection factor** | Not included | Explicitly included |
| **Application stage** | Initial screening | Detailed failure mode analysis |
| **Output** | Class A/B/C/D | Numeric RPN value (1–1000) |

The Criticality Matrix is a *high-level screening tool*, while FMEA-RPN is a *detailed engineering analysis* performed after initial classification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTR** — MTBF = time between failures (reliability metric); MTTR = time to repair (maintainability metric). Mixing them up loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Formulas** — Writing $CI = F_r \times C_s$ without mentioning the rating scale (1–10) loses 0.5 marks.
> 
> 3. **Not Drawing the Criticality Matrix** — In Part B 14-mark questions, examiners *expect* a diagram. Skipping it loses at least 2–3 marks.
> 
> 4. **One-Dimensional Classification** — Students often classify only by criticality. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects FIVE dimensions. Missing 3–4 dimensions = 4–5 marks lost.
> 
> 5. **No Justification for Strategy Assignment** — Simply stating "use PdM" without explaining WHY (based on CI value) loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 6. **Misapplying Pareto Rule** — 80/20 rule applies to failure frequency vs. downtime, NOT to all equipment uniformly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Equipment Classification:**
> 
> - Equipment classification is the **first step** in maintenance management — without it, no effective CM program is possible.
> - **Five dimensions** must be considered: Functional, Criticality, Failure Mode, Maintenance Strategy, Lifecycle Stage.
> - **Criticality Index** = $CI = F_r \times C_s$, scaled 1–100.
> - **Class boundaries**: A (76–100), B (46–75), C (21–45), D (1–20).
> - **Criticality Matrix** is a 2D plot of Failure Probability vs Consequence Severity.
> - **FMEA-RPN** = $S \times O \times D$, includes detection factor (unlike CI).
> - **Class A** = PdM + continuous monitoring; **Class D** = Run-to-failure acceptable.
> - **ISO 14224** governs failure data collection standards.
> - **API 580/581** governs Risk-Based Inspection in process industries.
> - The **Pareto Principle** (80/20) often applies — 20% of equipment causes 80% of failures.
> - Always **justify** strategy assignment using CI or RPN values — never state strategies without numerical basis.
> - **MTBF** (reliability) ≠ **MTTR** (maintainability); remember the distinction.
> - **Availability** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ is the foundational uptime metric.
> - For KTU 14-mark questions, **always draw a diagram** — Criticality Matrix is the most expected visual.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration Severity Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibration Severity Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration Severity Charts** are standardized graphical evaluation tools (defined primarily by the **ISO 10816 / ISO 20816** series of standards) used to classify the vibration amplitude of rotating machinery into discrete operational zones based on broadband RMS velocity measurements. They enable engineers to make rapid, quantitative decisions about machine health, acceptability of operation, and the urgency of corrective maintenance actions.

In KTU parlance, the Vibration Severity Chart is essentially a **decision-making graph** that maps the measured **RMS vibration velocity (in mm/s)** on the Y-axis against the **rotational/machine running speed (in RPM)** on the X-axis. The graph is divided into **four horizontal bands** (Zones A, B, C, D) representing progressively worsening vibration health.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Vitals Chart"

Imagine a doctor examining a patient. Instead of just listening to complaints, the doctor measures **vital signs**: body temperature, heart rate, blood pressure. Each vital has a chart with bands:
- **Green Zone** → Healthy
- **Yellow Zone** → Monitor closely
- **Orange Zone** → Take medication, restrict activity
- **Red Zone** → Emergency hospitalization

A **Vibration Severity Chart works exactly the same way for a machine**:
- The "vital sign" being measured is the **vibration velocity** (instead of heart rate).
- The "patient" is your **pump, motor, compressor, or turbine** (instead of a human).
- The four zones (A, B, C, D) tell the maintenance engineer: *Is this machine healthy, or does it need immediate attention?*

### 1.3 The Four Standard Zones (ISO 10816-3 / ISO 20816-3)

> [!NOTE]
> **Zone A** — Newly commissioned machines. Vibration is low and acceptable for unrestricted long-term operation.
> 
> **Zone B** — Acceptable for unrestricted long-term operation. The machine is considered fit for service, but maintenance planning should commence.
> 
> **Zone C** — Unsatisfactory for long-term continuous operation. The machine may run for a limited period until a convenient repair window is scheduled.
> 
> **Zone D** — Vibration severity is sufficient to cause **damage to the machine**. Immediate corrective action (shutdown, repair, balancing) is required.

### 1.4 Key Physical Units and Measurands (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Vibration Velocity (RMS, mm/s)** — the **primary universal measurement parameter** for mid-frequency vibration severity (10 Hz – 1000 Hz).
- **Vibration Displacement (µm, peak-to-peak)** — used for low-frequency vibration (below 10 Hz) such as in slow-speed reciprocating machines.
- **Vibration Acceleration (g, RMS)** — used for high-frequency vibration (above 1000 Hz) typically associated with bearing defects and gear mesh faults.
- **Frequency (Hz) / Rotational Speed (RPM)** — used to identify the source of vibration peaks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Use Velocity as the Primary Parameter?

The choice of **RMS velocity** as the primary severity measurand is rooted in vibration physics:

1. **Velocity is proportional to vibrational energy** for sinusoidal motion, hence it correlates well with mechanical fatigue and dynamic stress.
2. It is **frequency-independent across the mid-range** (10–1000 Hz), allowing a single chart to evaluate machines with vastly different operating speeds (e.g., a 600 RPM compressor vs. a 15,000 RPM turbine).
3. It is **directly measurable** by most eddy-current, accelerometric, and laser-Doppler transducers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At very low frequencies, displacement is used because velocity signals become too weak (velocity falls off as ω decreases). At very high frequencies, acceleration is used because bearing/gear impacts generate large high-frequency energy bursts.

### 2.2 Machine Classification (ISO 10816-3 / ISO 20816-3)

Different machine types have different vibration tolerances based on their **mass, stiffness of foundation, and power rating**:

| Class | Description | Typical Examples | Power Range |
|:-----:|:-----------|:-----------------|:-----------:|
| **Class I** | Small, light machines | Small electric motors, pumps | Up to **15 kW** |
| **Class II** | Medium-sized machines | Standard motors, pumps, generators | **15 – 75 kW** |
| **Class III** | Large, rigid-mounted | Industrial turbines, compressors on rigid foundations | Above **75 kW** |
| **Class IV** | Large, soft-mounted | Turbo-generators, large turbines on soft foundations | Above **75 kW** |

### 2.3 Zone Boundaries for RMS Velocity (Broadband, 10–1000 Hz)

| Zone | Class I (mm/s) | Class II (mm/s) | Class III (mm/s) | Class IV (mm/s) | Operational Meaning |
|:----:|:--------------:|:---------------:|:----------------:|:---------------:|:-------------------|
| **A** | 0 – 0.71 | 0 – 1.40 | 0 – 1.80 | 0 – 2.80 | Excellent — newly commissioned |
| **B** | 0.71 – 1.80 | 1.40 – 2.80 | 1.80 – 3.50 | 2.80 – 4.50 | Good — long-term OK |
| **C** | 1.80 – 4.50 | 2.80 – 7.10 | 3.50 – 8.50 | 4.50 – 11.20 | Unsatisfactory — short-term only |
| **D** | Above 4.50 | Above 7.10 | Above 8.50 | Above 11.20 | Damage likely — shutdown |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|:-:|:------------------|:--------------|
| 1 | $v(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | Velocity is time-derivative of displacement |
| 2 | $a(t) = \dfrac{dv(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2}$ | Acceleration is time-derivative of velocity |
| 3 | $X_{peak} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\omega} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{2\pi f}$ | Displacement to peak-velocity conversion |
| 4 | $A_{peak} = \omega \cdot V_{peak} = 2\pi f \cdot V_{peak}$ | Peak-acceleration from peak-velocity |
| 5 | $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS value of sinusoidal signal |
| 6 | $X_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_{RMS}}{2\pi f}$ | RMS displacement from RMS velocity |
| 7 | $A_{RMS} = (2\pi f) \cdot V_{RMS}$ | RMS acceleration from RMS velocity |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Rotational frequency (Hz) from RPM |
| 9 | $V_{crit} \propto \sqrt{4 \cdot d \cdot f^3 \cdot L}$ | Bearing life vibration criterion (extended check) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on absolute value bars in formulas:** Use `\vert x \vert` (LaTeX) to represent the absolute value of $x$, never the markdown pipe character, which would break the table formatting.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

1. **Predictive Maintenance** — periodic trending of RMS velocity values to predict impending failure.
2. **Acceptance Testing** — newly installed machines are checked against Zone A limits during commissioning.
3. **Insurance & Regulatory Compliance** — ISO 10816 limits are often cited in API 670, ISO 7919 (axial), and ISO 10816 (broadband) specifications.
4. **Fault Diagnosis Triage** — combined with frequency analysis, the zone tells *how serious* the issue is.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Displacement to Velocity to Acceleration

For a **simple harmonic vibration** with displacement signal $x(t)$:

$$
x(t) = X \sin(\omega t + \phi)
$$

where $X$ is the peak amplitude (in metres), $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency (in rad/s), and $\phi$ is the phase angle.

**Step 1:** Differentiate once with respect to time to obtain velocity.

$$
v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = X \omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)
$$

The peak velocity is therefore $V = X \omega$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate again to obtain acceleration.

$$
a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = -X \omega^2 \sin(\omega t + \phi)
$$

The peak acceleration is therefore $A = X \omega^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of derivation:** Velocity scales linearly with frequency ($\propto \omega$), and acceleration scales with the square of frequency ($\propto \omega^2$). This is precisely why **velocity is the most uniform measurement** across the 10–1000 Hz band — at low $f$, velocity falls off (use displacement), and at high $f$, velocity saturates (use acceleration).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Converting Between Measurands

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A motor running at $N = 3000 \text{ RPM}$ shows a measured RMS velocity of $V_{RMS} = 4.5 \text{ mm/s}$. Calculate the corresponding RMS displacement and RMS acceleration.

**Step 1: Convert RPM to Hz.**

$$
f = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the conversion formula for RMS displacement.**

$$
X_{RMS} = \frac{V_{RMS}}{2\pi f}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
X_{RMS} = \frac{4.5}{2 \pi \cdot 50} = \frac{4.5}{314.159}
$$

$$
X_{RMS} = 0.01432 \text{ mm} = 14.32 \text{ \mu m}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the conversion formula for RMS acceleration.**

$$
A_{RMS} = (2 \pi f) \cdot V_{RMS}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
A_{RMS} = (2 \pi \cdot 50) \cdot 4.5 = 314.159 \cdot 4.5
$$

$$
A_{RMS} = 1413.7 \text{ mm/s}^2
$$

Convert to g-units (where $1 g = 9810 \text{ mm/s}^2$):

$$
A_{RMS} = \frac{1413.7}{9810} = 0.144 g
$$

**Step 4: Identify the operating zone for a Class II machine.**

From the zone table in Section 2.3, $V_{RMS} = 4.5 \text{ mm/s}$ lies between 2.80 and 7.10 mm/s for Class II → **Zone C (Unsatisfactory — short-term operation only).**

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Decoding a Severity Chart Reading

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An engineer takes readings at three locations of a 1200 RPM centrifugal pump (Class II):
> 
> - Bearing DE (drive end): $V_{RMS} = 1.2 \text{ mm/s}$
> - Bearing NDE (non-drive end): $V_{RMS} = 3.8 \text{ mm/s}$
> - Pump casing: $V_{RMS} = 6.5 \text{ mm/s}$

**Step 1:** Convert speed to Hz.

$$
f = \frac{1200}{60} = 20 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with Class II thresholds.

| Location | Measured (mm/s) | Zone | Action |
|:---------|:----------------:|:----:|:-------|
| Bearing DE | 1.2 | B (0.71–1.40 borderline B) | OK, monitor |
| Bearing NDE | 3.8 | C (2.80–7.10) | Plan maintenance |
| Pump casing | 6.5 | C (2.80–7.10) | Schedule repair soon |

**Step 3:** Engineer conclusion. The pump is in **Zone C** overall — short-term operation permitted, but **corrective action (balancing, alignment, bearing inspection) must be scheduled within 1–2 weeks**.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix when solving KTU numerical or case-study questions on severity charts.

| Observation | Likely Root Cause | Recommended Action |
|:------------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| **Zone A on all bearings** | Machine in good health | Continue routine CM |
| **Zone B on all bearings** | Normal aging, minor imbalance | Continue trending |
| **Zone C on one bearing** | Localized fault (imbalance, misalignment, bearing race defect) | Schedule repair |
| **Zone D on one bearing** | Severe fault (looseness, severe bearing damage, rubbing) | Immediate shutdown |
| **Zone C/D on housing only** | Structural resonance or piping vibration | Check foundation stiffness |
| **Zone D across entire machine** | Catastrophic fault (shaft crack, severe rub, broken footing bolt) | Emergency shutdown |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the term **Vibration Severity Chart** as per ISO 10816. Name the four operational zones and indicate which zone corresponds to a machine requiring **immediate shutdown**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Vibration Severity Chart is a standardized graphical evaluation tool defined by ISO 10816 / ISO 20816 that classifies the **RMS broadband vibration velocity** of rotating machinery into four operational zones for condition assessment.

The four zones are:

1. **Zone A** — Newly commissioned machines, very good condition.
2. **Zone B** — Acceptable for unrestricted long-term operation.
3. **Zone C** — Unsatisfactory for long-term operation; short-term operation permitted.
4. **Zone D** — Vibration level is sufficient to cause **damage**; **immediate shutdown** is required.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

Why is **RMS velocity** chosen as the primary parameter for vibration severity assessment in the frequency range **10 Hz to 1000 Hz**, instead of displacement or acceleration?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. Velocity is proportional to the **vibrational energy** transmitted by the machine, making it a true indicator of fatigue-inducing dynamic loading.
2. Velocity remains within **measurable transducer limits** across the broad mid-frequency range where most rotating machinery faults manifest.
3. Velocity is approximately **frequency-independent** in this band, allowing a single ISO 10816 chart to evaluate machines operating at vastly different speeds.

(Displacement is used below 10 Hz because velocity signal weakens, and acceleration is used above 1000 Hz because bearing impacts generate high-frequency bursts.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and use of a vibration severity chart** as per ISO 10816-3 for evaluating the condition of rotating machinery. Explain the four operational zones. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole induction motor runs at $N = 1450 \text{ RPM}$. The measured RMS vibration velocity at the drive-end bearing is $V_{RMS} = 5.2 \text{ mm/s}$. Classify the machine into the appropriate **ISO 10816-3 Class II zone**. Compute the equivalent **RMS displacement** and **RMS acceleration in g**, and comment on the recommended maintenance action. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A vibration severity chart is plotted with **rotational speed (RPM) on the X-axis** and **broadband RMS velocity (mm/s) on the Y-axis**. Two horizontal boundary lines for each of four machine classes divide the chart into four zones. The boundaries depend on the machine class.

**Construction steps:**
- The X-axis spans 600 to 12000 RPM (log scale may be used).
- The Y-axis spans 0 to 20 mm/s.
- For each machine class (I, II, III, IV), two boundary lines are drawn — one separating Zone A from B, and one separating Zone B from C. A third line separates Zone C from D.

**Four zones:**
- **Zone A** — Excellent. Newly commissioned equipment; RMS velocity is small.
- **Zone B** — Acceptable for unrestricted long-term operation; routine monitoring.
- **Zone C** — Unsatisfactory for long-term operation; limited duration permitted while planning repair.
- **Zone D** — Damage probable; immediate corrective action mandatory.

**Use:** A measurement at a defined bearing location is taken, the RMS velocity is calculated, and the point is plotted on the chart. The zone in which the point falls determines the operational and maintenance decision.

**(b)** Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Frequency of operation:**

$$
f = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{1450}{60} = 24.17 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Zone classification (Class II limits from Section 2.3):**

- Zone A: 0 – 1.40 mm/s
- Zone B: 1.40 – 2.80 mm/s
- Zone C: 2.80 – 7.10 mm/s
- Zone D: > 7.10 mm/s

The measured value $V_{RMS} = 5.2 \text{ mm/s}$ lies between 2.80 and 7.10 mm/s → **Zone C (Unsatisfactory for long-term operation)**.

**Step 3 — RMS displacement:**

$$
X_{RMS} = \frac{V_{RMS}}{2\pi f} = \frac{5.2}{2 \pi \cdot 24.17} = \frac{5.2}{151.85} = 0.0342 \text{ mm} = 34.2 \text{ \mu m}
$$

**Step 4 — RMS acceleration:**

$$
A_{RMS} = (2 \pi f) \cdot V_{RMS} = 151.85 \cdot 5.2 = 789.6 \text{ mm/s}^2
$$

Convert to g:

$$
A_{RMS} = \frac{789.6}{9810} = 0.0805 g
$$

**Step 5 — Maintenance action:**

The machine is in **Zone C**, which means it is **unsatisfactory for long-term continuous operation**. The motor may be run for a **limited period (typically a few days to a few weeks)** while maintenance planning is finalized. **Corrective action** such as balancing, alignment check, and bearing inspection should be scheduled at the earliest opportunity.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of machines** into Class I, II, III, and IV as per ISO 10816-3. Discuss the rationale behind these classifications and the typical zone boundary values for each class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump operates at $N = 2950 \text{ RPM}$. The following RMS velocity readings are recorded at three locations:

- Bearing P1 (Drive End): $V_{RMS} = 2.6 \text{ mm/s}$
- Bearing P2 (Non-Drive End): $V_{RMS} = 4.9 \text{ mm/s}$
- Pump Casing: $V_{RMS} = 8.0 \text{ mm/s}$

The pump is classified under **ISO Class II**. Identify the vibration zone at each location, and recommend an overall action plan. Calculate the dominant **rotational frequency in Hz** and the corresponding **peak displacement at Bearing P2**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** ISO 10816-3 categorizes machines into four classes based on **physical size, mass, and rigidity of mounting foundation**:

| Class | Typical Machine | Typical Power |
|:-----:|:---------------|:-------------:|
| **I** | Small machines (motors, small pumps, fans) | Up to 15 kW |
| **II** | Medium machines (industrial motors, pumps, generators) | 15–75 kW |
| **III** | Large machines on rigid foundations (turbines, compressors) | > 75 kW |
| **IV** | Large machines on soft foundations (turbo-generators) | > 75 kW |

**Rationale:** Larger machines and those on soft foundations can tolerate higher absolute vibration amplitudes before damage occurs, because their **vibration energy is distributed across larger masses** and they are designed to operate flexibly. The zone boundary values increase progressively from Class I (most strict) to Class IV (most permissive). For example:

- Class I upper Zone B limit ≈ 1.80 mm/s
- Class IV upper Zone B limit ≈ 4.50 mm/s

This classification enables **standardized comparison** between machines of vastly different sizes without one rigid threshold unfairly penalizing larger equipment.

**(b) Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the dominant frequency:**

$$
f = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{2950}{60} = 49.17 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the zone for each location (Class II thresholds):**

| Location | $V_{RMS}$ (mm/s) | Zone | Boundary crossed |
|:---------|:----------------:|:----:|:----------------|
| P1 (Drive End) | 2.6 | **B** | Within 1.40–2.80 mm/s |
| P2 (Non-Drive End) | 4.9 | **C** | Within 2.80–7.10 mm/s |
| Pump Casing | 8.0 | **D** | Above 7.10 mm/s |

**Step 3 — Peak displacement at Bearing P2:**

$$
X_{peak} = \frac{V_{peak}}{2\pi f} = \frac{V_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2\pi f}
$$

$$
X_{peak} = \frac{4.9 \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2 \pi \cdot 49.17} = \frac{4.9 \cdot 1.4142}{308.96} = \frac{6.929}{308.96}
$$

$$
X_{peak} = 0.0224 \text{ mm} = 22.4 \text{ \mu m}
$$

**Step 4 — Action plan:**

- **P1 (Drive End) — Zone B:** Continue routine condition monitoring, log trend, plan next routine inspection.
- **P2 (Non-Drive End) — Zone C:** Schedule **corrective maintenance within 1–2 weeks**. Investigate possible causes: bearing wear, misalignment, or imbalance.
- **Pump Casing — Zone D:** Vibration level indicates **possible damage**. Recommend **immediate inspection of internal impeller balance, cavitation, and structural foundation**. If casing vibration is structural (resonance), stiffening or isolation may be required.

**Overall conclusion:** Because one location (Pump Casing) is in Zone D, the unit should be considered for **scheduled shutdown at the earliest opportunity**, with priority given to verifying the integrity of internal components.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls students make — and where marks are lost:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $\sqrt{2}$** when converting RMS to peak (or vice versa). The KTU valuation key strictly checks this.
> 
> 2. **Using the wrong machine class** for zone classification. Always state the class explicitly before quoting zone limits.
> 
> 3. **Plotting Peak velocity instead of RMS velocity** on the severity chart. ISO 10816 uses RMS velocity — a common error.
> 
> 4. **Failing to mention the frequency range** (10–1000 Hz) when justifying the use of velocity as the primary parameter.
> 
> 5. **Omitting unit conversions** between mm, µm, m/s², and g. Always write the units explicitly in the final answer.
> 
> 6. **Confusing ISO 10816 (broadband vibration severity)** with **ISO 7919 (shaft vibration / axial vibration)**. They are related but distinct standards — mark loss if confused.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vibration Severity Charts**
> 
> - Vibration Severity Charts are defined by **ISO 10816 (legacy) / ISO 20816 (current)** standards.
> - Primary parameter: **Broadband RMS vibration velocity in mm/s**.
> - Applicable frequency range: **10 Hz to 1000 Hz**.
> - Four zones: **A (excellent)**, **B (acceptable)**, **C (short-term only)**, **D (damage/shutdown)**.
> - Four machine classes: **I (small), II (medium), III (large/rigid), IV (large/soft)**.
> - Zone limits become more permissive from Class I to Class IV.
> - Convert RPM to frequency using $f = N / 60$.
> - Convert RMS velocity to displacement: $X_{RMS} = V_{RMS} / (2 \pi f)$.
> - Convert RMS velocity to acceleration: $A_{RMS} = (2 \pi f) \cdot V_{RMS}$.
> - Convert peak to RMS using $V_{RMS} = V_{peak} / \sqrt{2}$.
> - Convert mm/s² to g using $1 g = 9810 \text{ mm/s}^2$.
> - Use **displacement** below 10 Hz; use **acceleration** above 1000 Hz.
> - Always declare the **machine class** before quoting zone thresholds.
> - The chart provides **severity**, not diagnosis — frequency analysis (FFT) is needed to identify the root cause.
> - A machine in **Zone C** may continue running briefly; **Zone D requires immediate shutdown**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Detection from Vibration Data** is defined as the systematic acquisition, processing, and interpretation of mechanical vibration signals to identify, classify, and quantify the operational health of rotating and reciprocating machinery. It is a non-destructive diagnostic technique that exploits the principle that every mechanical defect (unbalance, misalignment, bearing wear, gear pitting, looseness, cracks, etc.) modulates the vibration signature of a machine in a unique, repeatable, and measurable manner.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework (PEPET863 – Condition Monitoring and Maintenance Management, Module 3), vibration-based fault detection is positioned as the **cornerstone predictive maintenance technology** for rotating equipment. It is governed by international standards such as **ISO 10816 / ISO 20816** (for vibration severity evaluation) and **ISO 15243** (for bearing condition monitoring).

### Core Building Blocks of Vibration Monitoring

| Building Block | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Transducer (Sensor)** | Converts mechanical motion into electrical signal (Accelerometer, Velocity Probe, Eddy-Current Probe) |
| **Signal Conditioner** | Amplifies, filters, and linearises the raw sensor output |
| **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** | Samples the conditioned signal at a rate ≥ 2.5 × f_max (Nyquist) |
| **Signal Processor** | Performs FFT, envelope analysis, order analysis, time-waveform analysis |
| **Diagnostic Engine** | Applies statistical / AI / rule-based logic to classify the fault |
| **Display / Alarm Unit** | Presents RMS, Peak, Crest Factor, Kurtosis, Severity Zone (A/B/C/D) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Vibration is the King of CM Signals:** Vibration travels at the speed of sound through every solid component of a machine. Therefore, a single accelerometer mounted on the bearing housing can "hear" defects originating in the rotor, bearings, couplings, gears, blades, and even foundations. This makes vibration the highest information-density channel in condition monitoring.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Stethoscope"

Imagine a mechanical engineer is a **doctor** and the machine is a **patient**.

- The **stethoscope** = accelerometer (placed on the bearing cap)
- The **heartbeat** = shaft rotation frequency (1× running speed)
- The **irregular murmur** = abnormal vibration harmonics (2×, 3×, …)
- The **whistling sound** = high-frequency bearing defect impacts
- The **blood test report** = vibration spectrum (FFT plot)
- The **diagnosis** = fault type identification (unbalance, misalignment, …)

Just as a cardiologist interprets a stethoscope sound or an ECG waveform to diagnose heart disease, a vibration analyst reads a **time waveform** and a **frequency spectrum** to diagnose machine disease. **Unbalance** is the "asthma" of rotating machinery (dominant 1× peak), **misalignment** is the "arrhythmia" (dominant 2× peak), and **bearing spalling** is the "tuberculosis" (broadband high-frequency impact bursts modulated by bearing race frequencies).

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Terms (KTU Vocabulary)

The following terminology is **mandatory vocabulary** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE answers:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Vibration Metrics (Bold = ISO 10816 / ISO 20816 reference)**
> - **RMS Velocity (mm/s)** — global severity indicator, ISO 10816-3 zones
> - **Peak Acceleration (g)** — impact detection (bearing, gear)
> - **Crest Factor (Peak / RMS)** — typically 3–4 for healthy bearings, > 5 indicates incipient defects
> - **Kurtosis (K)** — 3 for Gaussian (healthy), > 5 indicates impulsive impacts
> - **Frequency (Hz / CPM / Orders)** — basis of fault classification
> - **dB ref (1g, 1mm/s, etc.)** — logarithmic severity scale

**Units used internationally:**

- **Displacement** → µm (micrometres) — for low-frequency (< 10 Hz) vibration
- **Velocity** → mm/s — for medium-frequency (10–1000 Hz) vibration — **ISO standard**
- **Acceleration** → g (9.81 m/s²) or m/s² — for high-frequency (> 1000 Hz) vibration

---

## 1.4 KTU Learning Outcomes Addressed

By the end of this topic, the student must be able to:

- **CO3 (Module 3):** Apply vibration signal processing techniques to identify common rotating-machinery faults.
- **CO4:** Evaluate machine condition against **ISO 10816 / ISO 20816** severity zones and recommend corrective maintenance actions.
- **CO5:** Interpret vibration spectra to differentiate between unbalance, misalignment, looseness, rolling-element bearing defects, and gear faults.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Vibration in Rotating Machinery

When a rotor rotates, any deviation from ideal geometry (mass, stiffness, geometry, lubrication) generates a **periodic forcing function**. By Fourier's theorem (1807), any periodic vibration signal $x(t)$ can be decomposed into a sum of sinusoids:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \cos(2\pi n f_0 t + \phi_n)
$$

where $f_0$ is the **fundamental running frequency** (shaft speed). Each integer multiple $n \cdot f_0$ is called an **order** (or **harmonic**). The amplitude $A_n$ and phase $\phi_n$ of each order carry diagnostic information.

---

## 2.2 The Three Diagnostic Domains

A vibration signal can be analysed in three equivalent but complementary domains. The KTU examiner expects students to know **all three** and to convert fluently between them.

### Domain 1 — Time Domain (Waveform Analysis)

The raw signal $x(t)$ is examined directly. Useful statistical descriptors:

**Mean value** (for AC vibration, expected ≈ 0):
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T x(t)\, dt
$$

**Root Mean Square (RMS)** — the most important overall severity metric:
$$
x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T x^2(t)\, dt}
$$

**Peak value**:
$$
x_{peak} = \max_{0 \le t \le T} \vert x(t) \vert
$$

**Crest Factor (CF)** — a powerful indicator of impulsiveness:
$$
CF = \frac{x_{peak}}{x_{rms}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation rule:** A healthy sinusoidal machine has CF ≈ √2 ≈ **1.41**. A healthy bearing has CF ≈ **3 – 4**. A spalled bearing has CF > **5 – 10**. CF > 6 almost always means impulsive impacts are dominating the signal.

**Kurtosis (K)** — the **fourth statistical moment**, normalised:
$$
K = \frac{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^4}{\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2\right]^2}
$$

> **Healthy Gaussian** signal → K = **3**. **Incipient bearing defect** → K rises to **5 – 8**. **Severe defect / advanced spalling** → K falls back toward 3 (because impacts become continuous and the signal reverts to broadband Gaussian noise). This non-monotonic behaviour is the **"Kurtosis Trap"** that KTU examiners love to test.

### Domain 2 — Frequency Domain (Spectrum / FFT Analysis)

The **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** converts N samples into N frequency bins:
$$
X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j 2\pi kn/N}, \quad k = 0,1,\dots,N-1
$$

The **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** is the computationally efficient O(N log N) implementation. For real-time CM, the FFT is recomputed every few seconds. The output is plotted as **amplitude (g or mm/s) vs frequency (Hz or CPM)**.

### Domain 3 — Angular / Order Domain (Order Analysis)

Used when shaft speed varies (start-up, shut-down, variable-speed drives like VFDs). The independent variable is the **order n = f / f_rotor**. This eliminates the smearing effect of FFT during ramp-up.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exam-ready formula bank** for vibration fault detection. Memorise every entry.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Running Frequency | $f_r = N/60$ (Hz), where N = rpm | Fundamental diagnostic reference |
| 2 | Unbalance Force (centrifugal) | $F = m\, e\, \omega^2 = m\, e\, (2\pi f_r)^2$ | Static, dynamic balancing |
| 3 | Unbalance Peak Location | $1\times f_r$ | Identifies mass unbalance |
| 4 | Misalignment Peak Location | $1\times,\, 2\times,\, 3\times f_r$ | Angular / parallel / offset |
| 5 | Looseness Peak Location | $1\times,\, 2\times,\, 3\times,\dots$ + many non-integer harmonics | Mechanical looseness |
| 6 | Bearing BPFO (Outer Race) | $BPFO = \frac{N}{2}\, f_r\, \left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$ | Outer race defect frequency |
| 7 | Bearing BPFI (Inner Race) | $BPFI = \frac{N}{2}\, f_r\, \left(1 + \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$ | Inner race defect frequency |
| 8 | Bearing BSF (Ball Spin) | $BSF = \frac{D}{2d}\, f_r\, \left[1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)^2\right]$ | Ball / roller defect |
| 9 | Bearing FTF (Cage) | $FTF = \frac{1}{2}\, f_r\, \left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$ | Cage / lubrication defect |
| 10 | Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF) | $GMF = Z \cdot f_r$ (Z = number of teeth) | Gear wear, pitting, breakage |
| 11 | RMS (continuous) | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2\, dt}$ | ISO 10816 severity |
| 12 | RMS (sampled) | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N x_i^2}$ | Digital signal processing |
| 13 | Crest Factor | $CF = x_{peak}/x_{rms}$ | Incipient impact detection |
| 14 | Kurtosis | $K = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i-\bar x)^4 \,/\, \left[\frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i-\bar x)^2\right]^2$ | Bearing health statistic |
| 15 | Vibration Severity (dB) | $L_v = 20 \log_{10}(v_{rms}/v_{ref})$ | Logarithmic severity scale |
| 16 | Transducer Sensitivity | $S = V_{out}/a_{input}$ (mV/g) | Accelerometer calibration |
| 17 | Natural Frequency | $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Resonance identification |
| 18 | Critical Speed | $N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ (rpm) | Rotor dynamics |
| 19 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \ge 2.5 \cdot f_{max}$ | DAQ card selection |
| 20 | Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | FFT bin spacing |

*(N = number of rolling elements, d = ball diameter, D = pitch diameter, α = contact angle)*

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Vibration-based fault detection is the **backbone of Industry 4.0 predictive maintenance**. It is used in:

- **Power generation**: Steam turbine-generator sets, wind turbine gearboxes, hydro turbines
- **Oil & Gas**: Centrifugal compressors, pumps (API 670 standard), motors
- **Manufacturing**: CNC spindles, rolling mills, paper machines
- **Aerospace**: Engine health monitoring (EHM), helicopter transmission HUMS
- **Railways**: Wheel-set bearings, traction motors
- **Marine**: Main propulsion shafts, propeller-induced vibrations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic Impact (cited in most KTU textbooks):** Unplanned downtime in a large petrochemical plant costs roughly **$ 50,000 – 500,000 per hour**. Vibration-based CM has been statistically proven to reduce breakdown maintenance by **70 – 90 %** and increase mean-time-between-failures (MTBF) by **2 – 3 times**.

---

## 2.5 Decision Logic — Which Metric to Use When?

| Frequency Range | Dominant Metric | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| < 10 Hz | **Displacement (µm peak-peak)** | Structural, foundation, slow rotors |
| 10 – 1000 Hz | **Velocity (mm/s RMS)** | ISO 10816-3 — overall health |
| > 1000 Hz | **Acceleration (g peak or RMS)** | Bearing, gear, cavitation |
| Impact bursts | **Crest Factor / Kurtosis** | Early defect detection |
| Cyclic patterns | **FFT orders / sidebands** | Fault classification |

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Centrifugal Unbalance Force

This is the most fundamental vibration equation in rotating machinery. It is **guaranteed to appear in KTU ESE Part A or as a sub-part in Part B**.

> **Given:** A rotor of mass m (kg) rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s). The centre of mass is offset from the rotation axis by an eccentricity e (m).
> **Find:** The centrifugal force F (N) generated.

**Step 1 — Kinematic model.**
The unbalanced mass travels in a circle of radius e. Its position vector is:

$$
\vec{r}(t) = e\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} + e\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity (first derivative with respect to time).**

$$
\vec{v}(t) = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = -e\omega\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{i} + e\omega\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{j}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration (second derivative).**

$$
\vec{a}(t) = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -e\omega^2\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} - e\omega^2\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j}
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of acceleration.**

$$
\vert \vec{a}(t) \vert = e\omega^2
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Newton's second law** $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ to obtain the centrifugal force magnitude:

$$
F = m\,e\,\omega^2
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to rotation frequency.** Since $\omega = 2\pi f_r$ where $f_r$ is the running frequency in Hz:

$$
F = m\, e\, (2\pi f_r)^2 = 4\pi^2 m\, e\, f_r^2
$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed result.**

$$
\boxed{F = m\,e\,\omega^2 = 4\pi^2\,m\,e\,f_r^2 \quad \text{[Newtons]}}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** Notice that the unbalance force grows with the **square** of speed. This is why a rotor that is acceptable at 1500 rpm becomes a violent shaker at 4500 rpm — force increases by **9 times**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Unbalance Force Calculation

> **Problem:** A grinding wheel has mass m = 25 kg and is rotated at N = 3000 rpm. After dynamic balancing, a residual eccentricity of e = 5 µm remains. Compute the centrifugal force.

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to Hz.**

$$
f_r = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert eccentricity to metres.**

$$
e = 5\ \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute angular velocity.**

$$
\omega = 2\pi f_r = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the unbalance force formula.**

$$
F = m\,e\,\omega^2 = 25 \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (314.16)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$
F = 25 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 98696
$$

$$
F = 12.34 \text{ N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** F ≈ **12.3 N** (≈ 1.26 kgf). For a 25 kg rotor, this seems tiny — but recall that vibration **force is not the same as vibration displacement**. The displacement at the bearing depends on the rotor-bearing system's stiffness k. The 1× vibration amplitude is $\delta = F/k$. A 12 N force on a bearing with stiffness k = 10⁷ N/m produces a displacement of only 1.2 µm — which is precisely why **balancing quality grades** (ISO 1940) are expressed in mm/s, not in Newtons.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Crest Factor and Kurtosis from Sampled Data

> **Problem:** A vibration data acquisition system captures N = 8 samples from a bearing housing: {0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 0.6, -0.4, -1.1, 0.7, 0.2} (units = g).
> **Find:** RMS, Peak, Crest Factor, and Kurtosis. Is the bearing healthy?

**Step 1 — Compute the mean.**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{8}(0 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.6 - 0.4 - 1.1 + 0.7 + 0.2) = \frac{1.5}{8} = 0.1875
$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations from the mean.**

$$
\{-0.1875,\ 0.3125,\ 0.8125,\ 0.4125,\ -0.5875,\ -1.2875,\ 0.5125,\ 0.0125\}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute squared deviations (for RMS and variance).**

$$
\{0.0352,\ 0.0977,\ 0.6602,\ 0.1702,\ 0.3452,\ 1.6577,\ 0.2627,\ 0.0002\}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum of squared deviations.**

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 3.229
$$

**Step 5 — RMS value.**

$$
x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3.229}{8}} = \sqrt{0.4036} = 0.635 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 6 — Peak value (absolute maximum deviation).**

$$
x_{peak} = 1.2875 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 7 — Crest Factor.**

$$
CF = \frac{x_{peak}}{x_{rms}} = \frac{1.2875}{0.635} = 2.03
$$

**Step 8 — Fourth power deviations (for Kurtosis numerator).**

$$
\{0.000062,\ 0.00953,\ 0.5457,\ 0.02898,\ 0.2095,\ 4.221,\ 0.1346,\ 0.000000002\}
$$

**Step 9 — Sum of fourth-power deviations.**

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^4 = 5.149
$$

**Step 10 — Kurtosis.**

$$
K = \frac{(1/8) \times 5.149}{\left[(1/8) \times 3.229\right]^2} = \frac{0.6436}{(0.4036)^2} = \frac{0.6436}{0.1629} = 3.95
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnosis:** Crest Factor = 2.03 (within healthy range, but on the lower side) and Kurtosis = 3.95 (slightly above the Gaussian baseline of 3). Conclusion: **Early-stage incipient bearing defect** — schedule re-check in 2 weeks, increase oil sampling frequency.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Bearing Defect Frequency Calculation

> **Problem:** A 6205 deep-groove ball bearing has N = 9 balls, pitch diameter D = 39 mm, ball diameter d = 7.94 mm, contact angle α = 0°, and the shaft runs at N_rpm = 1800 rpm.
> **Find:** BPFO, BPFI, BSF, and FTF.

**Step 1 — Shaft running frequency.**

$$
f_r = \frac{1800}{60} = 30 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute geometric ratio** $d/D$.

$$
\frac{d}{D} = \frac{7.94}{39} = 0.2036
$$

**Step 3 — Outer Race Frequency (BPFO).**

$$
BPFO = \frac{N}{2}\, f_r \left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) = \frac{9}{2} \times 30 \times (1 - 0.2036)
$$

$$
BPFO = 4.5 \times 30 \times 0.7964 = 107.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Inner Race Frequency (BPFI).**

$$
BPFI = \frac{N}{2}\, f_r \left(1 + \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) = 4.5 \times 30 \times (1 + 0.2036)
$$

$$
BPFI = 135 \times 1.2036 = 162.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Ball Spin Frequency (BSF).**

$$
BSF = \frac{D}{2d}\, f_r \left[1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)^2\right] = \frac{39}{2 \times 7.94} \times 30 \times \left[1 - 0.0414\right]
$$

$$
BSF = 2.456 \times 30 \times 0.9586 = 70.6 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 6 — Fundamental Train Frequency (FTF / Cage).**

$$
FTF = \frac{1}{2}\, f_r \left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 30 \times 0.7964 = 11.95 \text{ Hz}
$$

> **Final bearing diagnostic frequencies (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{BPFO \approx 107.5 \text{ Hz}, \quad BPFI \approx 162.5 \text{ Hz}, \quad BSF \approx 70.6 \text{ Hz}, \quad FTF \approx 11.95 \text{ Hz}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** In the FFT spectrum, look for **spectral lines exactly at these frequencies** ± 1–2 Hz tolerance. Their **sidebands** at 1× shaft frequency (modulation sidebands) are the smoking gun for distributed defects such as raceway waviness. **Envelope demodulation (EDM)** is the standard technique to extract these hidden tones from noisy high-frequency regions of the spectrum.

---

## 3.5 ISO 10816-3 Vibration Severity Zones (Machines > 15 kW, > 3000 rpm)

| Zone | RMS Velocity Range (mm/s) | Machine Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone A** | 0.00 – 1.40 | Newly commissioned; excellent | Continue routine CM |
| **Zone B** | 1.40 – 2.80 | Acceptable for long-term operation | Plan trend monitoring |
| **Zone C** | 2.80 – 4.50 | Unsatisfactory for long-term | Schedule repair in weeks |
| **Zone D** | 4.50 – 7.10 | Severe — damage may occur | Immediate shutdown |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **ISO 10816-3** (industrial machines) with **ISO 10816-2** (small machines < 15 kW) and **ISO 10816-4** (gearboxes). The zone boundaries differ. Always cite the **correct part number** in your answer.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Fault Signature Table (Highest-Priority KTU Content)

> **This is the single most important table in Module 3.** Memorise the columns.

| Fault Type | Dominant Frequency (× f_r) | Sidebands? | Phase (Dual-Probe) | Axial vs Radial | Typical Severity (mm/s RMS) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass Unbalance** | 1× | No | Stable, 0–90° between probes | Radial dominant | > 2.8 in Zone C |
| **Parallel Misalignment** | 2× strong, 1× present | Sometimes 3× | Out-of-phase, ~180° | Both, axial strong | > 4.5 |
| **Angular Misalignment | 1×, 2×, 3× | Harmonics | Out-of-phase | Axial dominant | > 3.5 |
| **Mechanical Looseness** | 1×, 2×, 3× + non-integer harmonics | Many | Unstable | Radial | Variable |
| **Bent Shaft** | 1× strong, 2× moderate | 2×, 3× | 90° between probes | Radial, axial | High |
| **Rolling Bearing Defect** | BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF | 1× sidebands | Envelope required | Radial | High-freq g |
| **Gear Mesh Fault** | GMF (Z × f_r) | 1× sidebands, "Christmas tree" | Sidebands indicate wear | Both | g peak high |
| **Blade-Pass Frequency** | BPF = N_b × f_r | Harmonics | Stable | Radial | In compressors |
| **Cavitation (Pump)** | Random broadband 0.5–5 kHz | None | None | Both, housing | High g RMS |
| **Resonance** | Amplified 1× or any order | None | Driven by resonance | Direction of mode | Variable |
| **Electrical (LOOSE BARS)** | 2× Line Frequency (2f_L) | 1× slip freq | Stable | Radial, all phases | > 1.4 |

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define vibration monitoring. List any four vibration parameters used in condition monitoring.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Vibration monitoring** is the non-invasive measurement and analysis of mechanical oscillations in a machine to assess its operational health and to detect incipient faults.

**Four key parameters:**

1. **RMS velocity (mm/s)** — ISO 10816 standard for overall severity
2. **Peak acceleration (g)** — for impact detection (bearings, gears)
3. **Crest Factor (Peak / RMS)** — impulsiveness indicator
4. **Kurtosis (4th moment)** — statistical indicator of bearing impact
5. *(any other valid)* Displacement (µm), Frequency (Hz / Orders)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by Crest Factor? What is its significance in bearing fault detection?**

**Model Answer:**

> Crest Factor (CF) is the ratio of the peak value of a vibration signal to its RMS value:
>
> $$CF = \frac{x_{peak}}{x_{rms}}$$
>
> For a **pure sinusoid**, CF = √2 ≈ 1.41. For a **healthy bearing**, CF lies between **3 and 4**. As a localised defect (spall, pit) develops on a bearing race, it produces **impulsive impacts**, which raise the peak value without appreciably increasing RMS. **CF rises above 5**, and may reach 8–10 in advanced stages. Thus, **Crest Factor is one of the earliest indicators of incipient bearing damage** — often rising months before RMS exceeds alarm thresholds.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions offer **internal choice**. We provide both alternatives.

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Vibration Analysis of a Real Machine

> `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]` A centrifugal pump runs at 3000 rpm. An accelerometer is mounted on the bearing housing. The FFT shows:
> - A dominant peak at 50 Hz (8 mm/s)
> - A strong peak at 100 Hz (5 mm/s)
> - Sidebands at 50 ± 25 Hz around 100 Hz
> - Bearing characteristic frequency tones at 162 Hz (BPFI) with 1× sidebands
>
> **Identify the faults. Justify each diagnosis using the spectrum. State the ISO 10816-3 zone.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fault Identification (7 marks)**

| Observed Peak | Frequency | Identification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **50 Hz** (8 mm/s) | 1× running speed (3000/60) | **Mass Unbalance** | 1× dominant in radial direction — classical unbalance signature |
| **100 Hz** (5 mm/s) | 2× running speed | **Misalignment** | Strong 2× harmonic — characteristic of shaft misalignment |
| **Sidebands 75 Hz & 125 Hz** | 100 ± 25 | **Misalignment modulation** | Sidebands at 1× spacing indicate cyclic modulation by shaft rotation (typical of misalignment) |
| **162 Hz with 1× sidebands** | BPFI (inner race) | **Inner race bearing defect** | Tone matches calculated BPFI, modulated by 1× → distributed defect on inner race |

**Total RMS velocity** (using square-root sum of squares):

$$
v_{rms} = \sqrt{8^2 + 5^2 + \text{noise}^2} \approx 9.4 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**(b) ISO 10816-3 Classification (7 marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since v_rms = 9.4 mm/s > 7.1 mm/s, the machine lies in **Zone D — Severe Damage Likely**. **Immediate shutdown and overhaul are recommended.**

**Recommended Actions:**

1. **Rebalance the impeller / shaft** to reduce 1× component
2. **Check coupling alignment** with laser system — correct to ≤ 0.05 mm
3. **Replace the bearing** showing BPFI tone (inner race defect)
4. **Re-measure vibration** after repair — target: Zone A (< 1.4 mm/s)
5. Update **CMMS trend log** with root-cause failure mode (RCFA)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often quote ISO 10816 boundaries incorrectly. The Class II boundary for **Zone A / B is 1.4 mm/s** (for machines on rigid foundations). For **Zone C / D**, the boundary is **4.5 mm/s** (not 7.1 — that is for Class III heavy-duty machines). Always state the foundation type and machine class explicitly in the answer.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Bearing Defect Frequency Computation & Diagnosis

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` A **6205-ZZ** deep-groove ball bearing has the following specifications:
> - Number of balls N = 9
> - Pitch diameter D = 39 mm
> - Ball diameter d = 7.94 mm
> - Contact angle α = 0°
>
> The shaft runs at **1500 rpm**. The vibration spectrum shows a strong peak at **75.2 Hz** with sidebands spaced at **25 Hz** (1× shaft frequency).
>
> **Compute the bearing characteristic frequencies and identify the fault type.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Computation of Bearing Frequencies (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Shaft frequency.**

$$
f_r = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric ratio.**

$$
\frac{d}{D} = \frac{7.94}{39} = 0.2036
$$

**Step 3 — BPFO.**

$$
BPFO = \frac{9}{2} \times 25 \times (1 - 0.2036) = 4.5 \times 25 \times 0.7964 = 89.6 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — BPFI.**

$$
BPFI = \frac{9}{2} \times 25 \times (1 + 0.2036) = 4.5 \times 25 \times 1.2036 = 135.4 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — BSF.**

$$
BSF = \frac{39}{2 \times 7.94} \times 25 \times \left[1 - (0.2036)^2\right] = 2.456 \times 25 \times 0.9586 = 58.85 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 6 — FTF (Cage).**

$$
FTF = \frac{1}{2} \times 25 \times (1 - 0.2036) = 12.5 \times 0.7964 = 9.95 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(b) Fault Identification (7 marks)**

> The observed peak is at **75.2 Hz** with sidebands spaced at **25 Hz (1×)**.
>
> None of the four calculated defect frequencies (BPFO = 89.6, BPFI = 135.4, BSF = 58.85, FTF = 9.95) directly match 75.2 Hz.
>
> However, **75.2 Hz ≈ 3 × f_r = 3 × 25 = 75 Hz**. This is a **strong 3× running frequency harmonic**.
>
> The presence of a strong 3× with sidebands indicates **mechanical looseness** (loose bearing housing, loose foot bolts, or structural crack at the pedestal).
>
> **Additional checks recommended:**
> - Tighten foundation bolts to OEM torque
> - Check bearing-housing bore for ovality
> - Use impulse-hammer modal test to identify structural resonance near 75 Hz
> - Inspect grout and baseplate for voids

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often mistakenly claim "75 Hz is the FTF" — wrong, FTF = 9.95 Hz. Others may declare "75 Hz is BPFO" — also wrong, BPFO = 89.6 Hz. **Always compute all four frequencies before matching.** The sideband spacing at 1× f_r (not at rotor frequency) is a **looseness signature** (looseness tones are *modulated* by shaft rotation, not by bearing race motion).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Mark Losers

> [!WARNING]
> **Five mark-losing mistakes observed in PEPET863 answer scripts:**
>
> 1. **Wrong ISO 10816 boundary** — quoting "1.4 mm/s" for Class III machines where the actual limit is **2.8 mm/s**. Always state the machine class.
> 2. **Confusing BPFO with BPFI** — BPFO is **outer** race (the outer race is stationary, so its frequency is *lower* than shaft frequency × N/2). BPFI is *higher*.
> 3. **Using Hz without /60 conversion** — if the question gives **rpm**, you must divide by 60. Many students forget this and get factor-of-60 errors.
> 4. **Ignoring the kurtosis trap** — saying "kurtosis is high = bad" without noting that **kurtosis peaks and then falls** as a defect progresses. This is the most sophisticated KTU question.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the bearing contact angle α** — if α ≠ 0°, the formulas change. Always state the assumption.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Use this checklist for last-day revision before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Vibration is a non-destructive CM technique** that measures displacement, velocity, or acceleration.
- [ ] **Accelerometer (ICP, 100 mV/g)** is the standard transducer for frequencies **> 10 Hz**; eddy-current probe for shaft-relative displacement < 10 Hz.
- [ ] **Three diagnostic domains:** Time, Frequency, Order.
- [ ] **RMS velocity (mm/s)** is the ISO 10816-3 standard severity metric.
- [ ] **ISO 10816-3 Class II (rigid foundation, 1500–3000 rpm):** Zone A < 1.4 → Zone B < 2.8 → Zone C < 4.5 → Zone D ≥ 4.5.
- [ ] **Crest Factor (CF):** healthy ≈ 3–4; defect > 5; advanced > 8.
- [ ] **Kurtosis (K):** healthy = 3; incipient > 5; advanced returns to ~3 (Kurtosis Trap).
- [ ] **Unbalance force:** $F = m e \omega^2 = 4\pi^2 m e f_r^2$ (grows with **square of speed**).
- [ ] **Unbalance spectrum:** dominant 1×, stable phase, radial.
- [ ] **Misalignment spectrum:** 2× dominant (parallel) or 1× + 2× + 3× (angular), axial strong.
- [ ] **Looseness spectrum:** 1×, 2×, 3× + many non-integer harmonics; phase unstable.
- [ ] **Bearing BPFO = (N/2)·f_r·(1 − d/D·cos α) — outer race.**
- [ ] **Bearing BPFI = (N/2)·f_r·(1 + d/D·cos α) — inner race.**
- [ ] **Bearing BSF = (D/2d)·f_r·[1 − (d/D·cos α)²] — ball.**
- [ ] **Bearing FTF = (1/2)·f_r·(1 − d/D·cos α) — cage.**
- [ ] **Gear Mesh Frequency (GMF) = Z · f_r;** sidebands = wear; "Christmas tree" = advanced wear.
- [ ] **Envelope Demodulation (EDM)** is the standard technique to extract bearing tones from noisy HF regions.
- [ ] **Nyquist:** Sample at $f_s \ge 2.5 \cdot f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- [ ] **FFT resolution:** $\Delta f = f_s / N$ (window length governs resolution).
- [ ] **Electrical fault signature:** Peak at 2× line frequency (100 Hz in India) with 1× slip-frequency sidebands.
- [ ] **Resonance check:** 1× amplitude > 80% of unfiltered RMS suggests structural resonance — perform modal test.
- [ ] **Always state units, tolerance, and ISO reference** in your KTU ESE answer for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Oil Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Oil Analysis (Lubricant Analysis / Wear Debris Analysis)** is a **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** condition monitoring technique in which representative samples of in-service lubricating oil (or hydraulic fluid, gearbox oil, transformer oil, etc.) are extracted from a machine and subjected to **physical, chemical, and spectrographic tests** in order to determine the condition of the oil itself, the presence of **wear debris / contamination**, and consequently the internal health of the host machine. The technique is formally classified under **Tribological Condition Monitoring (TCM)** and is governed by international standards such as: - **ISO 4406** – Cleanliness code for hydraulic fluids - **ASTM D5185** – Multi-element determination by ICP-AES (inductively coupled plasma – atomic emission spectroscopy) - **ASTM D445** – Kinematic viscosity - **ASTM D664 / D974** – Acid number (TAN / TBN) - **ASTM D892** – Foaming characteristics - **NAS 1638** – Cleanliness classification (legacy aerospace standard) ## 1.2 The Three Diagnostic Pillars of Oil Analysis | Pillar | What it Tells You | Example Parameter | |---|---|---| | **Oil Condition** | Whether the lubricant can still perform its job | Viscosity, TBN, oxidation, water content | | **Contamination** | Whether harmful external matter has entered | Dirt, water, fuel, coolant, soot | | **Machine Wear** | What is wearing inside the machine, and how fast | Fe, Cu, Pb, Cr, Al, Sn, Si (ppm) | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Key Insight:** A single oil sample simultaneously diagnoses the *oil*, the *contamination ingress*, AND the *wear mode of the machine*. This "triple diagnostic" power is the most frequently asked distinction in KTU Part A questions. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blood Test" for Machines Think of oil analysis exactly like a **blood test** at a pathology lab: - **Blood = Lubricating Oil**: It circulates through the "body" of the machine (engine, gearbox, hydraulic system). - **Doctor = Oil Laboratory**: A lab technician (spectrometer, viscometer) examines the sample. - **Sugar / Cholesterol Levels = Viscosity / TBN**: These indicate the *fluid's own health*. - **Bacteria / Virus = Wear Particles / Contaminants*: These indicate the *organs' (machine's) health*. - **Diagnosis = Maintenance Action**: A high white-blood-cell count (e.g., high Fe in oil) tells the doctor to investigate the patient's lungs (e.g., inspect cylinder liner). Just as a routine blood test can detect diabetes *before* symptoms appear, **trend-based oil analysis can detect incipient machine failure weeks or months before catastrophic breakdown** — the core philosophy of predictive maintenance. ## 1.4 The Lubricant's Triple Role in a Machine The reason oil analysis works so well is that lubricating oil simultaneously performs three critical engineering functions: 1. **Lubrication** — separates moving surfaces (reduces wear, friction). 2. **Cooling** — carries away frictional heat. 3. **Cleaning / Carrying** — suspends and transports wear debris, contaminants, and combustion by-products to the filter. Because the oil is constantly in contact with **every critical wearing surface**, it acts as a continuous information carrier about the machine's internal state. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Oil Analysis Workflow — Six Logical Steps 1. **Identify Sampling Point & Frequency** — chosen at a location where the oil is *well-mixed* and *representative* (typically upstream of the filter, downstream of the working components). 2. **Draw the Sample** — using a clean, dedicated vacuum pump or syringe sampler while the machine is *operating at normal load and temperature* (a "hot, running" sample). 3. **On-Site / In-Line Tests** — immediate measurements: temperature, pressure, color, smell, patch test (blotter spot test), water test strip. 4. **Laboratory Tests** — viscosity, TAN/TBN, FTIR, particle count, spectrographic analysis. 5. **Trend & Compare** — the new result is compared to the **Baseline (new oil) value** and the **running trend (last 3–5 samples)**. 6. **Diagnose & Decide** — issue an alert (Normal / Monitor / Investigate / Shutdown). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Single-sample "absolute" judgment is dangerous. Always interpret against a *trend*.** A single reading of 30 ppm Fe may be alarming or normal — the trend tells the truth. ## 2.2 Sampling Methods — Hierarchy of Reliability | Method | Reliability | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **In-line / On-line sensor (OLAS)** | ★★★★★ | Critical equipment, large plants | | **Live-zone vacuum sampling** | ★★★★ | Standard PdM practice (preferred) | | **Drain-plug sample (during shutdown)** | ★★ | Emergency fallback only | | **Dipper / bailer sample** | ★ | Avoid — non-representative | ## 2.3 Major Tests Performed on the Oil Sample ### A. Physical / Chemical Tests of the Oil Itself - **Kinematic Viscosity (cSt at 40 °C and 100 °C)** — measured via ASTM D445 capillary viscometer. Indicates oil degradation and contamination by fuel/coolant. - **Total Acid Number (TAN)** — ASTM D664, measured in **mg KOH/g**. Indicates acid buildup from oxidation. - **Total Base Number (TBN)** — ASTM D2896, measured in **mg KOH/g**. Indicates reserve alkalinity to neutralize acids; consumed over time especially in engine oils. - **Water Content (Karl Fischer Titration)** — ASTM D6304, in **ppm** or **%**. Excess water causes rust, cavitation, additive washout. - **Flash Point, Foaming, Air Release, Demulsibility** — secondary indicators. ### B. Wear Debris & Contamination Tests - **Spectrometric Oil Analysis (SOES / ICP-AES)** — quantifies dissolved and fine suspended wear metals (typically < 8 µm) in **ppm (mg/kg)**. - **Ferrography** (Analytical & Direct Reading) — separates and ranks wear particles (> 5 µm) by size, morphology, and concentration for failure mode identification. - **Particle Counting & Classification** — ISO 4406 / NAS 1638 cleanliness codes. - **FTIR Spectroscopy** — detects oxidation, nitration, soot, glycol, fuel dilution (qualitative + quantitative with calibrated methods). ## 2.4 The Spectrographic Element Menu (High-Yield for KTU) | Element | Typical Source in Machine | |---|---| | **Fe (Iron)** | Gears, shafts, cylinder liners, bearings, rust | | **Cu (Copper)** | Bearings, bushings, oil coolers, thrust washers | | **Al (Aluminium)** | Pistons, bearing cages, pumps | | **Pb (Lead)** | Bearing linings, solder | | **Cr (Chromium)** | Piston rings, roller bearings | | **Sn (Tin)** | Bearing overlays | | **Si (Silicon)** | Dust ingress, seal wear, anti-foam additive | | **Na, K (Sodium, Potassium)** | Coolant leak (antifreeze contains Na/K borate/phosphate) | | **Ag, Ni, Ti, V** | Specialized alloys, valve guides | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Decision Rules > [!NOTE] > The following equations, conversions, and trend rules are the **minimum** expected competence for a KTU University Exam answer on this topic. ### 2.5.1 Viscosity & Viscosity Index (VI) The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an empirical, unitless number indicating the rate of viscosity change with temperature. Higher VI ⇒ oil thickens less when cold, thins less when hot. $$ \text{VI} = 100 \times \frac{L - U}{L - H} $$ where: - $U$ = kinematic viscosity of the *sample* at 40 °C (cSt) - $L$ = viscosity at 40 °C of an oil of VI = 0 with the *same* viscosity at 100 °C as the sample - $H$ = viscosity at 40 °C of an oil of VI = 100 with the *same* viscosity at 100 °C as the sample ### 2.5.2 ISO 4406 Cleanliness Code The code is reported as three (or more) numbers representing cumulative particle counts per mL of fluid at threshold sizes **≥ 4 µm(c)**, **≥ 6 µm(c)**, and **≥ 14 µm(c)**. $$ \text{ISO Code} = \big[\, N_4 \;/\; N_6 \;/\; N_{14} \,\big] $$ The reported code is the **log (base 10) bin** of the count rounded up, e.g., a count of 50,000 particles/mL at ≥ 4 µm ⇒ code 23 (since $10^{4.69} \approx 49{,}000$ falls in bin 23). ### 2.5.3 Wear Rate / Severity Trending A linear wear trend in **ppm per operating hour** allows prediction of remaining life: $$ \text{Wear Rate} = \frac{C_2 - C_1}{\Delta t} \quad [\text{ppm/hr}] $$ where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are successive concentration readings and $\Delta t$ is the operating hours between samples. ### 2.5.4 Particle Count Conversion (Simplified Bin Rule) $$ \text{ISO Bin Number} = \left\lceil \log_{10}(N) \right\rceil_{\text{adjusted}} $$ For exam purposes, use the lookup: | Range (particles/mL) | ISO Code | |---|---| | 1 – 2 | 1 | | 5,001 – 10,000 | 13 | | 40,001 – 80,000 | 22 | | 160,001 – 320,000 | 24 | | 1,000,001 – 2,000,000 | 27 | ### 2.5.5 Decision Rule: TBN for Engine Oils $$ \text{TBN}_{\text{used}} = \text{TBN}_{\text{new}} - \Delta \text{TBN} $$ - **TBN > 50 % of new value** → Continue in service - **TBN = 30 – 50 % of new value** → Shorten drain interval - **TBN < 30 % of new value** → Change oil immediately ## 2.6 Wear Debris Morphology — The Ferrographic Fingerprint | Particle Morphology | Indicative Failure Mode | |---|---| | **Normal rubbing (small, thin platelets, 1–15 µm)** | Healthy operation | | **Cutting wear (spiraled, sharp, elongated "curl shavings")** | Abrasive wear from hard contaminant (Si) | | **Sliding wear (long, thin, parallel-sided)** | Adhesive wear, boundary lubrication failure | | **Spherical particles (smooth, often 1–5 µm)** | Rolling contact fatigue (early stage) | | **Spherical with cracks / "spalling"** | Advanced rolling element fatigue | | **Chunky / severe sliding (large, thick, irregular)** | Severe gear/bearing distress — imminent failure | | **Lacquer / varnish (clear, amorphous film)** | Oil oxidation / hot-spot degradation | | **Brass / bronze color (Cu, Zn alloy particles)** | Bush / thrust-washer failure | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ferrography is the only oil-analysis technique that reveals *failure mode*, not just *failure presence*.** Spectrometric SOES tells you *what* is wearing; ferrography tells you *how* it is wearing. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aviation (Jet engines, APUs)**: Spectrometric oil analysis is mandatory — used to schedule "on-condition" engine overhauls, saving millions per fleet per year. - **Wind turbines (gearbox + hydraulic)**: Avoids tower-climb inspections; remote trend monitoring. - **Power generation (gas/steam turbines, transformers)**: DGA — Dissolved Gas Analysis of transformer oil is a special branch of oil analysis detecting electrical faults. - **Mining & cement (giant gearboxes, hydraulic shovels)**: High-value, hard-to-replace assets — PdM via oil analysis is standard. - **Marine (main propulsion lube, hydraulic)**: ISO 4406 compliance mandatory for ship-owner warranty claims. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The following worked numericals are aligned with KTU's typical "Part B (14 marks)" pattern, where one or two short computations (5–7 marks each) are embedded within a descriptive answer. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Viscosity Index Calculation **Problem.** A hydraulic oil has a kinematic viscosity of **30 cSt at 100 °C**. From the standard VI reference tables, oils of the same 100 °C viscosity have: - $L = 570$ cSt (at 40 °C, VI = 0 reference oil) - $H = 290$ cSt (at 40 °C, VI = 100 reference oil) - The actual sample's 40 °C viscosity is **$U = 310$ cSt**. Calculate the **Viscosity Index** of the oil and interpret it. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1.** Write down the VI definition: $$ \text{VI} = 100 \times \frac{L - U}{L - H} $$ **Step 2.** Substitute the given values (use `\vert` for absolute value safety inside tables, but here we are in display math, so plain `|` is fine): $$ \text{VI} = 100 \times \frac{570 - 310}{570 - 290} $$ **Step 3.** Compute the numerator: $$ 570 - 310 = 260 $$ **Step 4.** Compute the denominator: $$ 570 - 290 = 280 $$ **Step 5.** Form the ratio and multiply by 100: $$ \text{VI} = 100 \times \frac{260}{280} = 100 \times 0.9286 $$ **Step 6.** Final result: $$ \boxed{\text{VI} \approx 92.86 \;\;( \approx 93 )} $$ **Step 7. Engineering Interpretation.** - VI > 90 ⇒ a **High-VI (HVI)** oil with very good temperature–viscosity stability. - Suitable for **wide-temperature hydraulic systems** (e.g., outdoor machinery, arctic operations, precision hydraulics). - A significant drop in VI across service life would indicate **shear breakdown of VI improver (polymer) additives** — a strong wear-debris / oil-condition alarm. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — ISO 4406 Cleanliness Code Assignment **Problem.** A hydraulic fluid sample is analyzed in a laser particle counter. The cumulative particle counts are: | Threshold size | Count per mL | |---|---| | ≥ 4 µm(c) | **75,000** | | ≥ 6 µm(c) | **19,000** | | ≥ 14 µm(c) | **1,200** | Determine the **ISO 4406 cleanliness code**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1.** Recall the bin assignment rule — the code number is the integer such that the count lies between $10^{N-1}$ and $10^{N}$ particles/mL, then rounded up to the next integer (in standard practice the code is the bin number). **Step 2.** For ≥ 4 µm: 75,000 particles/mL - $10^4 = 10{,}000$ and $10^5 = 100{,}000$ - 75,000 lies in the bin $[10^4,\,10^5)$, which is **Code 23** (codes are offset such that bin $10^4$–$2\cdot10^4$ is 22, $2\cdot10^4$–$4\cdot10^4$ is 22, $4\cdot10^4$–$8\cdot10^4$ is 23 — i.e., count 40,001–80,000 ⇒ code 23). **Step 3.** For ≥ 6 µm: 19,000 particles/mL - 19,000 lies in the range 16,001–32,000 ⇒ **Code 21**. **Step 4.** For ≥ 14 µm: 1,200 particles/mL - 1,200 lies in the range 1,001–2,000 ⇒ **Code 18**. **Step 5.** Assemble the code: $$ \boxed{\text{ISO 4406 Code} = 23 \;/\; 21 \;/\; 18 } $$ **Step 6. Engineering Interpretation.** - A typical *new* hydraulic oil leaving the refinery is around 18/16/13. - A typical *in-service* industrial hydraulic system target is 20/18/15. - A reading of **23/21/18** indicates **contamination ingress** — likely from failed filter elements, reservoir breather contamination, or hose degradation. Investigate filter status and seal integrity. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Wear Rate & Remaining Useful Life Estimation **Problem.** Two oil samples from a gearbox are drawn 500 hours apart. Spectrometric Fe content is: - Sample 1 (at $t_1$): 12 ppm - Sample 2 (at $t_2$): 27 ppm The catastrophic Fe threshold (gear-tooth fatigue limit) for this gearbox is **45 ppm**. Estimate (a) the **wear rate**, and (b) the **remaining operating hours before the alarm threshold**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1. Wear Rate formula:** $$ \text{Wear Rate} = \frac{C_2 - C_1}{\Delta t} $$ **Step 2.** Compute concentration rise: $$ \Delta C = 27 - 12 = 15 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 3.** Operating time interval: $$ \Delta t = 500 \text{ hr} $$ **Step 4.** Compute wear rate: $$ \text{Wear Rate} = \frac{15}{500} = 0.03 \;\text{ppm/hr} $$ **Step 5. Remaining concentration margin** before the alarm: $$ \text{Margin} = 45 - 27 = 18 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 6. Remaining life (linear extrapolation, assuming the rate continues):** $$ \text{Remaining Life} = \frac{\text{Margin}}{\text{Wear Rate}} = \frac{18}{0.03} = 600 \text{ hr} $$ **Step 7. Final answer:** $$ \boxed{\text{Wear Rate} = 0.03 \text{ ppm/hr}, \quad \text{Remaining Life} \approx 600 \text{ operating hours}} $$ **Step 8. Engineering Decision.** Schedule a **detailed vibration analysis + borescope inspection** within 200 hours; plan a **gear-set replacement / overhaul** in the next scheduled shutdown window (not later than 600 hours of operation). > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Board examiners expect students to **state the assumption (linear trend)** and to recommend a **condition-based action**, not just the number. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — TBN Decision Rule (Diesel Engine) **Problem.** A diesel engine uses oil with a **new-oil TBN = 12 mg KOH/g**. The current TBN reading is **5 mg KOH/g**. Apply the KTU/standard decision rule to recommend the action. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1.
