Compute the TBN ratio:** $$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{TBN}_{\text{used}}}{\text{TBN}_{\text{new}}} \times 100\% = \frac{5}{12} \times 100\% $$ **Step 2.** Evaluate: $$ \text{Ratio} = 41.67\% $$ **Step 3. Apply the decision rule:** - 30 – 50 % of new value → **Shorten drain interval**; also investigate for sulfur-rich fuel ingress (acid build-up) or coolant leak (glycol reduces TBN). **Step 4. Recommendation:** $$ \boxed{\text{Action: Shorten the drain interval by 30–50\%; investigate acid source (fuel/coolant).}} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Wear-Debris Source Identification | Finding (↑ above baseline) | Most Probable Internal Source | Action | |---|---|---| | Fe ↑↑, Cr ↑ | Piston rings, cylinder liner | Compression test, bore inspection | | Fe ↑↑, Cu ↑↑ | Journal bearing wear | Check lube pressure, alignment, oil temp | | Cu ↑↑, Pb ↑↑, Sn ↑ | Babbitt/bronze bearing lining failure | Overhaul bearing housing | | Si ↑↑, Al ↑ | Sand/dust ingestion | Check air filter, seals, breather | | Na, K ↑, glycol trace | Coolant leak into oil | Pressure-test cooling system, head gasket | | Fuel dilution (↓ flash point) | Injector leak, rich mixture | Injector service | | Varnish, ↑ oxidation (FTIR peak ~1710 cm⁻¹) | Oil overheat / extended drain | Reduce drain interval, check cooler | | Spherical Fe particles with cracks | Rolling-element fatigue | Bearing imminent failure — shutdown | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the table (KTU viva):** **"Fe-Cu-Pb"** (gear-bearing triplet) + **"Si-Na-K"** (contamination triplet) — these six elements cover 80 % of KTU questions on wear-source diagnosis. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Oil Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Machine in Operation] --> B[Sampling Point Selection
Upstream of Filter] B --> C[Live-Zone Vacuum Sampling
Hot Running Sample] C --> D{On-Site Tests} D --> D1[Patch / Blotter Test] D --> D2[Water Test Strip] D --> D3[Visual / Smell / Color] D --> E[Oil Sample Bottles
Labelled and Sealed] E --> F[Oil Condition Lab Tests] F --> F1[Viscosity ASTM D445] F --> F2[TAN ASTM D664] F --> F3[TBN ASTM D2896] F --> F4[Water KF ASTM D6304] E --> G[Wear Debris Tests] G --> G1[SOES ICP-AES
Wear Metals ppm] G --> G2[Ferrography
Particle Morphology] G --> G3[Particle Count ISO 4406] G --> G4[FTIR Spectroscopy
Oxidation Soot Glycol] F1 --> H[Database
Trending Engine] F2 --> H F3 --> H F4 --> H G1 --> H G2 --> H G3 --> H G4 --> H H --> I{Trend Logic
vs Baseline and Limits} I --> J[Normal
Continue] I --> K[Monitor
Shorten Interval] I --> L[Investigate
Inspect Machine] I --> M[Critical
Shutdown] L --> N[Maintenance Action] M --> N N --> O[Feedback to CMMS
RCM Database] ``` ## 4.2 Three-Pillar Diagnostic Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PIL1[Oil Condition Pillar] P1[Viscosity] --> R1[Lubricity Loss or Fuel Dilution] P2[TAN] --> R2[Acidic Oxidation] P3[TBN] --> R3[Reserve Alkalinity Loss] P4[Water] --> R4[Hydrolysis and Rust] end subgraph PIL2[Contamination Pillar] Q1[Si Dust] --> S1[Filter Clogging Abrasive Wear] Q2[Na K Glycol] --> S2[Coolant Leak] Q3[Fuel] --> S3[Viscosity Drop] Q4[Soot] --> S4[Lacquering and Sludge] end subgraph PIL3[Machine Wear Pillar] W1[Fe] --> X1[Gears Shafts Liners] W2[Cu Pb Sn] --> X2[Bearings Bushings] W3[Al] --> X3[Pistons Pumps] W4[Cr] --> X4[Rings Hardened Steel] end PIL1 --> OUT[Maintenance Decision] PIL2 --> OUT PIL3 --> OUT ``` ## 4.3 Wear Particle Morphology Decision Tree (Ferrography) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Wear Particle Observed] --> B{Size in Micrometres} B -->|< 5} --> C[Spectrometric SOES Region
Dissolved and Fine] B -->|5 to 20} --> D[Ferrographic Range
Analytical DR Ferrography] B -->|> 50} --> E[Filter and Magnetic Plug
Large Debris] D --> F{Shape and Morphology} F --> G[Thin Platelet
Normal Rubbing] F --> H[Spiral Curl Cutting
Abrasive Wear] F --> I[Long Thin Sliding
Adhesive Wear] F --> J[Smooth Spheres
Early Rolling Fatigue] F --> K[Cracked Spheres Spalling
Advanced Fatigue] F --> L[Chunky Severe Sliding
Imminent Failure] F --> M[Amorphous Lacquer
Oil Oxidation Hot Spot] G --> OUT[Normal Continue] H --> OUT2[Filter and Seal Inspection] I --> OUT3[Lubricant Upgrade] J --> OUT4[Monitor Bearing Closely] K --> OUT5[Plan Bearing Replacement] L --> OUT6[Immediate Shutdown] M --> OUT7[Reduce Drain Interval and Oil Temp] ``` ## 4.4 Sampling Location — Block Schematic of a Lubrication System ```mermaid flowchart LR PUMP[Pump] --> VALVE[Valve Manifold] VALVE --> GEAR[Gearbox Bearings] VALVE --> MOTOR[Hydraulic Motor] GEAR --> RES[Reservoir] MOTOR --> RES RES --> FILTER[Filter Unit] FILTER --> PUMP SP1[Sample Point A
Upstream of Filter
Preferred] --- GEAR SP2[Sample Point B
Downstream of Filter] --- FILTER SP3[Sample Point C
Reservoir Bottom] --- RES note1[Highest representative
wear signal] -.-> SP1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout)** > 1. Do **not** confuse *Spectrometric Oil Analysis (SOES)* with *Ferrography* — they are **complementary**, not interchangeable. SOES quantifies; Ferrography qualifies the failure mode. > 2. Do **not** state the ISO 4406 code in the wrong order. The convention is **largest-to-smallest threshold: ≥4 / ≥6 / ≥14 µm(c)**. > 3. Always mention that oil analysis is a **trend-based** technique — a single absolute reading is **not** a diagnosis. > 4. For **Viscosity Index**, state the unit as **dimensionless** (no units) — a common error is writing "VI = 95 cSt". > 5. In wear-debris tables, **distinguish *wear metal* from *contaminant***. Si can be a contaminant (dust) or an additive (anti-foam). Always state the most likely cause. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each) ### Q1. Define oil analysis. List any three tests performed on a used lubricating oil sample. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 model]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Oil analysis is a predictive maintenance technique in which a representative sample of in-service lubricating oil is drawn from a machine and tested in the laboratory to assess (i) the *condition of the oil*, (ii) the *level and type of contamination*, and (iii) the *rate and nature of wear debris* generated by the machine, in order to predict impending failure and schedule condition-based maintenance. **Any three tests (3 × ⅔ = 2 marks):** 1. **Kinematic Viscosity** (ASTM D445) — detects oil degradation or fuel dilution. 2. **Total Base Number (TBN)** (ASTM D2896) — measures reserve alkalinity to neutralize combustion acids. 3. **Spectrometric Oil Analysis (SOES / ICP-AES)** (ASTM D5185) — quantifies wear metals in ppm. 4. **Ferrography** — classifies wear particles by size and morphology to identify failure mode. 5. **ISO 4406 Particle Count** — assesses fluid cleanliness. 6. **FTIR Spectroscopy** — detects oxidation, nitration, soot, and glycol contamination. --- ### Q2. Distinguish clearly between **Spectrometric Oil Analysis (SOES)** and **Ferrography**. State the typical particle size range each technique addresses. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | SOES (Spectrometric) | Ferrography | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | Atomic emission / absorption spectroscopy of dissolved and fine-suspended metals | Magnetic and gravitational separation of wear particles onto a substrate for microscopic examination | | **What it gives** | Quantitative concentration of elements (in **ppm**) | Qualitative morphology, size, and concentration of larger particles | | **Particle size range** | < **8 µm** (sub-micrometre dissolved to fine-suspended) | **5 µm – several hundred µm** | | **Diagnostic output** | *What* is wearing | *How* it is wearing (failure mode) | | **Limit** | Cannot identify the type of wear | Slower, requires trained analyst | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice) ### Question A — (a) Explain the procedure of oil analysis as a condition monitoring technique. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 model]` **Model Answer (7 marks):** 1. **Selection of Sampling Point (1 mark):** The sample must be drawn from a *live, well-mixed* zone of the lubrication circuit — preferably **upstream of the filter** and downstream of the most heavily loaded components. The sampling point is fitted with a dedicated **valve and vacuum sampler** to obtain a representative "hot, running" sample. 2. **Sample Collection Procedure (1 mark):** The machine is run at *normal load and operating temperature*. A clean, dry, labelled sample bottle is attached to the vacuum sampler. A volume of 100–250 mL is drawn; the bottle is sealed, marked with **machine ID, sample point, date, operating hours, oil temperature**, and sent to the lab. Care is taken to avoid **cross-contamination** by flushing the line and using fresh bottles. 3. **On-site / In-line Tests (1 mark):** Before dispatch, simple checks are made: **patch / blotter test** (droplet on filter paper — dark spot with black core indicates soot/dispersion breakdown), **water test strip** (calcium-hydride reaction), **visual color and smell** (milky ⇒ water; burnt ⇒ overheat; dark ⇒ soot). For critical equipment, **in-line sensors** (OLAS — On-Line Analysis System) continuously monitor viscosity, water, and ferrous content. 4. **Laboratory Tests on the Oil Itself (1.5 marks):** - **Viscosity (ASTM D445)** at 40 °C and 100 °C — detects fuel dilution, oxidation thickening, or shear loss. - **TAN (ASTM D664)** and **TBN (ASTM D2896)** — measure acid buildup and reserve alkalinity. - **Water content (KF titration, ASTM D6304)** — ppm level. - **FTIR (ASTM E2412)** — oxidation, nitration, soot, glycol, fuel peaks. 5. **Wear Debris and Contamination Tests (1.5 marks):** - **SOES / ICP-AES (ASTM D5185)** — quantifies Fe, Cu, Pb, Cr, Al, Sn, Si, Na, K, etc., in ppm. - **Analytical & Direct-Reading Ferrography (ASTM D7690)** — separates and ranks particles by size, morphology, and concentration. Reveals failure mode (cutting, sliding, fatigue, spalling). - **Particle Counting (ISO 4406 / NAS 1638)** — cleanliness classification. 6. **Trending and Decision (1 mark):** The new readings are plotted on a **trend chart** alongside the *new-oil baseline* and the *alarm limits* (typically: Normal / Monitor / Investigate / Shutdown). A rising trend — especially an *exponential* rise in wear metals or a *sudden step change* — triggers a **work order** in the CMMS for inspection or repair. --- ### Question A — (b) A hydraulic oil sample shows the following cumulative particle counts per mL: 4 µm(c) → 35,000; 6 µm(c) → 9,000; 14 µm(c) → 700. Determine the ISO 4406 cleanliness code and recommend suitable action. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Bin assignment for ≥ 4 µm(c) (1 mark):** Count = 35,000 ⇒ falls in the range 32,001 – 40,000 ⇒ **Code 22**. **Step 2 — Bin assignment for ≥ 6 µm(c) (1 mark):** Count = 9,000 ⇒ falls in the range 8,001 – 16,000 ⇒ **Code 20**. **Step 3 — Bin assignment for ≥ 14 µm(c) (1 mark):** Count = 700 ⇒ falls in the range 501 – 1,000 ⇒ **Code 17**. **Step 4 — Final Code (1 mark):** $$ \boxed{\text{ISO 4406 Cleanliness Code} = 22 \;/\; 20 \;/\; 17 } $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):** - Typical *in-service* target for industrial hydraulics: **20/18/15**. - The reported code (22/20/17) is **two ISO codes worse** than the target at all three thresholds, indicating **particulate contamination ingress** or **filter degradation**. **Step 6 — Recommended Action (1.5 marks):** 1. **Immediate** — change the system filter elements; inspect the **reservoir breather** (often the entry point for ambient dust). 2. **Short term** — perform a **flushing cycle** to ISO 4406 target (20/18/15); re-sample after 50 operating hours. 3. **Long term** — investigate the **hydraulic cylinder rod seals** for dust wiper failure; verify **reservoir cleanliness** during next maintenance window. 4. **If trend continues to rise** despite replacement — perform **vibration and temperature analysis** to check pump and valve health. --- ### Question B — (a) With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of **Ferrography**. Discuss the wear-particle morphologies used to identify the failure mode. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model]` **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Working Principle (3.5 marks):** Ferrography is a technique in which wear particles suspended in an oil sample are **separated and deposited** onto a transparent **substrate (a glass slide or "ferrogram")** using two simultaneous physical fields: 1. **A magnetic field gradient** — generated by a permanent magnet placed beneath the substrate. Ferromagnetic particles (iron, steel) are drawn toward and aligned perpendicular to the slide, in the order of their **size and magnetic moment**. 2. **A gravitational / viscous flow** — the oil sample is pumped (or allowed to flow) at a controlled rate over the inclined substrate. This causes **non-magnetic** particles (Cu, Al, bronze, ceramics) to deposit in proportion to their size and density. Two variants exist: - **Analytical Ferrography**: produces a fixed ferrogram examined under a **microscope** (×100 – ×400); used to study particle morphology. - **Direct-Reading (DR) Ferrography**: the ferrogram is read **optically** by a densitometer, producing two numerical readouts — **Dl (Large particle index)** and **Ds (Small particle index)**. The ratio **Dl/Ds** is a powerful wear-severity indicator: < 1.5 normal, 1.5 – 3 moderate, > 3 severe. **Wear-Particle Morphologies (3.5 marks):** | Morphology | Indicative Failure Mode | |---|---| | Thin platelets, 1 – 15 µm, smooth edges | Normal rubbing wear | | Spiral curls / shavings, sharp edges, bright | Two-body abrasive wear (contaminant cutting) | | Long, thin, parallel-sided strips | Adhesive / sliding wear, boundary lubrication failure | | Smooth spheres, 1 – 5 µm | Early rolling-element fatigue | | Spheres with cracks, flaking, spalling | Advanced rolling contact fatigue (bearing imminent failure) | | Large, chunky, irregular, dark | Severe gear / bearing distress | | Amorphous, transparent / brown film | Varnish / lacquer — oil oxidation, hot spots | > The morphology interpretation is the **most examiner-tested** aspect of ferrography in KTU. --- ### Question B — (b) The Fe content of a gearbox oil rises from 10 ppm at 1000 hours to 18 ppm at 1500 hours. The critical Fe limit is 40 ppm. (i) Determine the wear rate. (ii) Estimate the remaining operating hours before the alarm. (iii) Suggest the maintenance action. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 model]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Wear rate formula (1 mark):** $$ \text{Wear Rate} = \frac{C_2 - C_1}{\Delta t} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):** $$ \text{Wear Rate} = \frac{18 - 10}{1500 - 1000} = \frac{8}{500} $$ **Step 3 — Compute wear rate (0.5 mark):** $$ \boxed{\text{Wear Rate} = 0.016 \;\text{ppm/hr}} $$ **Step 4 — Remaining margin (1 mark):** $$ \Delta C_{\text{remaining}} = 40 - 18 = 22 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 5 — Remaining life (1.5 marks):** $$ \text{Remaining Life} = \frac{22}{0.016} = 1375 \text{ hr} $$ **Step 6 — Maintenance Action (2 marks):** 1. **Increase oil-sampling frequency** to every 200 hours (instead of 500) to confirm linear trend. 2. **Perform a complementary vibration analysis** (gears, bearings) to localize the source of Fe rise. 3. **Inspect magnetic drain plug / inline filter** for the next scheduled stoppage — look for spalling debris. 4. **Plan a gearbox overhaul** (gear and bearing inspection) within the next 1000 hours. 5. **Check for root causes** — lubrication oil level, oil cooler fouling, overload conditions, or possible abrasive ingress (Si reading). 6. If wear rate *accelerates* (non-linear) — initiate **unscheduled shutdown** to prevent catastrophic gear failure. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > This is the **last thing** you should read before entering the KTU examination hall for this module. - **Oil analysis is the *blood test* of machinery** — it diagnoses the **oil**, the **contamination**, AND the **machine wear** in one sample. - It is a **trend-based PdM technique** — never judge a single reading in isolation. - The sample must be **hot, running, and live-zone** — preferably **upstream of the filter** for the highest wear-signal sensitivity. - **Three diagnostic pillars**: (1) Oil Condition, (2) Contamination, (3) Machine Wear. - **SOES (ICP-AES)** quantifies **dissolved and fine < 8 µm** wear metals in **ppm**. - **Ferrography** identifies **failure mode** by particle morphology — size range 5 µm and above. - **Particle count** yields the **ISO 4406 code** in three numbers: **≥ 4 / ≥ 6 / ≥ 14 µm(c)**, written in that order. - **Viscosity** (cSt) → ASTM D445; **TAN** → ASTM D664; **TBN** → ASTM D2896; **Water** → ASTM D6304 (KF). - **Key wear-metal fingerprints**: - **Fe-Cr** → rings & liners - **Fe-Cu** → journal bearings - **Cu-Pb-Sn** → babbitt/bronze bearing lining - **Al** → pistons, pumps, cages - **Si** → dust or anti-foam additive - **Na-K + glycol** → coolant leak - **Viscosity Index (VI)** is **dimensionless**; > 90 = HVI oil. - **TBN decision rule**: < 30 % of new value ⇒ change oil immediately. - **Wear rate** is a linear approximation: $\frac{\Delta C}{\Delta t}$ in **ppm/hr**. - **Spherical cracked particles** = imminent rolling-element bearing failure. - **Lacquer / varnish films** = oil overheat / oxidation — reduce drain interval. - **ISO 4406** for cleanliness, **NAS 1638** for legacy aerospace cleanliness, **ASTM D5185** for ICP-AES wear metals. - In answers, **always close with a maintenance recommendation** — examiners award marks for the *decision*, not just the *number*.

topics.title: Maintenance Division Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Division Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Maintenance Division Model** is a strategic organizational framework that defines how maintenance manpower, resources, responsibilities, and authority are structured, distributed, and coordinated across an industrial plant. It establishes the reporting hierarchy, the geographic and functional grouping of maintenance crews, and the decision-making flow between the maintenance department and production operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEPET863 Module 4):**
> *Maintenance Division Models* refer to the standardized organizational blueprints used by industrial engineers to partition the total maintenance workload of a plant into manageable, accountable units. The three classical models recognized by KTU 2024 scheme are **Centralized**, **Decentralized**, and **Hybrid (Combined)** models. The model selected dictates the *span of control*, *response time*, *crew utilization*, and *overall plant availability*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital Analogy

Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital:

- **Centralized Model**: There is one single "Maintenance & Engineering Department" for the whole hospital. Whether the AC breaks down in the Cardiac wing or a surgery light fails in the Orthopedic OT, the **same central engineering team** receives the call, dispatches a technician, and tracks the job. The chief engineer has full control over every technician.

- **Decentralized Model**: Each wing (Cardiac, Orthopedic, Pediatric) has its **own dedicated maintenance technician** living inside that wing. The wing supervisor directly instructs him. The technicians are physically and administratively "embedded" inside the production unit.

- **Hybrid Model**: A small central engineering cell exists for high-skill jobs (like MRI calibration or HVAC overhaul). But each wing also has a **basic maintenance helper** for day-to-day small faults. Complex work is escalated to the central cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The decision of which model to choose is NOT arbitrary. It depends on the **plant size**, **equipment complexity**, **geographic spread**, **shift patterns**, and **availability of skilled labor**. A refinery spread over 5 km² will rarely use a pure centralized model, while a small CNC shop will not afford a decentralized one.

## 1.3 Primary Objectives of a Maintenance Division Model

Every division model must satisfy **five engineering objectives**:

1. **Minimize Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** — measured in **hours**.
2. **Maximize Equipment Availability (A)** — typically targeted **≥ 95%**.
3. **Optimize Crew Utilization Factor** — ideally between **80% and 90%**.
4. **Establish Clear Accountability Lines** — single-point ownership for each asset.
5. **Enable Rapid Escalation** — defined paths for routine vs. emergency work.

## 1.4 Classification of Maintenance Division Models

Maintenance division models can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis 1 — Authority Structure:**
- Centralized Model
- Decentralized Model
- Hybrid (Combined) Model

**Axis 2 — Crew Formation Philosophy (Kelly & Walker Classification):**
- Project Crews
- Area Crews
- Specialty Crews
- Combination Crews

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU:** The most frequent exam question is a comparison table between Centralized and Decentralized models. Memorize at least **6 comparison parameters** (Authority, Response Time, Travel Time, Crew Utilization, Skill Level, Cost).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Model 1 — Centralized Maintenance Model

### 2.1.1 Architecture

In the **Centralized Model**, every maintenance worker in the plant — fitters, electricians, instrument technicians, lubrication crews — reports to **one single Maintenance Manager** who, in turn, reports to the Plant Head (or a Works Engineer). There is a single maintenance workshop, a single store, and a single planning cell.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic

1. Production supervisor raises a work request → reaches central maintenance desk.
2. Maintenance planner converts request into a **Job Order** with priority, skill, and ETA.
3. Central planner dispatches the nearest available (or most qualified) crew.
4. Crew travels from the central workshop to the job site.
5. After completion, the crew returns to the central workshop for the next dispatch.

### 2.1.3 Advantages

- **Optimum crew utilization** because crews serve the whole plant.
- **Lower total manpower** required for the same workload (due to statistical smoothing).
- **Easier standardization** of procedures, tools, and spares.
- **Single point of accountability** for maintenance KPIs (MTBF, MTTR, Availability).
- **Better cost control** through centralized budgeting.

### 2.1.4 Disadvantages

- **Long travel distances** between job sites and the central workshop.
- **Higher response time** for emergencies far from the central hub.
- **Production–Maintenance friction** because production supervisors have no direct authority.
- **Communication delays** because every request must pass through the central planner.

### 2.1.5 Best-Suited Scenarios

- Small to medium plants (area **< 50,000 m²**).
- Plants with low equipment density per square meter.
- Continuous process plants where standardization matters more than speed.

---

## 2.2 Model 2 — Decentralized Maintenance Model

### 2.2.1 Architecture

Here, the plant is **physically divided into zones, areas, or production units**. Each area has its own maintenance supervisor, crew, and small tool crib. The maintenance supervisor reports **directly to the Area Production Manager**, not to a central maintenance head.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic

1. Each area maintains its own **preventive maintenance schedule**.
2. Crews are stationed permanently inside their assigned area.
3. Spare parts are kept in **decentralized satellite stores**.
4. The maintenance supervisor attends daily production meetings of his area.
5. Major overhauls may still be coordinated through a small central planning cell.

### 2.2.3 Advantages

- **Very fast response time** — crew is already on-site.
- **Zero travel time** for routine breakdowns.
- **Strong production–maintenance integration** at the shop floor.
- **Local accountability** — area managers own their equipment health.

### 2.2.4 Disadvantages

- **Lower crew utilization** (each crew has idle time within its own area).
- **Higher total manpower** needed — typically **15%–30% more** than centralized.
- **Difficulty in standardization** — each area develops its own procedures.
- **Skill dilution** — crews cannot develop deep specialization.
- **Higher spares inventory** because each area holds its own stock.

### 2.2.5 Best-Suited Scenarios

- Large geographically dispersed plants (oil refineries, steel plants, power stations).
- Plants with high equipment density and high breakdown frequency.
- Plants operating on a **24 × 7** basis where downtime is extremely costly.

---

## 2.3 Model 3 — Hybrid (Combined) Maintenance Model

This is the **most widely implemented model in modern industry**. It combines the responsiveness of decentralization with the efficiency of centralization.

- **Central cell** handles: planning, scheduling, major overhauls, condition monitoring, specialty skills (vibration analysis, NDT, balancing), and central spares.
- **Decentralized cells** (one per area) handle: routine inspections, lubrication, minor repairs, cleaning, and breakdown first-response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reality:** Over 70% of large Indian manufacturing plants (auto, pharma, FMCG) use a hybrid model. KTU examiners often ask students to design a hybrid model for a given plant layout.

---

## 2.4 Crew Formation Models (Kelly & Walker Classification)

| Aspect | Project Crew | Area Crew | Specialty Crew | Combination Crew |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Assignment** | Assigned to a specific project/job | Assigned to a geographic area | Assigned by trade specialty | Mix of area + specialty |
| **Best For** | One-time capital projects | Steady-state operations | Plants with diverse trades | Large multi-product plants |
| **Utilization** | High only during project | High & steady | Moderate | High |
| **Flexibility** | Low | Medium | Low | High |
| **Supervisor** | Project Engineer | Area Maintenance Sup. | Trade Foreman | Area Sup. + Central Coord. |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all derivable formulas** required for numerical problems on Maintenance Division Models. All symbols, boundary conditions, and units are explicitly stated. Use `\vert` instead of the bare pipe `|` symbol.

\begin{aligned}
\text{Crew Utilization Factor (CUF)} &= \frac{\text{Actual productive hours}}{\text{Total available hours}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\text{Availability (A)} &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{\text{Total downtime (hours)}}{\text{Number of failures}}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Crews Required} &= \frac{\text{Total maintenance work-hours per week}}{\text{Crew capacity per week}}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\text{Crew Capacity per Week} &= N_{\text{workers}} \times H_{\text{shift}} \times D_{\text{weeks}} \times \text{Utilization Factor}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\text{Travel Time (Centralized)} &= \frac{2 \times \text{Distance to farthest equipment}}{\text{Average crew speed}}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\text{Manpower Multiplier (Decentralized)} &= 1.15 \text{ to } 1.30
\end{aligned}

Where:
- $N_{\text{workers}}$ = number of workers in the crew
- $H_{\text{shift}}$ = hours per shift (typically **8 hours**)
- $D_{\text{weeks}}$ = working days per week (typically **5 or 6**)
- $\text{MTBF}$ = Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
- $\text{MTTR}$ = Mean Time To Repair (hours)

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries (e.g., BPCL Kochi):** Hybrid model with central planning and area-based execution.
- **Automobile Assembly (e.g., Maruti Suzuki):** Combination crews in each shop, with central NDT and vibration cell.
- **Power Plants (NTPC):** Specialty crews for boiler, turbine, and electrical, each with dedicated area responsibility.
- **Aerospace MRO:** Project crews assigned to specific aircraft overhaul campaigns.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Crew Size Determination Using Work-Load Method

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A process plant has the following annual maintenance workload data:

- Total preventive maintenance hours = **6,000 hours/year**
- Total breakdown maintenance hours = **3,500 hours/year**
- Total modification/project hours = **1,500 hours/year**
- Crew utilization target = **85%**
- Each worker works **8 hours/day × 300 days/year × 1 shift = 2,400 hours/year** (gross)
- Net productive hours per worker per year = 2,400 × 0.85 = **2,040 hours/year**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the total annual maintenance work-load.**

The total workload $W_{\text{total}}$ is the arithmetic sum of all three categories of work:

$$
W_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{PM}} + W_{\text{BM}} + W_{\text{Proj}}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 6{,}000 + 3{,}500 + 1{,}500 = 11{,}000 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the net productive hours available per worker per year.**

Each worker is available for $H_{\text{gross}}$ hours per year, but only a fraction equal to the Utilization Factor (UF) is spent on productive maintenance. The remainder is consumed by:

- Waiting for job assignment
- Travel
- Tooling up
- Administrative breaks

$$
H_{\text{net}} = H_{\text{gross}} \times \text{UF}
$$

$$
H_{\text{net}} = 2{,}400 \times 0.85 = 2{,}040 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the required crew size.**

The number of workers $N$ required is the ceiling of the total workload divided by the net productive capacity per worker. The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ensures that we round up to the next whole person (since a fraction of a worker cannot be hired).

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{H_{\text{net}}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{11{,}000}{2{,}040} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N = \lceil 5.392 \rceil = 6 \text{ workers}
$$

**Step 4: Verification.**

Six workers provide $6 \times 2{,}040 = 12{,}240$ productive hours per year, which exceeds the demand of 11,000 hours. The excess of 1,240 hours serves as a **buffer for unexpected emergency work** or for **training and skill upgradation**.

### 3.1.3 Analytical Result

> **Answer:** The plant requires a maintenance crew of **6 workers** (plus 1 supervisor) under a centralized model. Under a decentralized model, the same workload distributed across 3 areas would require approximately $6 \times 1.20 = 7.2 \approx 8$ workers (applying the 20% manpower multiplier).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Kelly–Walker Crew Size Optimization Model

### 3.2.1 Conceptual Foundation

The Kelly–Walker model balances **two opposing costs**:

1. **Waiting Cost (C_w):** Cost incurred when a failed machine waits for an idle crew. This DECREASES as crew size increases.
2. **Crew Cost (C_c):** Salary and overhead of idle crew members. This INCREASES as crew size increases.

The **optimal crew size** is the one that **minimizes the sum** of these two costs.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Model the failure arrival rate.**

Assume machine failures follow a Poisson distribution with mean arrival rate $\lambda$ failures per unit time.

$$
P(n \text{ failures in time } t) = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}
$$

**Step 2: Model the expected number of machines waiting.**

Using queuing theory (M/M/c queue — Markovian arrivals, Markovian service, c servers), the expected number of failed machines waiting in queue is given by the Pollaczek–Khinchine formula in steady state:

$$
L_q = P_0 \cdot \frac{(c\rho)^c \cdot \rho}{c! \cdot (1 - \rho)^2}
$$

where:
- $c$ = number of crews (servers)
- $\rho$ = server utilization ratio = $\dfrac{\lambda}{c \mu}$
- $\mu$ = service rate of one crew (repairs per unit time)
- $P_0$ = probability of zero failed machines in system

**Step 3: Express the total expected cost per unit time.**

$$
C_{\text{total}}(c) = C_w \cdot L_q + C_c \cdot c
$$

**Step 4: Find the optimal crew size by differentiation.**

The optimal $c^*$ is the smallest integer $c$ that satisfies:

$$
C_{\text{total}}(c^*) \leq C_{\text{total}}(c^* + 1)
$$

This iterative search yields the **minimum total cost** point.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- Machine failure rate: $\lambda = 2$ failures/hour
- Service rate per crew: $\mu = 3$ repairs/hour
- Waiting cost: $C_w = \text{Rs. } 500$ per machine-hour of waiting
- Crew cost: $C_c = \text{Rs. } 200$ per crew-hour

**Try c = 1:**
- $\rho = 2/3 = 0.667$
- System is unstable for c = 1 (since $\rho > 1$ is false, but borderline)

**Try c = 2:**
- $\rho = 2/(2 \times 3) = 0.333$
- Using M/M/2 formulas: $L_q \approx 0.033$ machines (low waiting)
- $C_{\text{total}} = 500 \times 0.033 + 200 \times 2 = 16.5 + 400 = \text{Rs. } 416.5$/hour

**Try c = 3:**
- $\rho = 2/(3 \times 3) = 0.222$
- $L_q \approx 0.004$ machines
- $C_{\text{total}} = 500 \times 0.004 + 200 \times 3 = 2.0 + 600 = \text{Rs. } 602$/hour

**Try c = 4:**
- $L_q \approx 0.0005$ machines
- $C_{\text{total}} = 500 \times 0.0005 + 200 \times 4 = 0.25 + 800 = \text{Rs. } 800.25$/hour

**Conclusion:** The total cost is **minimum at c = 2 crews**. The Kelly–Walker model recommends **2 maintenance crews** for this scenario.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Model to Choose?

| Plant Parameter | Choose Centralized If | Choose Decentralized If | Choose Hybrid If |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plant area | **< 50,000 m²** | **> 200,000 m²** | 50,000 – 200,000 m² |
| Equipment density | Low (sparse) | High (dense) | Mixed |
| Failure frequency | Low–Medium | High | Medium–High |
| Skill diversity | Low | Moderate | High |
| Shift operation | Single shift | 24 × 7 | 24 × 7 |
| Capital available | Limited | Abundant | Moderate |
| Management maturity | Low | High | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Centralized Maintenance Organization

```mermaid
graph TD
    PH[Plant Head] --> MM[Maintenance Manager]
    MM --> MP[Maintenance Planner]
    MM --> MW[Maintenance Workshop]
    MM --> MS[Materials and Stores]
    MP --> C1[Crew 1 Fitters]
    MP --> C2[Crew 2 Electricians]
    MP --> C3[Crew 3 Instrument Tech]
    C1 --> EQ1[Equipment Area A]
    C2 --> EQ2[Equipment Area B]
    C3 --> EQ3[Equipment Area C]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Decentralized Maintenance Organization

```mermaid
graph TD
    PH[Plant Head] --> APM1[Area Production Manager 1]
    PH --> APM2[Area Production Manager 2]
    PH --> APM3[Area Production Manager 3]
    APM1 --> AMS1[Area Maintenance Supervisor 1]
    APM2 --> AMS2[Area Maintenance Supervisor 2]
    APM3 --> AMS3[Area Maintenance Supervisor 3]
    AMS1 --> AC1[Area Crew 1]
    AMS2 --> AC2[Area Crew 2]
    AMS3 --> AC3[Area Crew 3]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Hybrid (Combined) Maintenance Organization

```mermaid
graph TD
    PH[Plant Head] --> CEH[Chief Engineer Maintenance]
    CEH --> CPC[Central Planning Cell]
    CEH --> CSS[Central Specialty Support]
    CEH --> AM1[Area Maintenance Zone 1]
    CEH --> AM2[Area Maintenance Zone 2]
    CEH --> AM3[Area Maintenance Zone 3]
    AM1 --> RC1[Routine Crew 1]
    AM2 --> RC2[Routine Crew 2]
    AM3 --> RC3[Routine Crew 3]
    CSS --> NDT[NDT and Vibration Team]
    CSS --> OV[Overhaul Specialists]
    CSS --> LUB[Central Lubrication Cell]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Kelly–Walker Cost Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Crew Size 1] --> B[Crew Size 2]
    B --> C[Crew Size 3]
    C --> D[Crew Size 4]
    D --> E[Crew Size 5]
    A --- CW[Waiting Cost Decreasing]
    E --- CC[Crew Cost Increasing]
    B -.-> OPT[OPTIMUM at Minimum Total Cost]
    C -.-> OPT
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Work Request Flow in Centralized Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Production Supervisor raises Work Request] --> P2[Central Maintenance Desk]
    P2 --> P3[Maintenance Planner converts to Job Order]
    P3 --> P4[Priority and Skill Assignment]
    P4 --> P5[Crew Dispatch from Central Workshop]
    P5 --> P6[Crew Travels to Equipment]
    P6 --> P7[Job Execution and Sign Off]
    P7 --> P8[Feedback to Planner for KPI Update]
```

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Functional Architecture of Hybrid Decision Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Maintenance Job Identified] --> Q1{Is it a routine minor fault?}
    Q1 -- YES --> R1[Assign to Area Crew]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Does it need specialty skill?}
    Q2 -- YES --> R2[Escalate to Central Specialty Cell]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is it a major shutdown project?}
    Q3 -- YES --> R3[Form Project Crew]
    Q3 -- NO --> R4[Assign to Combination Crew with Central Support]
    R1 --> END[Job Closure and Documentation]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
    R4 --> END
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Centralized and Decentralized maintenance organizations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Centralized | Decentralized |
|---|---|---|
| Authority | Single Maintenance Manager controls all crews | Each area supervisor controls his own crew |
| Response Time | Slower (depends on distance from central workshop) | Faster (crew is on-site) |
| Crew Utilization | Higher (80%–90%) | Lower (60%–75%) |
| Manpower Required | Lower | 15%–30% higher |
| Spares Inventory | Single central store | Multiple satellite stores |
| Travel Time | Significant | Negligible |
| Standardization | Strong | Weak |
| Best Suited For | Small plants | Large dispersed plants |

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on plant size, equipment density, and operating shifts. Most modern plants adopt a **hybrid model** combining the strengths of both. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a Combination Crew in maintenance organization? Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Combination Crew** is a hybrid crew structure in which workers are assigned by area for routine jobs but can be temporarily pooled under a central coordinator for major shutdowns, turnarounds, or specialty tasks. Each crew member has a **home area** but reports to the central coordinator during special campaigns.

**Example:** In a fertilizer plant, the maintenance crew of the Ammonia section normally handles routine pump and compressor maintenance of that section. During the annual plant turnaround, the same crew is pooled with the Urea section crew under a Turnaround Coordinator to execute the full shutdown campaign in 21 days.

This model provides the **flexibility of area crews** with the **manpower elasticity of project crews**, achieving high utilization without permanent over-staffing. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A large cement plant has 4 production zones spread over 250,000 m², operates 24×7, and employs 120 maintenance workers. Design a suitable Maintenance Division Model for this plant with full justification.**

#### Part (a) — Model Selection and Justification (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Analyze Plant Parameters.**

- Plant area = 250,000 m² (very large)
- Operating pattern = 24 × 7 (continuous)
- 4 distinct production zones
- 120 maintenance workers
- High equipment density (crushers, kilns, mills, conveyors)

**Step 2: Eliminate Unsuitable Models.**

A **pure centralized model** is unsuitable because:
- The travel time from a central workshop to the farthest zone could exceed 45 minutes.
- A 24×7 operation demands immediate on-site response.
- 120 workers under one manager violates the **span of control principle** (ideal span = 6–8 direct reports).

A **pure decentralized model** is also unsuitable because:
- It would lead to skill dilution across zones.
- Standardization of safety procedures and condition monitoring would suffer.
- Spare parts inventory would balloon by 25%–30%.

**Step 3: Select the Hybrid Model.**

The plant should adopt a **Hybrid (Combined) Maintenance Model**.

**Architecture:**
- **Central Maintenance Cell (15 workers):** Planning, scheduling, condition monitoring (vibration, thermography, oil analysis), NDT, major overhaul specialists, central spares store, CMMS administration.
- **4 Zone Maintenance Cells (≈ 25 workers each):** Routine inspections, lubrication, minor repairs, breakdown first-response, preventive maintenance execution, local tool cribs.
- **1 Central Coordinator (Chief Maintenance Engineer):** Reports to Plant Head, coordinates all 5 cells.

**Step 4: Justification Matrix.**

| Criterion | How Hybrid Model Satisfies It |
|---|---|
| Response Time | Zone crews on-site → response within 10–15 minutes |
| Crew Utilization | Central pool absorbs zone peak loads → 85% utilization |
| Standardization | Central cell enforces SOPs and CMMS discipline |
| Skill Depth | Specialty teams (vibration, NDT) develop deep expertise |
| Spares Cost | Central store with zone-level min–max replenishment |
| Span of Control | Chief Engineer manages 5 direct reports |

**(7 marks awarded for full model selection + justification)**

#### Part (b) — Crew Size Calculation Using Work-Load Method (7 Marks)

**Given Data (assumed for the plant):**
- Total annual PM hours = 45,000 hours
- Total annual breakdown hours = 25,000 hours
- Total annual project/modification hours = 15,000 hours
- Worker gross hours = 8 hours/day × 300 days × 2 shifts = 4,800 hours/year
- Utilization factor = 80% (allowing for travel, breaks, waiting)

**Step 1: Compute Total Workload.**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 45{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 85{,}000 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Net Productive Hours per Worker.**

$$
H_{\text{net}} = 4{,}800 \times 0.80 = 3{,}840 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Crew Size.**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \left\lceil \frac{85{,}000}{3{,}840} \right\rceil = \lceil 22.135 \rceil = 23 \text{ workers per zone}
$$

**Step 4: Distribute Across Zones.**

- Each of 4 zones requires **23 workers** (including 1 supervisor + 22 technicians).
- Central cell: **15 specialists** (as designed in part a).
- Total workforce = 4 × 23 + 15 = **107 workers** (round up to 120 as per sanctioned strength, providing 13 workers as buffer).

**Step 5: Validation.**

- Total productive capacity = 120 × 3,840 = 460,800 hours/year.
- Demand = 85,000 hours/year.
- Buffer = 375,800 hours/year (used for training, shutdowns, condition monitoring rounds).

**(7 marks awarded for correct arithmetic + interpretation)**

---

### Question B1 (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the Kelly–Walker model for determining optimum maintenance crew size. A workshop receives 2 machine breakdowns per hour on average. Each crew can repair at the rate of 3 machines per hour. The waiting cost per machine per hour is Rs. 500 and crew cost is Rs. 200 per hour. Determine the optimum number of crews.**

#### Part (a) — Kelly–Walker Model Theory (7 Marks)

The **Kelly–Walker model** is a **queuing-theory-based decision tool** used to determine the optimum size of a maintenance crew. It balances two opposing cost components:

1. **Waiting Cost (C_w):** Penalty cost incurred when failed machines wait in queue because no crew is free. This DECREASES as crew size increases.

2. **Idle Crew Cost (C_c):** Salary, overhead, and idle-time cost of maintenance crews. This INCREASES linearly with crew size.

The model uses an **M/M/c queuing system**:
- **M** = Markovian (Poisson) arrival of failures with rate $\lambda$
- **M** = Markovian (exponential) service time with rate $\mu$
- **c** = number of parallel servers (crews)

The expected number of failed machines waiting in the queue (Pollaczek–Khinchine formula):

$$
L_q = P_0 \cdot \frac{(c\rho)^c \cdot \rho}{c! \cdot (1-\rho)^2}
$$

Total expected cost per unit time:

$$
C_{\text{total}}(c) = C_w \cdot L_q + C_c \cdot c
$$

The optimum $c^*$ is the integer that minimizes $C_{\text{total}}$. The procedure is iterative:

- Try $c = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
- Compute $L_q$ and $C_{\text{total}}$ for each.
- Select the smallest $c$ such that increasing $c$ further raises $C_{\text{total}}$.

**Engineering Insight:** The Kelly–Walker model is particularly valuable during the **design stage of a new plant** when the maintenance budget is being finalized. It also helps in justifying crew expansions during operational audits.

**(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 2$ failures/hour
- $\mu = 3$ repairs/hour per crew
- $C_w = \text{Rs. } 500$ per machine-hour
- $C_c = \text{Rs. } 200$ per crew-hour

**Step 1: Compute Traffic Intensity for Each Crew Configuration.**

The traffic intensity per server is $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c \mu}$.

**Step 2: Iterative Cost Calculation.**

| c | $\rho$ | $L_q$ (approx.) | Waiting Cost $C_w \cdot L_q$ | Crew Cost $C_c \cdot c$ | $C_{\text{total}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.667 (unstable if $\rho \geq 1$, but borderline) | Very high | Very high | 200 | Unacceptably high |
| 2 | 0.333 | 0.033 | 16.5 | 400 | **416.5** |
| 3 | 0.222 | 0.004 | 2.0 | 600 | 602.0 |
| 4 | 0.167 | 0.0005 | 0.25 | 800 | 800.25 |
| 5 | 0.133 | 0.00006 | 0.03 | 1000 | 1000.03 |

**Step 3: Identify the Optimum.**

The minimum total cost occurs at **c = 2 crews**, where $C_{\text{total}} = \text{Rs. } 416.5$/hour.

**Step 4: Verification by Trend.**

- From c = 1 → c = 2: Cost DROPS from "very high" to 416.5 (steep drop due to queue vanishing).
- From c = 2 → c = 3: Cost INCREASES from 416.5 to 602 (Rs. +185.5).
- Hence c = 2 is the global minimum.

**Conclusion:** The optimum number of maintenance crews for this workshop is **2 crews**. Adding a third crew would increase the total cost by Rs. 185.5/hour because the additional waiting-cost savings (Rs. 14.5/hour) do not justify the additional crew cost (Rs. 200/hour).

**(7 marks awarded for full iterative solution + economic interpretation)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "crew size" with "number of workers":** A "crew" of 4 workers is different from "4 crews of 1 worker each." Read the question wording carefully. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the Utilization Factor:** Many students divide total hours by gross hours (2,400) instead of net hours (2,040). The Utilization Factor (85%) is a MUST-USE in work-load calculations. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Not rounding UP the crew size:** Always use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling function). A fraction of a worker cannot be hired. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **In Kelly–Walker problems, students often compute only 2 values of c** and conclude without checking that the minimum is global. KTU examiners require at least 3 iterations to confirm the optimum. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 5. **Writing advantages/disadvantages without engineering justification:** Don't just list points — quantify them. Mention "15%–30% higher manpower" rather than "more manpower." **(Lose 1 mark per vague point)**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Maintenance Division Models** classify how maintenance manpower and authority are distributed: **Centralized**, **Decentralized**, or **Hybrid**.
- **Centralized Model** offers higher crew utilization and lower manpower cost but suffers from high response time — best for small plants under **50,000 m²**.
- **Decentralized Model** offers fastest response and strongest production-maintenance integration but requires **15%–30% extra manpower** — best for large dispersed plants operating 24×7.
- **Hybrid Model** is the **most widely adopted** in modern industry — combines central planning/specialty with area-based execution.
- **Kelly–Walker Crew Classification** divides crews into **Project, Area, Specialty, and Combination** types.
- **Work-Load Method** for crew sizing uses: $N = \lceil W_{\text{total}} / (H_{\text{gross}} \times \text{UF}) \rceil$.
- **Kelly–Walker Cost Model** uses queuing theory to balance **waiting cost** and **crew cost**, giving optimum crew count.
- The **span of control principle** dictates that a single supervisor should ideally have **6–8 direct reports** in maintenance.
- Always apply the **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ when rounding crew sizes.
- The **Utilization Factor** in maintenance typically ranges from **75% to 90%**; values above 90% indicate under-staffing.
- In KTU exams, **comparison tables** (Centralized vs Decentralized) fetch 3 marks easily — memorize at least 6 comparison parameters.
- For numerical problems on Kelly–Walker, perform **at least 3 iterations** of crew size to identify the global cost minimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedures of JIT Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET863 - CONDITION MONITORING AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Procedures of JIT Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**JIT (Just-in-Time) Maintenance** is a strategic maintenance management philosophy in which all maintenance activities — preventive servicing, spare parts replenishment, manpower deployment, and tool changeovers — are scheduled and executed **exactly at the moment they are required** by the production system, eliminating the waste of over-maintenance, excess inventory, and unnecessary machine downtime.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, JIT Maintenance is positioned as the operational extension of **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** and **Lean Manufacturing** principles into the maintenance function. It shifts the maintenance paradigm from a *reactive time-based* approach to a *demand-driven, condition-triggered* approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The procedures of JIT Maintenance focus on the **step-by-step methodology** used to integrate maintenance activities with the production schedule so that the *right maintenance, in the right quantity, at the right time* is delivered to the shop floor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Blood Bank" Model

Imagine a hospital blood bank:
- It does **not** stockpile massive quantities of every blood type "just in case" (that would be wasteful — blood expires).
- It does **not** wait until a patient is bleeding on the operation table to order blood (that would be catastrophic).
- Instead, it operates a **pull system** — blood is replenished *only* when actual demand signals reach the supplier, and stock is sized to cover the *expected* lead time.

**JIT Maintenance works identically with spare parts, lubricants, and maintenance crews:**

| Hospital Blood Bank | JIT Maintenance Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient demand | Machine condition / production schedule |
| Blood inventory | Spare parts inventory |
| Expiry of blood | Obsolescence / degradation of stored spares |
| Delivery lead time | Procurement lead time of spares |
| Blood replenishment order | Maintenance work order |

### 1.3 Core Principles of JIT Maintenance (Mandatory KTU Definition Set)

1. **Zero Defects Philosophy** — Maintenance work is done right the first time; no rework.
2. **Pull System (Demand-Driven)** — Maintenance actions are triggered by *actual need* signals, not by calendar.
3. **Waste Elimination (Muda)** — Removal of the **7 Wastes (Muda)** in maintenance: transport, inventory, motion, waiting, overproduction, over-processing, and defects.
4. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** — Machine operators handle routine upkeep (cleaning, lubrication, inspection).
5. **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** — Small, incremental improvements in maintenance procedures are encouraged daily.
6. **Takt Time Alignment** — Maintenance cycles are synchronized with the customer's production rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reference:** Industry-recognized availability benchmark for JIT systems is **> 90% (World-Class)**, with OEE targets of **> 85%**.

### 1.4 The "7 Wastes" (Muda) Adapted to Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a frequently asked KTU question. Memorize the 7 wastes in the maintenance context.

1. **Transportation Waste** — Unnecessary movement of spares, tools, or technicians.
2. **Inventory Waste** — Excess spare parts tying up capital and storage space.
3. **Motion Waste** — Technicians walking long distances to fetch tools.
4. **Waiting Waste** — Crews idle because the job is not ready, or parts are unavailable.
5. **Over-Production Waste** — Doing preventive maintenance more frequently than required.
6. **Over-Processing Waste** — Using a more expensive repair when a simple cleaning would suffice.
7. **Defect Waste** — Rework caused by poor-quality maintenance work.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why" Behind JIT Maintenance

Traditional maintenance systems suffer from three structural problems:
- **Excess Spare Inventory** — Capital locked up in parts that may never be used.
- **Excess Downtime** — Machines are stopped *even when they are healthy* because of rigid PM schedules.
- **Disconnection from Production** — Maintenance is treated as a cost center, not as a value-adding partner.

JIT Maintenance addresses each of these by aligning the maintenance function with **production rhythm** (Takt time) and **actual asset condition** (monitored via condition monitoring techniques covered earlier in the syllabus).

### 2.2 The Six Core Procedures of JIT Maintenance

The KTU syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain the **procedures** (i.e., the methodology) used to implement JIT Maintenance. The standard six-step procedure is:

#### **Procedure 1: Establish Equipment Criticality Classification**
- Categorize all plant assets into **A, B, C** classes based on their impact on production, safety, and quality.
- **A-Class (Critical)** — High downtime cost; JIT must maintain near-zero failure probability.
- **B-Class (Important)** — Moderate impact; standard preventive care.
- **C-Class (General)** — Low impact; run-to-failure acceptable.

#### **Procedure 2: Implement Condition Monitoring (CM) Systems**
- Install **vibration analyzers, thermography, oil analysis, ultrasonic detectors, and motor current signature analysis** on A-Class machines.
- Real-time data feeds the **JIT trigger** — a maintenance work order is auto-generated *only* when a parameter crosses the preset threshold.

#### **Procedure 3: Design the Pull-Based Spare Parts Replenishment System**
- Use a **Kanban** (visual signal card) system for spare parts.
- A *withdrawal Kanban* signals that a part has been used; a *production-order Kanban* signals replenishment to the supplier.
- Spare stock is sized to cover **lead time × average daily usage**, not 12 months of usage.

#### **Procedure 4: Synchronize Maintenance with Production Takt Time**
- Maintenance windows are negotiated with production planning so that interventions occur during **planned production breaks** (shift changes, weekends, or natural idle periods).
- This eliminates the "maintenance versus production" conflict.

#### **Procedure 5: Empower Autonomous Maintenance (AM) by Operators**
- Operators are trained in basic maintenance skills: cleaning, lubricating, tightening, inspection, and condition reporting.
- This reduces the burden on the maintenance department and catches defects early.

#### **Procedure 6: Establish Kaizen and Continuous Improvement Cycles**
- After every maintenance action, a **post-job review** is conducted to identify waste.
- The "**5 Whys**" root cause analysis technique is applied to eliminate recurring problems.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are KTU high-yield. Memorize the relationships, not just the symbols.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ (Availability) | $A = \dfrac{T_{run}}{T_{run} + T_{down}}$ | Fraction of time the equipment is ready to produce | % or fraction |
| $OEE$ (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | World-class benchmark for manufacturing productivity | % |
| $P$ (Performance Rate) | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times N_{total}}{T_{run}}$ | Speed efficiency vs design speed | % |
| $Q$ (Quality Rate) | $Q = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | First-pass yield | % |
| $MTBF$ (Mean Time Between Failures) | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{run}}{N_{f}}$ | Reliability indicator | hours |
| $MTTR$ (Mean Time To Repair) | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_{f}}$ | Maintainability indicator | hours |
| $T_{takt}$ (Takt Time) | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$ | Customer-driven production rate | sec/unit |
| $N_{opt}$ (Optimal Spare Stock) | $N_{opt} = L_{t} \times U_{d}$ | Reorder quantity in JIT Kanban | units |
| $L_{t}$ (Lead Time) | Procurement / repair duration | days | — |
| $U_{d}$ (Average Daily Usage) | Consumption rate of part | units/day | — |

**Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:**
- $0 \leq A \leq 1$ (or $0\% \leq A \leq 100\%$)
- $0 \leq OEE \leq 1$ (or $0\% \leq OEE \leq 100\%$)
- **World-Class OEE** = **85%** (industry benchmark)
- **World-Class Availability** = **90%**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

JIT Maintenance is the backbone of modern manufacturing in **automotive (Toyota Production System), semiconductor fabrication (TSMC), aerospace (Boeing), and pharmaceutical (Pfizer) plants**. It enables:
- **Inventory Carrying Cost Reduction** of 20–40% (typical industry figure).
- **Equipment Availability Improvement** of 5–15% within 2 years of implementation.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) extension** of 30–50% through early fault detection.
- **Shop Floor Space Recovery** because spare parts warehouses can shrink dramatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** Maintenance is no longer a "repair shop" — under JIT, it is a **proactive, data-driven, production-integrated function** that directly contributes to the plant's bottom line.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the OEE Equation from First Principles

The **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** is the single most important metric in JIT Maintenance. The KTU examiner expects you to derive the three-component OEE equation from its basic definitions. The full step-by-step derivation is given below.

### **Step 1 — Define the Planned Production Time**

$$
T_{planned} = T_{calendar} - T_{scheduled\ downtime}
$$

where $T_{calendar}$ is the total time in the period (e.g., 1 day = 24 h) and $T_{scheduled\ downtime}$ includes planned breaks, weekends, and maintenance windows.

### **Step 2 — Define the Operating Time (Running Time)**

$$
T_{run} = T_{planned} - T_{unscheduled\ downtime}
$$

Unscheduled downtime = unplanned stoppages caused by **breakdowns** and **setup/changeover losses** (the very things JIT Maintenance targets to minimize).

### **Step 3 — Define the Availability Component**

$$
A = \frac{T_{run}}{T_{planned}}
$$

Availability captures *how often* the machine is actually running when it is supposed to be. JIT Maintenance targets this metric via autonomous maintenance and predictive interventions.

### **Step 4 — Define the Performance Component**

$$
P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times N_{total}}{T_{run}}
$$

The numerator is the *theoretical minimum time* required to produce $N_{total}$ units at the design speed. The denominator is the *actual running time*. If the machine runs at design speed, $P = 1$ (or 100%). If it runs slow or has minor stops, $P < 1$.

### **Step 5 — Define the Quality Component**

$$
Q = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}
$$

The fraction of units that pass quality inspection on first pass. Defects are often a symptom of poor machine condition — JIT Maintenance aims to keep machines within tolerance to drive $Q$ upward.

### **Step 6 — Combine the Three Components**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q
$$

$$
OEE = \left( \frac{T_{run}}{T_{planned}} \right) \times \left( \frac{I_{ct} \cdot N_{total}}{T_{run}} \right) \times \left( \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \right)
$$

where $I_{ct}$ is the ideal cycle time.

### **Step 7 — Simplification (Cancellation of $T_{run}$ and $N_{total}$)**

$$
OEE = \frac{I_{ct} \cdot N_{good}}{T_{planned}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This elegant simplification shows that **OEE is fundamentally the ratio of the time that *good* parts were produced at *design speed* to the *total planned production time***. The 6 Big Losses in manufacturing are all hidden inside the three multipliers A, P, and Q.

### **Step 8 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style Problem)**

**Problem:** A CNC machine in a JIT plant has the following data for one shift (8 hours):
- Scheduled break time = 30 min
- Unplanned downtime (breakdowns) = 25 min
- Setup and adjustment losses = 15 min
- Minor stops and reduced speed = 20 min
- Total units produced = 280
- Defective units = 12
- Ideal cycle time = 90 sec/unit

**Solution:**

**Step 8.1 — Planned Production Time:**
$$
T_{planned} = 8 \times 60 - 30 = 480 - 30 = 450 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 8.2 — Unscheduled Downtime:**
$$
T_{unsched} = 25 + 15 = 40 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 8.3 — Operating Time:**
$$
T_{run} = 450 - 40 = 410 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 8.4 — Availability:**
$$
A = \frac{410}{450} = 0.9111 = 91.11\%
$$

**Step 8.5 — Performance:**

First, total time available at design speed:
$$
I_{ct} \cdot N_{total} = 90 \times 280 = 25200 \text{ sec} = 420 \text{ min}
$$

Then:
$$
P = \frac{420}{410} = 1.0244 \to \text{clamped to } 100\%
$$

Since performance cannot exceed 100%, the effective performance = **100%** (the machine actually outpaced ideal — meaning the cycle time assumption is conservative). For KTU problems, the clamped value is used.

**Step 8.6 — Quality:**
$$
Q = \frac{280 - 12}{280} = \frac{268}{280} = 0.9571 = 95.71\%
$$

**Step 8.7 — OEE:**
$$
OEE = 0.9111 \times 1.0000 \times 0.9571 = 0.8721 = 87.21\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The plant is operating at **87.21% OEE**, which is **above the world-class 85% benchmark** — confirming that JIT Maintenance procedures are working effectively.

## 3.2 Derivation of Optimal Spare Stock (Kanban Reorder Quantity)

In a JIT Kanban system, the reorder quantity is determined by **lead time demand**, not annual demand.

**Starting Principle:** Stock must never fall below zero during the lead time window.

$$
N_{opt} = L_{t} \times U_{d}
$$

If safety stock $S$ is desired:
$$
N_{opt} = (L_{t} \times U_{d}) + S
$$

For a fluctuating demand with standard deviation $\sigma_{d}$:
$$
S = z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L_{t}}
$$

where $z$ is the **service-level z-score** (e.g., $z = 1.65$ for 95% service level).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Procedures to Outcomes

| JIT Procedure | Primary Metric Improved | Typical % Gain | Failure Mode if Skipped |
|---|---|---|---|
| Criticality Classification | Maintenance focus | 100% focus on 20% of assets | Wasted effort on non-critical equipment |
| Condition Monitoring | $MTBF$ ↑ | +30% to +50% | Unplanned breakdowns remain high |
| Kanban Spare Replenishment | Inventory cost ↓ | −20% to −40% | Capital tied up in dead stock |
| Takt Time Synchronization | $A$ ↑ | +5% to +10% | Maintenance vs production conflict |
| Autonomous Maintenance | $MTTR$ ↓ | −15% to −25% | Operator-detected defects missed |
| Kaizen / 5-Whys | All three (A, P, Q) | Continuous | Recurring problems repeat |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Six-Procedure JIT Maintenance Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: JIT Maintenance Program] --> B1[Procedure 1: Equipment Criticality Classification A B C]
    B1 --> C1[Procedure 2: Install Condition Monitoring Sensors on A Class Machines]
    C1 --> D1[Procedure 3: Implement Kanban Based Spare Parts Replenishment]
    D1 --> E1[Procedure 4: Synchronize Maintenance Windows with Production Takt Time]
    E1 --> F1[Procedure 5: Train Operators in Autonomous Maintenance Jishu Hozen]
    F1 --> G1[Procedure 6: Kaizen Cycle and 5 Whys Root Cause Analysis]
    G1 --> H1[Outcome: Improved OEE Availability Performance Quality]
    H1 --> I1[Feedback Loop: Continuous Improvement Re enters Procedure 1]
    I1 -.-> B1
    H1 --> J1[Key Performance Indicators MTBF Up MTTR Down Spare Stock Down]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The dashed feedback line from H1 back to B1 represents the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** continuous improvement loop embedded in every JIT Maintenance program.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — JIT Maintenance Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        I1[Production Schedule Takt Time]
        I2[Condition Monitoring Data Vibration Temperature Oil]
        I3[Kanban Signals Spare Withdrawal]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Processing Layer JIT Brain]
        P1[Threshold Analysis Module]
        P2[Maintenance Work Order Generator]
        P3[Spare Replenishment Trigger]
        P4[Autonomous Maintenance Schedule]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1[Preventive Work Order]
        O2[Spare Parts Reorder]
        O3[Operator AM Checklist]
        O4[Predictive Intervention Alert]
    end

    subgraph OutcomeLayer[Outcome Layer]
        R1[Increased MTBF]
        R2[Decreased MTTR]
        R3[Reduced Inventory Cost]
        R4[OEE World Class Greater than 85 percent]
    end

    I1 --> P2
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P3
    P1 --> P4
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    P2 --> O4
    P3 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    O1 --> R1
    O2 --> R3
    O3 --> R2
    O4 --> R4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — The Pull-Based Maintenance Trigger

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Machine Asset
    participant CM as Condition Monitoring System
    participant TC as Threshold Comparator
    participant WO as Work Order Generator
    participant MT as Maintenance Technician
    participant SP as Spare Parts Kanban
    participant OP as Production Schedule

    M->>CM: Real Time Vibration and Temperature Data
    CM->>TC: Stream Parameter Values
    TC->>TC: Compare to Preset Threshold
    alt Threshold Exceeded
        TC->>WO: Trigger Maintenance Alert
        WO->>MT: Assign Job and Skill Requirement
        MT->>SP: Request Spare Part via Kanban Card
        SP->>MT: Deliver Spare within Lead Time
        MT->>M: Execute Just in Time Repair
        MT->>WO: Close Work Order with Time Stamp
    else Threshold Not Exceeded
        TC->>CM: Continue Normal Monitoring
    end
    WO->>OP: Update Availability Statistic
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define JIT Maintenance. State any four of its key principles.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Just-in-Time (JIT) Maintenance is a maintenance management philosophy in which maintenance activities — preventive servicing, spare parts replenishment, and crew deployment — are scheduled and executed **exactly when demanded by the asset's condition or the production schedule**, eliminating waste of over-maintenance and excess inventory.

**Four Key Principles:**

1. **Pull-Based Demand Trigger** — Maintenance actions are initiated by actual condition signals, not by fixed calendar intervals.
2. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** — Machine operators perform routine upkeep (cleaning, lubrication, basic inspection) to catch early defects.
3. **Waste Elimination (Muda Removal)** — The 7 wastes of transport, inventory, motion, waiting, over-production, over-processing, and defects are systematically eliminated from maintenance processes.
4. **Kaizen and Continuous Improvement** — Small, daily improvements are made in maintenance procedures through techniques like 5-Whys root cause analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Principles (also acceptable for full marks):** Zero-defect philosophy, Takt-time alignment, total productive maintenance integration, supplier partnership for spares.

---

### **Question 2: What is the role of Kanban in JIT Maintenance?** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A Kanban is a **visual signal card (or electronic token)** that authorizes the movement or production of one unit (or a standard container) of a maintenance spare part.

**Role in JIT Maintenance:**

1. **Pull Trigger:** When a maintenance technician withdraws a spare from the storeroom, the *withdrawal Kanban* is sent back to the supplier (or central stores) to authorize replenishment of **only that one unit**.
2. **Inventory Cap:** The total number of Kanban cards in circulation is fixed; this cap on in-process inventory prevents over-stocking of spares.
3. **Information Flow:** The Kanban carries critical data — part number, quantity, supplier code, and storage location — eliminating paperwork delays.
4. **Lead Time Discipline:** Because replenishment is triggered by *usage* (not by forecast), the system forces the supplier to maintain consistent lead times; variability is exposed and eliminated.
5. **Waste Reduction:** Excess inventory waste (Muda #2) is structurally impossible under a well-designed Kanban loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** A Kanban is **not** a forecast-based reorder system. It is a *real-usage-driven* signal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer an internal choice. Both sub-parts (a) and (b) must be answered from the *same* chosen question.

---

### **Question A: (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**"Explain the six procedural steps for implementing JIT Maintenance in a manufacturing plant. Illustrate with a flow diagram."**

**Model Answer:**

The implementation of JIT Maintenance is carried out in a structured six-step procedure. Each step is explained below.

**Step 1: Equipment Criticality Classification (1 mark)**
All machines in the plant are categorized into A, B, C classes based on their impact on production output, product quality, safety, and replacement cost. Only A-class (critical) assets receive intensive JIT attention; C-class assets may be run-to-failure.

**Step 2: Installation of Condition Monitoring Systems (1 mark)**
A-class machines are equipped with vibration sensors, infrared thermography, oil wear debris analysis, and motor current signature analyzers. Real-time data streams to a central monitoring station.

**Step 3: Kanban-Based Spare Parts System (1.5 marks)**
Spare parts are replenished via a pull-Kanban loop. The reorder quantity is calculated as $N_{opt} = L_t \times U_d + S$, where $L_t$ is lead time, $U_d$ is daily usage, and $S$ is safety stock.

**Step 4: Takt-Time Synchronization of Maintenance (1 mark)**
Maintenance windows are pre-scheduled during production breaks, changeovers, or natural idle periods. The maintenance department participates in production planning meetings.

**Step 5: Autonomous Maintenance by Operators (1.5 marks)**
Operators are trained in 7-step autonomous maintenance: cleaning, lubrication, tightening, inspection, early-defect detection, calibration, and condition recording. This shifts first-line upkeep from the maintenance crew to the operator.

**Step 6: Kaizen and 5-Whys Continuous Improvement (1 mark)**
Every maintenance intervention is followed by a post-job review using the 5-Whys technique to identify the root cause and prevent recurrence. Small improvements are made daily.

**Flow Diagram (textual representation — refer to Section 4.1):**
Criticality → Condition Monitoring → Kanban → Takt Sync → AM → Kaizen → (Loop back).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**"A stamping press in a JIT plant operates two shifts of 8 hours each. The data for one day is as follows: scheduled break time per shift = 40 min, unplanned breakdown time = 55 min, setup time = 20 min, minor stop time = 18 min, reduced speed loss = 12 min, total pieces produced = 1450, defective pieces = 42, ideal cycle time = 32 seconds. Calculate the OEE."**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Total Calendar Time and Planned Production Time**
$$
T_{calendar} = 2 \times 8 \times 60 = 960 \text{ min}
$$
$$
T_{scheduled} = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ min}
$$
$$
T_{planned} = 960 - 80 = 880 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2: Unscheduled Downtime**
$$
T_{unsched} = 55 + 20 = 75 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3: Operating Time**
$$
T_{run} = 880 - 75 = 805 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4: Availability**
$$
A = \frac{805}{880} = 0.9148 = 91.48\%
$$

**Step 5: Performance**

Ideal time to produce 1450 units:
$$
I_{ct} \cdot N_{total} = 32 \times 1450 = 46400 \text{ sec} = 773.33 \text{ min}
$$

Performance:
$$
P = \frac{773.33}{805} = 0.9608 = 96.08\%
$$

**Step 6: Quality**
$$
Q = \frac{1450 - 42}{1450} = \frac{1408}{1450} = 0.9710 = 97.10\%
$$

**Step 7: OEE**
$$
OEE = 0.9148 \times 0.9608 \times 0.9710 = 0.8535 = 85.35\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The plant's OEE of **85.35%** is at the **world-class threshold (≥ 85%)**, indicating that the JIT Maintenance procedures are functioning effectively. The dominant loss is **Performance (3.92% gap from 100%)** — likely due to minor stops and reduced speed; this should be the focus of the next Kaizen cycle.

---

### **Question B: (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**"Discuss the role of condition monitoring in enabling JIT Maintenance. Explain with examples how vibration analysis and thermography act as pull-triggers for maintenance actions."**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Condition Monitoring in JIT Maintenance (3.5 marks):**

Condition monitoring (CM) is the **enabling technology** of JIT Maintenance. Without real-time data on machine health, the "JIT trigger" — the exact moment when maintenance is needed — cannot be determined. CM converts the traditional time-based PM schedule into a **condition-based, demand-driven** system.

The pull-trigger mechanism works as follows: each parameter (vibration, temperature, oil debris level) has a **preset threshold** (alarm and trip levels). When the parameter crosses the alarm threshold, a work order is auto-generated. This is the **pull signal** that initiates JIT maintenance. If the parameter stays below the threshold, *no* maintenance is done — saving labour, spares, and avoiding the over-processing waste of unnecessary PM.

**Example 1: Vibration Analysis on a Rotating Machine (2 marks)**

A centrifugal pump fitted with an accelerometer shows broadband vibration rising from 2.8 mm/s (baseline) to 6.5 mm/s. The alarm threshold is 5.5 mm/s. The CM system:
- Detects the threshold crossing at 14:32 on Tuesday.
- Auto-generates a work order for the maintenance crew to inspect the bearing.
- The crew arrives, finds outer-race spalling, replaces the bearing (takes 90 min).
- The pump returns to service with vibration back to 2.9 mm/s.
- **No spare bearing was replaced in advance** — the bearing was procured via the Kanban loop *just in time* for this failure.

**Example 2: Infrared Thermography on an Electrical Panel (1.5 marks)**

A thermographic scan of a motor control centre (MCC) shows a phase terminal at 92 °C, while the other two phases are at 58 °C and 60 °C. The threshold for "asymmetry fault" is a 25 °C delta. The CM system:
- Flags the MCC panel for JIT intervention.
- A planned shutdown of 2 hours is negotiated with production for the next natural break.
- The loose terminal is re-torqued, contact resistance drops, and the temperature normalises to 61 °C.
- **No preventive replacement of healthy terminals was done** — only the faulty one, exactly when needed.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**"Explain the 7 Wastes (Muda) in the context of JIT Maintenance. For each waste, give one practical example of how it is eliminated."**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Seven Wastes framework, originally codified by Taiichi Ohno of Toyota, is adapted to the maintenance function. Each waste is explained with its practical elimination example.

| # | Waste (Muda) | Meaning in Maintenance | Practical Elimination Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transportation** | Unnecessary movement of spares, tools, or crew | Layout storerooms next to consuming machines; deploy mobile tool carts |
| 2 | **Inventory** | Excess spare parts tying up capital | Replace 12-month stock with Kanban-driven lead-time stock |
| 3 | **Motion** | Technicians walking to fetch tools | Use shadow boards at machine for all required tools |
| 4 | **Waiting** | Crews idle because job/parts not ready | Synchronize maintenance windows with production breaks |
| 5 | **Over-Production** | PM done more frequently than required | Switch from time-based PM to condition-triggered interventions |
| 6 | **Over-Processing** | Expensive repair when cleaning would suffice | Train technicians to use the minimum effective intervention (e.g., clean vs replace) |
| 7 | **Defects** | Rework from poor maintenance work | Standardize work instructions; use torque wrenches; verify first-time-right |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark question, examiners look for **at least one concrete industry example per waste**. A purely theoretical answer without examples will lose up to 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in JIT Maintenance Questions (KTU 2024 Examiner Feedback):**
>
> 1. **Confusing JIT Manufacturing with JIT Maintenance** — JIT Manufacturing is about producing the *right product* in the *right quantity* at the *right time*. JIT Maintenance is about doing the *right maintenance* at the *right moment* based on *machine condition*. Mixing up these two concepts is the #1 reason for losing marks in definition questions.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Clamp Performance to 100%** — In OEE calculations, if your computed $P$ exceeds 1.0, you must clamp it to 1.0 (or 100%) before using it in the OEE formula. Failing to clamp is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Mixing Units in Calculations** — Cycle time is often given in seconds, while downtime is given in minutes. Convert *all* times to the same unit before computing $A$, $P$, or $OEE$. A common error is using minutes for $T_{run}$ and seconds for $I_{ct} \cdot N_{total}$.
>
> 4. **Writing "Kanban = Just-in-Time"** — Kanban is a *tool* used *within* a JIT system, not a synonym for JIT. Always state the relationship clearly: *Kanban is the pull mechanism that enables JIT.*
>
> 5. **Skipping the Continuous Improvement Loop** — A procedure list that ends at "Kaizen" without mentioning the **feedback loop** to Step 1 is incomplete. JIT Maintenance is a *cyclic* system, not a linear one.
>
